facet: Time-domain & phase-space scopes: waveform/oscilloscope, Lissajous/goniometer/vectorscope, inter-channel correlation & mid/side, and delay-embedding phase portraits.
summary: This facet covers the geometric scopes that plot the signal against itself or against its other channel, rather than against frequency. The unifying idea is that a sound's hidden structure becomes visible as GEOMETRY: stereo phase/width appears as the shape and tilt of a Lissajous blob in the goniometer (a 45°-rotated mid/side plot), periodicity and timbral nonlinearity appear as closed vs. fuzzy orbits in a delay-embedding phase portrait x(t) vs x(t+tau), and self-similarity over time appears as diagonals in a recurrence plot. The single most important rendering result is the woscope/Tasios closed-form line integral of a Gaussian beam, which gives a physically exact, GPU-cheap erf-difference formula that turns any XY scope into something genuinely breathtaking (true phosphor glow, brightness inversely proportional to beam speed) instead of jagged polylines. Autonomous dimensioning is unusually rich here: the goniometer auto-tilts/auto-scales to the correlation and RMS of the incoming stereo field, and the delay-embedding scope can auto-pick tau from the first minimum of average mutual information (Fraser-Swinney) and the embedding dimension from false-nearest-neighbors, so the portrait literally reshapes itself to the sound's own time-scales. All of these are realtime-trivial in Rust+egui (CPU geometry) with the beam shader on wgpu for the wow factor.

techniques.name: Glowing-beam XY oscilloscope (woscope Gaussian line-integral)
techniques.one_line: Plot left vs right (or x(t) vs y(t)) as a true electron beam: each segment is rendered as the analytic line integral of a 2D Gaussian, giving real phosphor glow and speed-dependent brightness instead of a flat polyline.
techniques.the_math:
Beam point-spread is a 2D Gaussian I(d)=(1/(σ√(2π)))·e^(−d²/2σ²). For a segment with start at origin and end at (l,0) in segment-local coords, the accumulated intensity at point p=(p_x,p_y) is the integral along the path:
F(p) = ∫₀¹ I(‖p − t·(l,0)‖) dt = (1/(2l))·e^(−p_y²/(2σ²))·[ erf(p_x/(√2·σ)) − erf((p_x−l)/(√2·σ)) ].
GLSL erf (Winitzki/Abramowitz form used by woscope): erf(x)=sign(x)·(1 − 1/(1+(0.278393+(0.230389+0.078108·a²)·a)·a)²), a=|x|.
Brightness ∝ 1/l per unit length, i.e. a slow beam (short segment between samples) dwells longer and glows brighter — exactly the analog CRT behavior. Quad per segment: dir=(end−start)/l, orth=(−dir.y,dir.x); 4 corners = start/end ± σ·dir ± σ·orth, generated in the vertex shader from gl_VertexID; additive blending accumulates overlapping segments. Stereo feed: x=L, y=R (or rotate to mid/side, below).
techniques.what_it_reveals: The exact instantaneous trajectory of the stereo vector — phase relationships, transient asymmetries, and DC/sub offsets that are inaudible and invisible in a normal waveform. Bright knots show where the signal lingers (sustained tones); faint sweeps show fast transients. Reveals the 'shape' of a stereo image as a living figure.
techniques.why_breathtaking: This is the single highest wow-per-line-of-code visual in audio: real oscilloscope-music glow, anti-aliased for free by the erf, with luminous overlaps and motion blur baked into the math. It looks like a physical instrument, not a plot.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: σ (beam width) scales with viewport DPI; per-frame the XY extent is auto-fit to the running peak (a slow-release max of |L|,|R|) so quiet passages don't shrink to a dot and loud ones don't clip — the scope auto-zooms to the signal's current dynamic range. Decay/persistence time-constant can adapt to detected tempo so the trail length tracks the music's pulse.
techniques.implementation_notes: Ideal split: CPU (Rust) builds the per-segment instance buffer from the audio ring; wgpu/glow draws instanced quads with additive blend and the erf fragment shader. ~1–2k segments/frame is trivial. egui-only fallback: approximate with a thick anti-aliased polyline whose alpha ∝ 1/segment_length (cheap, ~70% of the look). Gotchas: premultiplied additive blending and an HDR-ish accumulation (render to f16 target then tonemap) to keep bright knots from clipping to flat white.

Simulating an XY oscilloscope on the GPU — Nick Tasios
woscope source (MIT)

techniques.name: Goniometer / vectorscope (45°-rotated mid/side Lissajous)
techniques.one_line: The professional stereo scope: plot L vs R but rotated 45° so the vertical axis is Mid and the horizontal axis is Side, making mono a vertical line, out-of-phase a horizontal line, and width a visible spread.
techniques.the_math: Mid/Side decomposition: M=(L+R)/√2, S=(L−R)/√2 (the √2 keeps it an orthonormal rotation / energy-preserving). Screen mapping: x = S = (L−R)/√2, y = M = (L+R)/√2. Equivalently a 45° rotation of the raw (L,R) plot: [x;y] = R(45°)·[L;R]. Consequences: dual-mono (L=R) ⇒ S=0 ⇒ a vertical line; polarity-inverted (L=−R) ⇒ M=0 ⇒ a horizontal line (the danger state); left-only ⇒ 45° diagonal one way, right-only ⇒ the other diagonal; a decorrelated stereo field ⇒ a fuzzy 'ball of string' filling the upper half. Instantaneous stereo width can be read as the aspect ratio of the point cloud: width ≈ std(S)/std(M).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Mono compatibility and phase problems at a glance — the exact failure (a horizontal smear) that silently cancels bass on a mono club system or phone speaker. Reveals stereo width, panning balance (overall tilt), and how 'mid-heavy' vs 'side-heavy' the mix is — none of which a mono waveform shows.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The tangled-yarn figure is hypnotic and instantly readable; rendered with the glowing-beam shader above it becomes both a diagnostic and an art object. The 45° tilt is the 'aha' that makes phase legible.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Auto-gains on running RMS so the figure fills the scope regardless of level; the display can auto-rotate a hair to null out a detected static balance offset (so intentional width reads cleanly). A color map keyed to the live correlation coefficient (below) makes the figure self-annotate: green when mono-safe, red when collapsing.
techniques.implementation_notes: Pure CPU geometry in Rust — one rotation per sample, then feed into the beam renderer or a plain egui line. The √2 normalization matters if you also show numeric width. Trivial realtime. Pair with the correlation meter for the full pro-metering panel.

references.title: Goniometer (audio) — Wikipedia
Goniometer — Sound on Sound glossary
jsGoniometer (Gonio/Vectorscope/Correlation/Phase reference impl)

techniques.name: Inter-channel correlation meter (running Pearson ρ)
techniques.one_line: A single bar from −1 to +1 (with red/yellow/green zones) giving the normalized correlation between L and R — the numeric companion that quantifies what the goniometer shows.
techniques.the_math: Time-domain windowed Pearson coefficient: ρ = ⟨L·R⟩ / √(⟨L²⟩·⟨R²⟩), where ⟨·⟩ is a leaky/exponential moving average ⟨x⟩ₙ = α·xₙ + (1−α)·⟨x⟩ₙ₋₁ over the three accumulators (LR, L², R²). ρ=+1 mono/in-phase (mono-safe), ρ=0 fully decorrelated (max natural width), ρ=−1 polarity-inverted (cancels in mono). Frequency-domain per-bin coherence variant: Cr(k)=X_L(k)·X_R(k)* / (|X_L(k)|·|X_R(k)|), magnitude in [0,1], phase = inter-channel phase; can be shown as a coherence-vs-frequency curve.
techniques.what_it_reveals: A single trustworthy number for mono compatibility and the degree of decorrelation — catches phase problems even when the goniometer figure is ambiguous, and (in the per-bin form) tells you WHICH frequencies are out of phase (e.g. only the reverb tail or only the sub).
techniques.why_breathtaking: Not a beauty technique on its own (modest score), but as a thin animated gradient bar under the goniometer it completes a strikingly clean pro-metering aesthetic and gives the color logic for everything else.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The EMA time-constant α auto-adapts: short window for transient material, long for sustained, chosen from detected crest factor so the meter is neither jittery nor sluggish. The green/yellow/red thresholds can shift with program type.
techniques.implementation_notes: Three multiply-accumulates per sample — essentially free. Frequency-domain coherence reuses the rustfft you already have. The only subtlety is the EMA leak factor (α≈1−e^(−1/(τ·fs))) to set the integration time in seconds.

techniques.name: Delay-embedding phase portrait x(t) vs x(t+τ) (Takens reconstruction)
techniques.one_line: From a MONO signal, plot the point cloud (x(t), x(t+τ)) — Takens' theorem says this 2D (or 3D) shadow has the same topology as the sound's true dynamical attractor; pure tones make ellipses, rich timbres make ornate looping orbits, noise fills the square.
techniques.the_math: Delay vector: v(t) = [x(t), x(t+τ), x(t+2τ), …, x(t+(m−1)τ)]. Takens: for embedding dimension m > 2·d_A (d_A = attractor box-counting dimension), the delay map is a diffeomorphic embedding — the reconstructed orbit preserves the true phase-space topology. 2D scope uses m=2: a scatter/orbit of (x(t), x(t+τ)). A perfect sinusoid x=sin(ωt) gives an ellipse whose tilt/eccentricity = cos(ωτ): at ωτ=π/2 it's a circle, at ωτ=π a 45° line — so the SAME math as Lissajous, but self-referential. Harmonics add inner loops; period-doubling (e.g. a growl/subharmonic) splits the orbit into 2 nested loops — visible bifurcation.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The nonlinear / periodic structure of timbre that neither waveform nor spectrum shows directly: number of independent oscillation modes, period-doubling and chaos in growls/distortion/vocal fry, and how 'noisy' vs 'tonal' a sound is (thin closed loop = pure pitch; space-filling fuzz = noise). It's the geometry of periodicity itself.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The orbits are genuinely beautiful and uncanny — a flute traces a clean glowing ellipse, an overdriven guitar a tangled knot, a snare an exploding cloud. With the beam shader and a 3D (m=3) rotating embedding it's mesmerizing and feels like seeing the 'soul' of the sound.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This is the standout for autonomy. τ is chosen AUTOMATICALLY as the first minimum of Average Mutual Information I(τ)=Σ P(xᵢ,xᵢ₊τ)·log₂[P(xᵢ,xᵢ₊τ)/(P(xᵢ)P(xᵢ₊τ))] (Fraser–Swinney) — so the portrait expands to the signal's own dominant period instead of a fixed lag. Embedding dimension m is chosen by False-Nearest-Neighbors (increase m until the fraction of neighbors that 'unfold' apart drops below threshold). Result: the scope literally re-dimensions and re-scales its delay to whatever is playing, frame by frame.
techniques.implementation_notes: Geometry is a ring-buffer lookup — trivial CPU. The intelligence is the AMI/FNN estimator: bin samples into a histogram (e.g. 32–64 bins), compute I(τ) over a sweep of τ (a few ms range), take the first local min — a few thousand ops, run every ~100 ms on a downsampled window, not per-frame. m=3 embedding rendered as a slowly auto-rotating 3D point cloud on wgpu. Gotcha: AMI is noisy on short windows — smooth I(τ) before min-finding; clamp τ to a sane [0.1·period, 0.5·period] band as a fallback.

references.title: Takens's theorem — Wikipedia

Fraser & Swinney, Independent coordinates for strange attractors from mutual information (Phys. Rev. A 1986)

techniques.name: Recurrence plot of the embedded signal (RQA)
techniques.one_line: Take the delay-embedded trajectory and draw a 2D black/white image R(i,j)=1 when states at times i and j are close — periodic sound makes parallel diagonals, structure changes make blocks, noise makes a speckle field.
techniques.the_math: Recurrence matrix: R(i,j) = Θ(ε − ‖v(i) − v(j)‖), Θ = Heaviside step, ε = threshold radius, v = delay vectors from the embedding above. The plot is symmetric with a main diagonal (i=j). Diagonal line of length ℓ ⇒ the trajectory revisits the same path ℓ steps later ⇒ periodicity at that lag. RQA scalar features: %recurrence (density of 1s), determinism DET = (fraction of points on diagonals ≥ ℓ_min), L_max (longest diagonal → predictability time / inverse of largest Lyapunov exponent), entropy of diagonal-length distribution. ε is often set so recurrence density ≈ 1–5%.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Long-range temporal self-similarity and form: repeated phrases, loop points, tempo/meter regularity (diagonal spacing = period), and transitions between sections (block boundaries) — structure across seconds that no instantaneous scope can show. DET and L_max quantify how predictable/periodic vs chaotic the sound is.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The plots are striking abstract textures — gridlines for a drum loop, fading diagonals for a decaying note, chaotic confetti for noise — and they encode musical FORM as visible pattern. More cerebral than the glowing scopes but high-insight.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: ε is auto-set from a target recurrence density (e.g. solve for ε giving 2% ones) so the image has good contrast for any signal level — a self-calibrating threshold. τ and m inherit the AMI/FNN auto-selection from the phase-portrait technique. Window length can auto-track to a few periods of the detected fundamental so the texture stays legible.
techniques.implementation_notes: R is O(N²) in window length — keep N small (e.g. 256–512 embedded points over a ~1–2 s window) and update incrementally (one new row/column per hop) rather than recomputing. Render as a texture in egui. The auto-ε bisection is cheap. Gotcha: O(N²) memory/time forces a downsampled, windowed view — this is a 'slow scope' panel, not a per-audio-frame visual.

cross_links: Lissajous tilt cos(ωτ) is mathematically identical to the delay-embedding ellipse and to the stereo phase scope — the same rotation/phase math unifies stereo (x=L,y=R) and self-referential (x=x(t),y=x(t+τ)) scopes; a single shared renderer serves both.

The glowing-beam Gaussian-erf line renderer is a general primitive: it should also drive any spectrogram-trajectory, partial-tracking, or sinusoidal-model path in the frequency-domain facets, not just XY scopes.

Inter-channel correlation ρ and per-bin coherence Cr(k) connect to the spectrogram/STFT facet (stereo or L/R difference spectrograms) and to spatial/ambisonic facets (the same M/S decomposition generalizes to spatial encoding).
Recurrence plots and RQA bridge this facet to self-similarity/structure-analysis facets (SSMs from MFCC/chroma in MIR) — same diagonal-detection math, different feature space.
Autonomous tau via AMI and dimension via false-nearest-neighbors is a reusable auto-dimensioning engine that can set window sizes, hop sizes, and lag axes across other time-frequency facets.

summary: The Fourier/spectral family is the load-bearing core of sound visualization: the STFT spectrogram |X(t,f)|^2 maps a 1-D waveform onto a 2-D time-frequency intensity field where pitch, harmonics, formants, transients, and noise all become legible spatial structure no ear can isolate. The deepest beauty and insight come not from the raw magnitude image but from how it is shaped: a log-frequency axis that turns octaves into equal pixel spans, a dB-magnitude axis matched to logarithmic loudness perception, window choice trading main-lobe width against side-lobe leakage under the hard Gabor uncertainty floor (sigma_t * sigma_f >= 1/(4 pi), achieved only by a Gaussian window), and zero-padding that sinc-interpolates the spectrum for smooth display without adding real resolution. The two standout techniques for a realtime egui app are the perceptually-axed log/dB spectrogram (the canonical, gorgeous, cheap baseline) and the reassigned spectrogram, which uses the discarded STFT phase to push each energy quantum to its true instantaneous-frequency / group-delay coordinate, collapsing blurry ridges into hairline curves. Autonomous dimensioning is natural here: hop and window can adapt to spectral flux, the dB floor and color map can auto-scale to the running magnitude percentile, and constant-Q binning self-allocates resolution logarithmically across the band.

techniques.name: Log-frequency, dB-magnitude STFT spectrogram (perceptually-axed)
techniques.one_line: The canonical |STFT|^2 heatmap, but with a log2 frequency axis and a 20*log10 dB color scale so octaves and loudness map linearly to screen — the bread-and-butter, done beautifully.
techniques.the_math: STFT: X(m,k) = sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n + mH) w(n) e^{-j 2 pi k n / N}, hop H, window w of length N. Power spectrogram S(m,k) = |X(m,k)|^2. Display magnitude in dB: D(m,k) = 20*log10(|X(m,k)| / A_ref) = 10*log10(S/S_ref), clipped to a floor [D_max - R, D_max] for dynamic range R (e.g. 80 dB). Frequency of bin k: f_k = k*fs/N (linear). Map to screen via log axis y = (log2(f) - log2(f_lo)) / (log2(f_hi) - log2(f_lo)). Hann window w(n)=0.5-0.5cos(2 pi n/N) (=cos^2(pi n/N)); its -31 dB side lobes and 4*Omega_M main-lobe width set the leakage/resolution tradeoff vs rectangular (-13 dB) or Blackman-Harris (3-term: a0=0.42438,a1=0.49734,a2=0.07829, ~-71 dB). Color = colormap(clamp((D - floor)/R, 0, 1)).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Harmonic stacks (integer-multiple ridges revealing pitch and timbre), formant bands in speech/vowels, vibrato and pitch glides as wavy ridges, inharmonicity, beating between close partials, broadband transients as vertical streaks, and the noise floor texture — none separable by ear. The log-f axis makes a musical octave a constant vertical distance so chords and harmony become geometric patterns; the dB axis exposes quiet partials 60-80 dB below the fundamental that a linear scale crushes to black.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It is the iconic, instantly-readable image of sound: a glowing river of harmonics on black. Perceptual axes make it both physically honest and gorgeous — a piano chord becomes parallel luminous combs, a voice becomes flowing formant ribbons. With a good colormap (magma/viridis/inferno) and additive glow it reads as living light.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Window length N and hop H can auto-adapt to content: track spectral flux SF(m)=sum_k (|X(m,k)|-|X(m-1,k)|)_+ and shrink N (sharper time) where flux is high, grow N (sharper frequency) on sustained tones. The dB floor and ceiling auto-scale to running percentiles of |X| (e.g. ceiling = 99th pct, floor = ceiling - R) so the colormap always fills its range regardless of input gain. Frequency axis limits f_lo/f_hi snap to the band that actually carries energy (first/last bin above floor). Bins-per-octave for a log/CQT layout scale to available pixel height.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivially realtime in Rust: rustfft for the DFT, a ring buffer of windowed frames, hop ~25% of N (75% overlap with Hann for COLA). N=1024-4096 at 44.1 kHz. Upload the dB frame as a 1-D texture column scrolling a wgpu/glow quad (R8 or R16F), or paint via egui's texture API for CPU-only. dB and log-f remap done in the fragment shader or a precomputed bin->row LUT. Watch: log-f resampling of linearly-spaced bins needs interpolation (nearest looks blocky at low f); precompute per-row source-bin weights once. 9/10 feasibility.

references.title: Short-time Fourier transform — Wikipedia
Spectrum Analysis Windows — Smith, Spectral Audio Signal Processing (CCRMA)
Logarithms and Decibels — Smith, Mathematics of the DFT

techniques.name: Reassigned spectrogram (time-frequency reassignment)
techniques.one_line: Use the STFT's discarded phase to relocate every spectrogram pixel's energy to its true instantaneous-frequency and group-delay coordinate, turning blurry ridges into razor-thin curves.
techniques.the_math: Compute three STFTs of the frame with three windows: w (original), T_w(n)=n*w(n) (time-ramped, n measured from window center), and D_w = dw/dn (derivative window; closed form for Hann). Let X_w, X_Tw, X_Dw be their STFTs. Reassigned time and frequency corrections (Auger-Flandrin / Fulop-Fitz efficient form): t_hat(m,k) = t_m + Re{ X_Tw(m,k) * conj(X_w(m,k)) / |X_w(m,k)|^2 }, omega_hat(m,k) = omega_k - Im{ X_Dw(m,k) * conj(X_w(m,k)) / |X_w(m,k)|^2 }. Equivalently these are the phase derivatives: channelized instantaneous frequency CIF = d/dt arg X, local group delay LGD = -d/domega arg X. Then accumulate energy |X_w(m,k)|^2 into the output bin at (t_hat, omega_hat) instead of (t_m, omega_k).
techniques.what_it_reveals: The true instantaneous frequency trajectory of each partial and the precise onset time of each transient — information that classical |STFT|^2 smears over the window's main lobe. Sinusoidal components collapse to ~1-pixel-wide curves; impulses collapse to vertical hairlines; chirps become clean diagonal threads. It deconvolves the window's blur, so two partials closer than the main-lobe width can separate, and you can literally see micro-vibrato, glissando curvature, and onset timing the magnitude image hides.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It transforms a soft watercolor smear into an etched line drawing of the sound — same data, dramatically more crystalline. Ridges that were fat fuzzy bands become glowing filaments; a struck note's attack snaps into a knife-edge. The aesthetic jump from blurry to hyper-sharp is genuinely startling on screen.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Reassignment is intrinsically self-adapting: each coefficient computes its own corrected location, so resolution is allocated by the signal, not a fixed grid — no global window-size compromise. A consistency/concentration test (reassign only where the mixed second-order phase derivative indicates a pure line or impulse) auto-prunes noise points, so the displayed point density scales to how 'tonal' the audio is. Output accumulation grid resolution can be chosen finer than the analysis bins since positions are continuous.
techniques.implementation_notes: ~3x the FFT cost (three STFTs per frame) — still easily realtime with rustfft for N<=4096. D_w for a Hann window is analytic: w'(n) = (pi/N) sin(2 pi n/N). Scatter-accumulate into a float histogram texture; on GPU do it as point splatting (additive blending) in wgpu, on CPU as a guarded 2-D add. Gotchas: divide-by-near-zero where |X_w|~0 (gate on a magnitude threshold), and corrections can land outside the frame (clamp/discard). Scatter on CPU is the main perf risk for large grids; bucket by column. 7/10 feasibility.

references.title: A Unified Theory of Time-Frequency Reassignment — Fulop & Fitz (arXiv:0903.3080)
Algorithms for computing the time-corrected instantaneous frequency (reassigned) spectrogram — Fulop & Fitz, JASA 2006
Time-Frequency reassignment: from principles to algorithms — Flandrin et al.

techniques.name: Constant-Q / log-frequency transform spectrogram (CQT)
techniques.one_line: A geometrically-spaced filter bank with constant Q = f_k/Δf_k so every bin spans the same musical interval — finer frequency resolution in the bass, finer time resolution in the treble, automatically.
techniques.the_math: Center frequencies geometric: f_k = f_min * 2^(k/B), B bins per octave, k=0..K-1, K = B * n_octaves. Constant quality factor Q = f_k / Δf_k = 1/(2^(1/B) - 1). Per-bin window length N_k = Q * fs / f_k (longer at low f, shorter at high f → every bin holds the same number of cycles). CQT coefficient: X^CQ(k) = (1/N_k) sum_{n=0}^{N_k-1} x(n) w_{N_k}(n) e^{-j 2 pi Q n / N_k}. Display as |X^CQ| in dB on a y-axis already linear in log-frequency (one row per bin). Realtime/invertible variants use a nonstationary Gabor frame (Velasco/Holighaus/Schörkhuber).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Pitch and harmony directly: in CQT a constant interval is a constant pixel shift, so a transposed melody is the same pattern slid vertically, and overtone spacing of harmonic sounds becomes interval-invariant. It reveals chord structure, key, and pitch contour far more legibly than a linear-f STFT, and gives the bass the long windows it needs (resolving adjacent low notes) without over-blurring snappy high-frequency transients.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Music looks like music: octaves stack as evenly-spaced glowing rails, a chromatic run climbs a perfect staircase, vibrato is a sine ripple of constant amplitude regardless of register. The bass region gets dense, detailed structure while cymbals stay crisp — a balanced, organic image the linear spectrogram can't produce.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This IS autonomous dimensioning baked into the transform: resolution is allocated per-bin by frequency (time-frequency tradeoff varies continuously across the axis) instead of one global window. B (bins/octave) can auto-scale to pixel height; f_min/f_max snap to the active band; per-bin N_k self-derives from Q and f_k. The Q factor itself can adapt — raise B (sharper pitch) for tonal passages, lower it (sharper time) for percussive ones, detected via spectral flatness.
techniques.implementation_notes: More involved than a plain STFT: per-bin kernels of different lengths. Efficient route is the sparse spectral kernel (Brown-Puckette): precompute the CQT as a sparse complex matrix applied to one big FFT of the frame, so runtime is one FFT plus a sparse matvec — very realtime. Build kernels once at startup. Use an existing CQT crate or port the Schörkhuber toolbox math. Latency at low f is inherent (long N_k); for live display use the highest-octave block-rate and stagger octaves. 6/10 feasibility (kernel construction is the work; runtime is cheap).

references.title: Calculation of a Constant Q Spectral Transform — Judith C. Brown, JASA 1991
Constant-Q transform toolbox for music processing — Schörkhuber & Klapuri
Constant-Q transform — Wikipedia

techniques.name: Window-controlled leakage visualization under the Gabor limit
techniques.one_line: Make the time-frequency uncertainty tradeoff itself the visual: interactively/automatically morph the analysis window so the user sees leakage skirts tighten and ridges narrow, never beating the Gaussian-optimal sigma_t*sigma_f = 1/(4 pi) floor.
techniques.the_math: Windowed sinusoid spectrum = W(omega - omega_0): the true line is convolved with the window's Fourier transform W, whose main-lobe width and side-lobe level set resolution and leakage. Rectangular: main lobe 4 pi/N, first side lobe -13 dB, rolloff -6 dB/oct. Hann: 8 pi/N (2x wider), -31 dB, -18 dB/oct. Hamming: a=0.54, side lobes ~-43 dB. Blackman-Harris (3-term): -71 dB. Gabor/Heisenberg uncertainty: sigma_t * sigma_f >= 1/(4 pi), with equality iff w is Gaussian w(t)=e^{-a t^2} (the Gabor transform). Effective resolutions: Δf ≈ ENBW * fs/N where ENBW = N * sum w^2 / (sum w)^2 (equivalent noise bandwidth, the window's true resolution cost). Zero-padding to length L>N multiplies the displayed bin count but only sinc-interpolates: X_pad samples the same underlying DTFT W convolved spectrum on a denser grid (aliased-sinc / Dirichlet kernel), adding no new information.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Exactly why a peak is fat or a weak tone is buried: high side lobes (rectangular) drown a quiet partial sitting next to a loud one; a wide main lobe (Blackman-Harris) merges two close tones. Sweeping the window exposes which features are real signal vs leakage artifacts, and visually demonstrates the hard physical floor — you can sharpen frequency only by trading away time, and never past the Gaussian optimum. It also makes vivid that zero-padding's prettier curves are interpolation, not resolution.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Watching the spectral skirts collapse from a messy ragged base into a clean spike as the window morphs, or the ridge breathe between a sharp vertical (short window) and a sharp horizontal (long window), is a mesmerizing, physics-revealing animation — the uncertainty principle made into motion you can feel.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The system can auto-select the window/N per frame to minimize a concentration cost (e.g. Renyi entropy or kurtosis of the magnitude column — lower entropy = more concentrated = better-matched window), effectively letting the audio choose its own optimal time-frequency tradeoff. ENBW gives an automatic, window-independent measure to label the true Δf on the axis. Zero-pad factor auto-scales to pixel density so display interpolation matches screen resolution without wasted FFT.
techniques.implementation_notes: Cheapest of all: just recompute the window coefficients (closed-form for Hann/Hamming/Blackman-Harris/Gaussian) and reuse the same rustfft plan; optionally compute 2-3 windows in parallel for a side-by-side. Entropy/kurtosis concentration metric is a cheap reduction over each column for auto-selection. Animate by interpolating window parameter and recomputing — fully realtime. The only subtlety is labeling axes with ENBW-true resolution rather than naive fs/N. 9/10 feasibility.

references.title: Window function — Wikipedia (coefficients, side-lobe levels, ENBW)
Gabor uncertainty / Heisenberg-Gabor limit — Fourier, Gabor, Morlet or Wigner (arXiv:2101.06707)
Spectral Interpolation / zero-padding is sinc interpolation — Smith, SASP

techniques.name: Welch / averaged power spectral density profile (instantaneous spectrum analyzer)
techniques.one_line: A single live frequency-vs-power curve (the spectrum analyzer line), variance-reduced by averaging overlapping windowed periodograms, with a properly normalized PSD so the y-axis is true power density.
techniques.the_math: Per-block periodogram: P_m(k) = (1/(fs * sum_n w(n)^2)) * |sum_n x(n+mR) w(n) e^{-j 2 pi k n/N}|^2 (the sum w^2 normalization makes it a consistent PSD in units of power/Hz; divide by fs for one-sided density and double non-DC/Nyquist bins). Welch estimate = average over K overlapping blocks: S_hat(k) = (1/K) sum_{m=0}^{K-1} P_m(k). Overlap R = N/2 (50%) with Hann is the classic choice; averaging cuts the periodogram's ~100% variance by ~1/K_eff. Display 10*log10(S_hat) vs log-f. Equivalent exponential running average for realtime: S_hat_t = alpha * P_t + (1-alpha) * S_hat_{t-1}, alpha in (0,1] setting the smoothing time constant.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The stable spectral envelope and noise floor: the raw single-frame spectrum is so noisy (variance equal to the mean, regardless of N) that real spectral shape is hard to read; averaging exposes the true distribution of power across frequency, narrowband tones poking out of broadband noise, room modes, hum harmonics, and the spectral tilt/timbre of a source. It quantifies how much power lives in each band — the energy budget of the sound.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A clean, smoothly-breathing analyzer curve with a glowing peak-hold trace and a filled gradient under the line is a classic, satisfying piece of audio-UI eye-candy — calm and legible where the raw spectrum is a jittery mess. Peak-hold 'stalactites' and a slow decaying envelope give it life.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The smoothing factor alpha (or block count K) can auto-adapt to stationarity: detect spectral flux and average more (smoother) when the signal is steady, less (more responsive) during transients, so the curve is both stable and snappy when it needs to be. The dB y-range auto-scales to running min/max with hysteresis; peak-hold decay rate scales to observed dynamics. Bandwidth/resolution (N) trades off against variance (K) under a fixed total sample budget — auto-balanced by a target confidence-interval width.
techniques.implementation_notes: The simplest technique here and rock-solid realtime: one rustfft per hop, square, normalize by fs*sum(w^2), exponential-average into a persistent Vec<f32>, plot as an egui line/filled-shape with a second peak-hold Vec that decays each frame. No GPU needed. Only real care points are the correct PSD normalization (sum w^2, one-sided doubling) and not confusing power (PSD, sum-of-squares norm) with amplitude (coherent-gain, sum-of-window norm). 10/10 feasibility.

key_references.title: Spectrum Analysis Windows — J.O. Smith, Spectral Audio Signal Processing (CCRMA)
key_references.why: Authoritative closed-form window definitions (rectangular, Hann, Hamming, Blackman-Harris), exact main-lobe widths and side-lobe dB levels — the leakage/resolution tradeoff that governs every spectrogram.

key_references.title: A Unified Theory of Time-Frequency Reassignment — Fulop & Fitz (arXiv:0903.3080)
key_references.why: The reassignment math: relocating energy via phase derivatives / STFT ratios with time-ramped and derivative windows. The basis for the sharpest, most insightful spectral technique here.

key_references.title: Calculation of a Constant Q Spectral Transform — Judith C. Brown, JASA 1991
key_references.why: Original CQT: geometric f_k = f_min*2^(k/B), constant Q, per-bin window N_k = Q*fs/f_k — the log-frequency transform that self-allocates resolution and makes music look like music.

key_references.title: Window function — Wikipedia
key_references.why: Comprehensive window coefficient tables, ENBW (equivalent noise bandwidth) for true-resolution axis labeling, and side-lobe/rolloff specs across the whole window zoo — practical reference for implementation.

key_references.title: Welch's Method — J.O. Smith (CCRMA)
key_references.why: Exact averaged-periodogram PSD equation and normalization — the variance-reduction math behind a stable, correctly-scaled live spectrum-analyzer curve.

key_references.title: Fourier, Gabor, Morlet or Wigner: Comparison of Time-Frequency Transforms (arXiv:2101.06707)
key_references.why: States the Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty bound sigma_t*sigma_f >= 1/(4 pi) and the Gaussian-window equality case — the hard physical floor every spectral visualization works against.

key_references.title: Spectral Interpolation (zero-padding = sinc interpolation) — J.O. Smith, SASP
key_references.why: Proves zero-padding sinc-interpolates the spectrum (Dirichlet kernel) and does NOT add resolution — critical for honest display-resolution vs true-resolution autonomous scaling.

cross_links: Wavelet / multiresolution facet: CQT and the window-uncertainty technique are the bridge — the constant-Q log-frequency tiling is mathematically a wavelet/Morlet-like dyadic scaling, and superlets/synchrosqueezing extend reassignment into the wavelet domain.

Phase / instantaneous-frequency facet: reassignment is entirely driven by the STFT phase derivatives (channelized instantaneous frequency and local group delay) that the magnitude spectrogram throws away — phase-vocoder, synchrosqueezing, and analytic-signal/Hilbert methods share this machinery.

Perceptual / psychoacoustic facet: the dB-magnitude axis (loudness ~ log) and mel scale (m = 2595*log10(1+f/700)) and Bark/critical-band warping are perceptual reparameterizations of this same |STFT|^2 surface.

Chroma / music-information-retrieval facet: folding the CQT log-frequency axis modulo one octave yields the 12-bin chromagram for key/chord visualization; spectral flux from the STFT drives onset detection and the adaptive hop/window dimensioning.

Color-mapping / rendering facet: choice of perceptually-uniform colormap (viridis/magma) and additive glow blending on the GPU is what turns the raw dB matrix into a breathtaking image, shared with every 2-D-field visualization facet.

facet: Advanced time-frequency: scalograms (CWT), constant-Q transform, reassignment, synchrosqueezing, Wigner-Ville, S-transform, multitaper
summary: The vanilla STFT spectrogram is locked to a single window, so it pays a fixed Heisenberg tax (Δt·Δf ≥ 1/4π) everywhere on the plane: bass is time-smeared, transients are frequency-smeared, and harmonics blur into ridges. Every method in this facet breaks that single-window constraint in a different way. The CWT (Morlet scalogram) and the constant-Q transform give multi-resolution log-frequency tilings — wide windows for bass, narrow for treble — which is exactly how pitch and music are structured, so octaves become equally-spaced stripes. Reassignment and synchrosqueezing then take ANY of these representations and push each energy grain to the local center of gravity computed from the phase gradient, collapsing fat ridges into near-delta lines (synchrosqueezing keeps it invertible by only moving in frequency). The Wigner-Ville distribution achieves the theoretical maximum concentration (it saturates the uncertainty bound for a chirp) at the cost of bilinear cross-terms; the S-transform is a phase-referenced hybrid between STFT and CWT with absolutely-referenced phase; and the multitaper estimator trades a little resolution for a dramatic variance reduction (~factor K) that makes noisy/turbulent textures readable. The two highest-payoff techniques for a breathtaking, self-scaling visualizer are the synchrosqueezed CQT (razor-sharp musical lines that auto-tile by octave) and reassignment (a cheap algebraic post-process on an FFT you already compute).

techniques.name: Continuous Wavelet Transform / Morlet scalogram
techniques.one_line: Multi-resolution log-frequency tiling: a single Gaussian-windowed complex sinusoid stretched across scales so bass gets long windows and treble gets short ones.
techniques.the_math: CWT: W_x(a,b) = (1/√a) ∫ x(t) ψ*((t−b)/a) dt, scale a>0, translation b. Complex Morlet (Gabor) mother wavelet: ψ_σ(t) = c_σ π^(−1/4) e^(−t²/2)(e^(iσt) − κ_σ), with admissibility correction κ_σ = e^(−σ²/2) and normalization c_σ = (1 + e^(−σ²) − 2e^(−3σ²/4))^(−1/2); σ = central (carrier) frequency, typically ω₀ ≈ 5–6 so κ is negligible. Fourier transform: ψ̂_σ(ω) = c_σ π^(−1/4)[e^(−(σ−ω)²/2) − κ_σ e^(−ω²/2)]. Scale↔frequency map: f = ω₀ / (2π a) (so each scale a is a center frequency). Scalogram = |W_x(a,b)|². Q is constant: Δf/f = const because the wavelet self-scales with a — relative bandwidth is fixed, giving a logarithmic frequency lattice for free.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals transients and pitch structure simultaneously that a fixed-window STFT cannot: a kick-drum click stays a sharp vertical event (short effective window at high f) while a sustained bass note resolves in pitch (long effective window at low f). Octave-related harmonics sit at geometrically spaced scales, so musical intervals become visually self-similar. Exposes glissandi/vibrato as smooth curved ridges rather than staircased blocks.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The scalogram has an organic, fractal, flame-like quality — energy fans out in self-similar plumes because the basis is self-similar. Smooth curved ridges (chirps, portamento) render as elegant flowing filaments instead of the blocky terraces of an STFT, and the eye reads the log-frequency layout as natural musical space.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-scaling is intrinsic, not bolted on: the analyzing atom dilates with scale a, so time resolution is automatically fine where frequency is high and coarse where it is low — the representation adapts its own time–frequency cell aspect ratio to the content's frequency. Choose scales geometrically a_j = a_0·2^(j/B) (B voices per octave) and the frequency axis auto-tiles to whatever Nyquist/lowest-bin the signal spans; the only free knob (ω₀) trades time-vs-frequency concentration and can be set from an estimate of the signal's onset density.
techniques.implementation_notes: Two realtime paths in Rust. (1) FFT-domain: precompute ψ̂_σ(aω) for each scale once, multiply the rfft of a frame, inverse-FFT — rustfft handles this; cost ≈ N_scales × FFT. (2) Direct convolution for few scales. For a streaming visualizer, ~48–96 scales × a 4096-pt FFT at 60 fps is comfortable on CPU; offload the per-scale complex multiply to a wgpu compute shader if scale count is high. Watch edge effects (cone of influence) — taper or mirror-pad. Color by |W| and you have a GPU-friendly texture upload per frame.

references.title: Morlet wavelet — definition and Fourier transform (Wikipedia)
Daubechies, Lu, Wu — Synchrosqueezed wavelet transforms (CWT background), arXiv:0912.2437

techniques.name: Constant-Q Transform (CQT)
techniques.one_line: A bank of log-spaced filters whose center-frequency-to-bandwidth ratio Q is constant, so each musical semitone occupies the same pixel width and octaves are equally spaced stripes.
techniques.the_math: Center frequencies geometric: f_k = f_min · 2^(k/B), B = bins per octave (12 = semitones, 24/36/48 = finer). Constant quality factor Q = f_k / δf_k = 1/(2^(1/B) − 1) (e.g. B=12 → Q≈16.8; B=24 → Q≈34.1). Per-bin window length N_k = ⌈Q · f_s / f_k⌉ (long for low bins, short for high). Transform: X[k] = (1/N_k) Σ_{n=0}^{N_k−1} w_k[n] x[n] e^(−i 2π Q n / N_k), where w_k is a window (Hann/Blackman) of length N_k. Efficient Brown–Puckette form: precompute a sparse spectral kernel K[k,·] = FFT(w_k[n] e^(−i2πQn/N_k}) once, then X = (1/N) X̂ · K* where X̂ = FFT(x) — one big FFT per frame plus a sparse matrix multiply. Schörkhuber–Klapuri rasterized CQT computes octave-by-octave on recursively halved sample rates for perfect reconstruction.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals harmonic/pitch structure that a linear-frequency STFT hides: in an FFT, low notes cram into a few bins and high octaves waste hundreds of bins, so chords look lopsided. In CQT every pitch class lines up on a fixed grid — a transposition is a vertical shift, a chord is a fixed shape, harmonics of a note sit at log2(n) offsets. Makes key, chord voicing, and timbre-vs-pitch separation directly legible.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It is the representation that looks like music — a piano-roll-meets-spectrogram where harmony has geometry. Overlay a note grid and the visualization reads as a living score; pitch-class color wheels (mapping the 12 bins-per-octave residue to hue) make tonality glow. This is the spectrogram that musicians find beautiful because it matches their mental model.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Strongly self-dimensioning: the frequency axis IS the parameter — set f_min (e.g. C1≈32.7 Hz), f_max=Nyquist, B, and the bin count k_max = ⌈B·log2(f_max/f_min)⌉ falls out automatically; window length N_k self-adapts per bin to hold Q constant. You can auto-pick f_min from the detected fundamental and B from available vertical pixels (one bin per pixel-row), so the transform re-tiles itself to the instrument's range and the display height.
techniques.implementation_notes: Best realtime path is the precomputed sparse spectral kernel (Brown–Puckette / Schörkhuber–Klapuri): one rfft of the frame (rustfft) then a sparse complex matmul — the kernel is ~99% zeros so it's cheap and cache-friendly. Build the kernel once at startup for the chosen f_min/B. The low octaves need long windows (latency!) — for a live meter, accept that bass updates slower, or use the recursive-downsample octave scheme to bound cost. Entirely CPU-feasible at 60 fps for 7 octaves × 24 bins.

references.title: Constant-Q transform (Wikipedia) — X[k], Q, N[k], geometric bandwidths
Schörkhuber & Klapuri — Constant-Q Transform Toolbox for Music Processing (DAFx-10)
Brown & Puckette — An efficient algorithm for the calculation of a constant Q transform

techniques.name: Method of Reassignment (reassigned spectrogram/scalogram)
techniques.one_line: Take any STFT/CWT, then move each energy grain from its bin center to the local center of gravity computed from the phase gradient — turning blurry ridges into hairline-sharp curves.
techniques.the_math: Compute three STFTs of the same frame with related windows: X with window h, X_Th with the time-weighted window (T h)(t)=t·h(t), and X_Dh with the derivative window (D h)(t)=h'(t). Then reassign each point (t,ω) to: t̂(t,ω) = t − ℜ{ X_Th(t,ω) · X*(t,ω) / |X(t,ω)|² }, ω̂(t,ω) = ω + ℑ{ X_Dh(t,ω) · X*(t,ω) / |X(t,ω)|² }. Equivalently these are local group delay (∂φ/∂ω) and instantaneous frequency (∂φ/∂t) read off the phase φ. Accumulate |X(t,ω)|² into the histogram cell (t̂,ω̂) instead of (t,ω). (Auger–Flandrin generalize this to all of Cohen's class.)
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals the true ridge skeleton hidden under window smear: two close partials that the STFT merges into one fat blob separate into two crisp lines; the exact onset time of a transient snaps to a vertical impulse; chirp slopes become exact. It is, in effect, a deconvolution of the analysis window — you see where the energy *actually* is, not where the window spread it.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The visual transformation is dramatic and immediate — a soft, cloudy spectrogram resolves into a constellation of glowing filaments, like bringing a blurry photo into perfect focus. Harmonic stacks render as clean parallel threads; the 'wow' is the before/after sharpness toggle.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Adaptive by construction: the reassignment vectors are computed per-point from the local signal, so the effective resolution self-adjusts to content — sparse, well-separated components get pinpoint relocation; the window size becomes almost irrelevant because the smear it causes is undone. You can let the display bin size float and still get sub-bin localization, so the achievable resolution scales with the signal's own structure rather than a fixed grid.
techniques.implementation_notes: Very feasible and cheap: it's just THREE FFTs per frame (h, t·h, h') plus per-bin complex arithmetic — all available with rustfft. The only subtlety is the scatter/accumulate into the reassigned histogram (non-uniform write); do it on CPU for moderate sizes or as an atomic-add in a wgpu compute shader for big frames. Threshold low-magnitude bins (skip |X|²<ε) to avoid noisy reassignment. No invertibility (energy moves in both axes) — use synchrosqueezing if you need to reconstruct.

references.title: Reassignment method (Wikipedia) — t̂, ω̂ via X_Th, X_Dh
Fulop & Fitz — A Unified Theory of Time-Frequency Reassignment, arXiv:0903.3080
Auger, Flandrin et al. — Time-Frequency Reassignment and Synchrosqueezing: An Overview

techniques.name: Synchrosqueezing transform (SST)
techniques.one_line: Reassignment that only moves energy in frequency (never time), so the sharpened representation stays invertible — razor-thin ridges you can also resynthesize from.
techniques.the_math: From the CWT W_s(a,b), estimate a candidate instantaneous frequency at every (a,b): ω_s(a,b) = ℜ( ∂_b W_s(a,b) / (i 2π W_s(a,b)) ) wherever W_s≠0 (the phase time-derivative). Then 'squeeze' all scales whose candidate frequency lands near a target frequency ω_l onto that single frequency bin: T_s(ω_l,b) = ∫_{A(ω_l)} W_s(a,b) a^(−3/2) da, A(ω_l)={a : |ω_s(a,b)−ω_l|≤ Δω/2} — i.e. T_s(ω,b)=∫₀^∞ W_s(a,b) a^(−3/2) δ(ω−ω_s(a,b)) da. Because only the frequency coordinate changes, the original signal is recoverable: x(b) ≈ ℜ( C_ψ^{−1} Σ_l T_s(ω_l,b) ) (mode-wise reconstruction by integrating T over the ridge of each component). STFT-based variants (FSST) replace W with the STFT and use ω̂ = ω + ℑ{X_Dh X*/|X|²}.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals individual oscillatory MODES of a multicomponent signal and lets you extract each one: AM/FM components (vibrato + tremolo, beating partials, formant tracks) collapse to clean ridges you can integrate to recover that component's waveform. It exposes instantaneous frequency laws (chirp rates, micro-pitch drift) with the concentration of Wigner-Ville but without cross-terms, and — crucially — it round-trips, so it doubles as an analysis-resynthesis engine.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Same focus-snap beauty as reassignment, but the invertibility enables interactive magic in a visualizer: paint over a ridge and hear/solo that mode, or watch components peel apart. The ridges are even cleaner than reassignment for slowly-varying tones, giving an almost vector-graphic crispness.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-dimensioning through the ω_s map: the frequency reassignment target is derived from the data's own phase, so resolution adapts to each component automatically. The frequency-bin grid Δω can be chosen from the ridge density, and ridge-detection (greedy/dynamic-programming over argmax_ω |T|) auto-discovers the number of modes — the representation tells you its own intrinsic dimensionality (how many components are present).
techniques.implementation_notes: Feasible but heavier than plain reassignment. Need the CWT (or STFT) plus its b-derivative — compute ∂_b W either by an extra wavelet (b-derivative of ψ) or finite difference along b; rustfft does both transforms. Then a frequency-only scatter (1-D accumulate per time column) — simpler than 2-D reassignment and GPU-friendly. Reconstruction is optional for a pure visualizer. Threshold |W| to gate noise before computing ω_s (division by tiny W is unstable). Realtime at moderate scale counts on CPU; wgpu for large.

references.title: Daubechies, Lu, Wu — The Synchrosqueezing algorithm for time-varying spectral analysis, arXiv:1105.0010
Synchrosqueezing Transform (SST) overview — ω_s and T_s(ω,b) formulas
Thakur et al. — Synchrosqueezing: robustness & paleoclimate applications

techniques.name: Wigner-Ville distribution (WVD)
techniques.one_line: The bilinear time-frequency density that saturates the uncertainty bound — perfect concentration on a single chirp — at the price of oscillating cross-terms between every pair of components.
techniques.the_math: W_x(t,f) = ∫ x(t+τ/2) x*(t−τ/2) e^(−i2πfτ) dτ — the Fourier transform over lag τ of the instantaneous autocorrelation R_x(t,τ)=x(t+τ/2)x*(t−τ/2). (Use the analytic signal of x to get the 'Ville' version and avoid spectral aliasing.) Bilinearity → for x=x₁+x₂: W_{x₁+x₂} = W_{x₁} + W_{x₂} + 2ℜ{ W_{x₁,x₂} }, where the cross-WVD W_{x₁,x₂}(t,f)=∫ x₁(t+τ/2) x₂*(t−τ/2) e^{−i2πfτ}dτ sits MIDWAY in time and frequency between the two auto-terms and oscillates at a rate set by their separation. Smoothed/Cohen's-class fix: C_x(t,f) = ∫∫ Φ(θ,τ) A_x(θ,τ) e^{−i2π(θt+fτ)} dθ dτ, Φ a kernel on the ambiguity plane A_x; choosing Φ = a 2-D low-pass (e.g. Choi-Williams e^{−θ²τ²/σ}) suppresses the off-origin cross-terms.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals the *exact* instantaneous-frequency law with the highest possible concentration — for a linear chirp the WVD is a perfect 1-pixel-wide line tracing f(t), something no linear transform achieves. It marginalizes correctly to the true energy spectrum and instantaneous power. The cross-terms themselves encode phase relationships and time/frequency separations between components (interference fringes that literally measure the geometry between two tones).
techniques.why_breathtaking: Visually unique: brilliant, perfectly-thin auto-term ridges plus rippling moiré interference fringes between them — a physics-experiment aesthetic (it is literally the quantum Wigner function). On clean signals it is the sharpest TFR there is; the fringes, while a nuisance for reading, are mesmerizing structured patterns.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Less self-scaling on its own (no window to adapt — that's the point), BUT the adaptive variants are: the optimal-kernel / radially-Gaussian-kernel methods (Baraniuk-Jones) choose Φ on the ambiguity plane FROM the signal to maximally suppress cross-terms while preserving auto-terms — an autonomous, content-driven smoothing that adapts kernel shape per frame. So the 'dimensioning' knob becomes the data-driven ambiguity-domain kernel.
techniques.implementation_notes: Heaviest of the set and trickiest for realtime polyphonic audio (cross-terms explode with component count — a full music mix becomes unreadable without smoothing). Discrete pseudo-WVD with a frequency window: for each time t form the windowed lag product r[τ]=h[τ]x[t+τ]x*[t−τ] and FFT over τ (rustfft). Use the analytic signal (Hilbert via FFT) to halve the lag range and prevent aliasing. Smoothed pseudo-WVD (time + frequency smoothing) is the practical realtime form. Best reserved for solo/monophonic or didactic 'pure chirp' visualizations rather than full mixes.

references.title: Wigner distribution function (Wikipedia) — W_x(t,f) and cross-term structure
Comparison of Time-Frequency Transforms (Wigner included), arXiv:2101.06707
Cross-term suppression in the WVD (overview), IET Signal Processing

techniques.name: Stockwell S-transform
techniques.one_line: A phase-referenced hybrid of STFT and CWT: a Gaussian window whose width scales as 1/|f|, giving frequency-adaptive resolution while keeping absolute (not local) phase.
techniques.the_math: S(τ,f) = ∫ x(t) (|f|/√(2π)) e^{−(τ−t)² f²/2} e^{−i2πft} dt. The Gaussian σ(f)=1/|f| narrows as f grows (CWT-like multiresolution), but the modulation e^{−i2πft} is referenced to the absolute time origin, not to the window center — so phase is globally referenced (unlike the CWT). Generalized window: replace 1/|f| width by a tunable σ(f)=p/|f|^q. Key property: integrating over τ recovers the Fourier spectrum exactly, ∫ S(τ,f) dτ = X(f) (direct, invertible link to the FFT). Efficient compute: S(τ,f) = IFFT_τ{ X(α+f) · Ĝ(α,f) } where X is the FFT of x and Ĝ(α,f)=e^{−2π²α²/f²} is the Gaussian's FT — i.e. one FFT, then per-frequency a windowed shift-and-inverse-FFT.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals frequency content with the multiresolution benefit of wavelets PLUS interpretable absolute phase — you can read true phase relationships across the whole record (useful for transient polarity, dispersion, and aligning events to a global clock). Power-quality and geophysics use it to localize when a frequency event happens and its phase; for audio it gives clean low-frequency localization (wide window) and sharp high-frequency timing (narrow window) with a direct FFT tie-in.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Produces smooth, naturally-balanced spectrograms — the |f| amplitude factor and frequency-adaptive window make high-frequency detail pop without the bass washing out, giving a polished, high-dynamic-range look. The absolute-phase channel can be color-mapped to hue (magnitude→brightness, phase→hue) for a vivid phase-painted display.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Inherently self-scaling like the CWT (window width is a function of f, not a fixed constant), so time–frequency cell aspect ratio adapts automatically across the band. The generalized S-transform exposes (p,q) knobs that can be auto-tuned to the signal (e.g. broaden the window for tonal content, narrow for percussive) — a content-adaptive resolution dial bolted onto a fixed-FFT backbone.
techniques.implementation_notes: Feasible and FFT-friendly: compute X=FFT(x) once (rustfft), then for each output frequency f multiply the frequency-shifted spectrum by the precomputed Gaussian Ĝ(·,f) and inverse-FFT — cost ≈ N_freq × FFT, comparable to a dense CWT. Precompute all Ĝ Gaussians at startup. Naive S-transform is O(N² log N)-ish, so for realtime limit the number of output frequencies (it's a visualizer, you only need ~display-height rows) or use the discrete orthonormal/fast-S variants. CPU-feasible at moderate row counts; GPU for full resolution.

references.title: Stockwell S-transform formula and frequency-dependent Gaussian (survey), arXiv:2402.06645
A Brief Overview of the S-transforms (RIMS Kyoto)
The S-transform with windows of arbitrary and varying shape

techniques.name: Multitaper spectrogram (Thomson / DPSS-Slepian)
techniques.one_line: Average K independent eigenspectra taken with orthogonal Slepian tapers to cut variance by ~K — turning a noisy, speckled spectrogram into a smooth, statistically stable image.
techniques.the_math: Choose time-bandwidth product NW; take K ≈ 2NW−1 tapers h_k[n] = the Discrete Prolate Spheroidal Sequences (Slepian sequences), the eigenvectors of the concentration problem maximizing energy of the FT inside [−W,W]: they solve Σ_m [sin(2πW(n−m))/(π(n−m))] h_k[m] = λ_k h_k[n], with eigenvalues λ_k≈1 (well-concentrated) for k<2NW. Each eigenspectrum: Ŝ_k(f) = | Σ_{n} h_k[n] x[n] e^{−i2πfn} |². Combine: simple average Ŝ(f) = (1/K) Σ_{k=0}^{K−1} Ŝ_k(f), or Thomson adaptive weighting Ŝ(f)=Σ_k d_k(f)² Ŝ_k(f) / Σ_k d_k(f)², with weights d_k(f)=√λ_k S(f)/(λ_k S(f)+(1−λ_k)σ²) that downweight tapers leaking broadband bias. Variance drops by ≈ factor K vs a single tapered periodogram; resolution bandwidth is 2W.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals the STABLE underlying spectral shape buried in estimation noise: a single-taper spectrogram of noise/turbulence/breath/reverb tails is wildly speckled (χ²₂ at every bin), and that speckle masks weak, broad, or stochastic structure (noise floors, formant envelopes, the colored-noise character of a texture). Multitaper exposes those broadband features and gives statistically defensible peaks (you can put error bars / F-test harmonic significance on lines).
techniques.why_breathtaking: The aesthetic is 'creamy' — smooth, photographic gradients instead of TV-static grain. Stochastic/ambient material (rain, wind, reverberant tails, pads) renders as soft glowing fields with readable structure, a markedly more refined look than the jittery raw spectrogram. Pair with a sharpener (CQT/reassignment for tones) to get both crisp lines and smooth fields.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-dimensioning via the single parameter NW, which sets BOTH the resolution bandwidth (2W) and the taper count K=2NW−1 — pick NW from the desired variance/resolution tradeoff and everything else follows. NW can be auto-set from the local spectral flatness / SNR (more tapers where the signal is noisy and broadband, fewer where it is tonal and you want resolution), giving a content-adaptive smoothing budget. Adaptive weights d_k(f) further self-tune per frequency.
techniques.implementation_notes: Feasible: precompute the K DPSS tapers once at startup (eigen-solve the tridiagonal Slepian matrix — there's a classic tridiagonal formulation; or ship precomputed tables) since they depend only on N and NW, not the data. Per frame: K windowed FFTs (rustfft) and average — cost = K× a normal spectrogram (K typically 3–8, so ~3–8× a plain STFT, easily realtime). Embarrassingly parallel across tapers → trivial to thread or run as K FFTs on GPU. Adaptive weighting adds a cheap per-bin iteration. This is the lowest-risk, highest-polish add-on of the set.

references.title: Multitaper (Wikipedia) — Ŝ(f) averaging, DPSS, NW, K=2NW−1
Thomson's Multitaper Method Revisited, arXiv:2103.11586
Multi-Taper Method (MTM) — UCLA Theoretical Climate Dynamics

key_references.title: Auger, Flandrin, Lin, McLaughlin, Meignen, Oberlin, Wu — Time-Frequency Reassignment and Synchrosqueezing: An Overview
key_references.why: The single best survey tying reassignment and synchrosqueezing together — both as phase-gradient relocation, one invertible (SST), one not. Defines the candidate IF map and the squeezing integral, and explains why SST keeps reconstruction. Foundational for the two sharpest techniques in this facet.

key_references.title: Daubechies, Lu, Wu — The Synchrosqueezing algorithm for time-varying spectral analysis (arXiv:1105.0010)
key_references.why: Canonical synchrosqueezing paper: gives ω_s(a,b) from the CWT phase derivative, the T_s(ω,b) reassignment-in-frequency integral with the a^(−3/2) Jacobian, and the mode-wise reconstruction — the math behind invertible razor-sharp ridges.

key_references.title: Fulop & Fitz — A Unified Theory of Time-Frequency Reassignment (arXiv:0903.3080)
key_references.why: Derives the practical 3-FFT reassignment algorithm (window h, time-weighted t·h, derivative h') and the t̂/ω̂ formulas — exactly the cheap realtime recipe to bolt onto an FFT the visualizer already computes.

key_references.title: Schörkhuber & Klapuri — Constant-Q Transform Toolbox for Music Processing (DAFx-10)
key_references.why: Efficient, invertible CQT via sparse spectral kernel and octave-recursive downsampling — the implementation blueprint for a realtime log-frequency musical spectrogram with selectable bins/octave.

key_references.title: Brown & Puckette — An efficient algorithm for the calculation of a constant Q transform (JASA 1992)
key_references.why: Original FFT + frequency-domain-kernel CQT; defines Q, geometric f_k, per-bin window N_k — the math grounding the constant-Q technique.

key_references.title: Multitaper (Wikipedia) and Thomson's Multitaper Method Revisited (arXiv:2103.11586)
key_references.why: DPSS/Slepian tapers, K≈2NW−1, K-fold variance reduction, and adaptive weighting — the statistically-grounded smoothing that makes noisy/ambient textures readable and is the cheapest high-polish add-on.

cross_links: Reassignment and synchrosqueezing are POST-PROCESSORS: apply them on top of the STFT spectrogram (linear-frequency facet) OR on the CQT/CWT here to sharpen any base representation — they compose rather than compete.
Synchrosqueezing's invertibility connects to the analysis-resynthesis / sonification facet: extracted ridges can be resynthesized, so a visualizer doubles as a mode-soloing audio tool.

The CQT's log-frequency, bins-per-octave grid feeds directly into pitch/chroma and music-theory visualizations (chroma vectors, tonnetz, key wheels) — map the 12-per-octave residue to hue for a pitch-class color facet.

Wigner-Ville is the bilinear extreme of Cohen's class; the smoothed-WVD ↔ spectrogram ↔ S-transform relationship links this facet to any Cohen's-class / ambiguity-plane facet, and the optimal-kernel idea ties to autonomous/adaptive-parameter methods.

Multitaper variance reduction pairs with the perceptual/loudness facet: a statistically smooth spectral estimate gives stable per-band energy for meters, mel/Bark mapping, and noise-floor visualization.

The phase channels exploited by reassignment (∂φ/∂t = instantaneous frequency, ∂φ/∂ω = group delay) and the S-transform's absolute phase connect to phase-coloring / phase-vocoder visualization techniques (magnitude→brightness, phase→hue).

facet: Psychoacoustics & perceptual scales — building perceptually-honest sound visuals whose frequency axis, time axis, and brightness match human hearing rather than raw physics
summary: The single highest-leverage idea in this facet: a raw linear-frequency, dB-magnitude spectrogram is a lie about what we hear. The ear is a warped, level-dependent, compressive, masking-driven instrument, and three independent warps must be composed to be honest — a frequency warp (mel/Bark/ERB), an amplitude warp (equal-loudness contours + Stevens' compressive power law to sones), and a masking/gating model (simultaneous + temporal masking, LUFS gating). The most breathtaking-and-honest object is the ERB-rate excitation/specific-loudness "auditory spectrogram" (Moore-Glasberg/Zwicker), where the y-axis is critical-band-spaced, brightness is sones not dB, and masked energy is visibly suppressed — what survives on screen is literally what you can hear. The most autonomous-dimensioning gift of these scales is that they are content-agnostic, fixed perceptual rulers: ERB-rate runs ~0–42 Cams, Bark ~0–24, loudness ~0–N sones — so axes self-calibrate to perception, not to the file. MFCCs add a low-dimensional timbre embedding (decorrelated log-mel via DCT) that auto-reduces a 40-band spectrum to ~13 perceptually-orthogonal axes ideal for color/scatter mapping. The honest move everywhere is: warp the axis to a perceptual scale, map brightness through loudness (sones) not raw power, and gate/suppress what masking makes inaudible.

techniques.name: ERB-rate auditory spectrogram (gammatone / Cam-warped y-axis)
techniques.one_line: Replace the linear Hz y-axis with the ERB-rate (Cam) scale and gammatone filter bandwidths, so equal screen-pixels = equal cochlear distance.
techniques.the_math: ERB bandwidth (Glasberg & Moore 1990): ERB(f) = 24.7·(4.37·f/1000 + 1) = 0.108·f + 24.7 Hz. ERB-rate (cumulative # of ERBs below f, the 'Cam' axis): ERBS(f) = 21.4·log10(0.00437·f + 1). Inverse: f = (10^(E/21.4) − 1)/0.00437. Gammatone filter impulse response (4th-order, order n=4): g(t) = a·t^(n−1)·e^(−2π·b·ERB(fc)·t)·cos(2π·fc·t + φ), with b≈1.019. Center freqs placed at equal ERBS steps: E_k = E0 + kΔ, f_k = ERBS⁻¹(E_k). Excitation = sum of |FFT|² weighted by each gammatone |H(f)|².
techniques.what_it_reveals: Low-frequency harmonic spacing and formants that are crammed into the bottom 1/20th of a linear FFT spread out to fill half the display; high-frequency hiss is compressed. You see vowel formants, bass note partials, and chord voicings resolved the way the cochlea resolves them — structure invisible in a linear plot becomes obvious.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A piano roll-like clarity emerges for free: octaves become roughly equal vertical jumps, and the whole image breathes with the warm low-end emphasis the ear actually has. Pairs beautifully with a perceptual colormap.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The ERB-rate axis is a fixed perceptual ruler (0 Hz → 0 Cam, 20 kHz → ~42.5 Cam) independent of the audio. Number of bands self-scales: pick N filters equally spaced in ERBS to exactly fill the available pixel height, so vertical resolution auto-adapts to window size with no per-file tuning. Bandwidths auto-widen with frequency by construction.
techniques.implementation_notes: Very feasible. rustfft for the STFT, then a precomputed N×K weighting matrix (gammatone or triangular magnitude responses sampled on the FFT grid) → one matrix-vector multiply per frame. N≈128–256 bands fills a display; matrix is constant so it's a cheap GEMV (can go to wgpu compute or just SIMD on CPU). Gotcha: gammatone is IIR-natural but for a spectrogram use its magnitude response as FFT-domain weights (cheaper, phase-irrelevant for display). Precompute ERBS⁻¹ band centers once.

references.title: Equivalent Rectangular Bandwidth — CCRMA (J.O. Smith)
Loudness Spectrogram Examples — Spectral Audio Signal Processing
Auditory scales of frequency representation (Traunmüller, ERB, mel)

techniques.name: Bark-band spectrogram with Traunmüller warp (critical-band display)
techniques.one_line: 24-25 critical-band y-axis (Bark/Zwicker), the scale on which masking and loudness summation actually happen.
techniques.the_math: Traunmüller (1990) Hz→Bark: z = 26.81·f/(1960 + f) − 0.53. Corrections: if z<2, z += 0.15·(2−z); if z>20.1, z += 0.22·(z−20.1). Inverse: f = 1960·(z+0.53)/(26.81 − z − 0.53). Zwicker-Terhardt alt form: z = 13·arctan(0.00076·f) + 3.5·arctan((f/7500)²). Critical bandwidth: CB(f) ≈ 25 + 75·(1 + 1.4·(f/1000)²)^0.69 Hz. Each Bark integrates power within one critical band: E(z) = Σ_{f∈band z} |X(f)|².
techniques.what_it_reveals: Shows the signal at the resolution of the cochlea's ~24 independent channels — the natural grid for masking. Two tones inside one Bark band fuse perceptually (you can show that fusion); tones in separate bands stay distinct. Reveals which spectral peaks actually compete for the same auditory channel.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Compact, drum-tight 24-row display where every row is a 'real' perceptual channel; makes a track's spectral balance legible at a glance, like a perceptual EQ readout.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Exactly ~24 bands span the entire audible range regardless of content — the most aggressively self-dimensioning of all scales (a fixed 24-row canvas). Sample rate only changes the top band; everything below auto-fits. No parameter to tune.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivially feasible and cheap — coarser than ERB so an even smaller weighting matrix (K_fft × 24). Triangular or rectangular band weights summed from the FFT. This is the same machinery as the mel/ERB filterbank with different center spacing. Ideal lightweight 'perceptual meter' widget in egui (24 vertical bars).

references.title: Bark scale — Traunmüller formula and corrections
Updated analytical expressions for critical bandwidth and critical-band rate (DAGA 2015)
Bark scale — Wikipedia

techniques.name: Mel filterbank spectrogram (triangular mel filters)
techniques.one_line: The workhorse perceptual frequency warp from speech/MIR: log-spaced triangular filters above 1 kHz.
techniques.the_math: Hz→mel (HTK/O'Shaughnessy): m = 2595·log10(1 + f/700). Inverse: f = 700·(10^(m/2595) − 1). Slaney alt: linear below 1 kHz, log above. Build M filters: pick M+2 points equally spaced in mel between f_low and f_high, map back to Hz → centers c_1..c_M with edges. Triangular weight for filter m at FFT bin freq f: H_m(f) = 0 for f<c_{m−1}; (f−c_{m−1})/(c_m−c_{m−1}) rising; (c_{m+1}−f)/(c_{m+1}−c_m) falling; 0 above c_{m+1}. Mel energy: S_m = Σ_f H_m(f)·|X(f)|².
techniques.what_it_reveals: Speech/vocal structure becomes maximally legible (it's the front-end of every ASR system). Compresses the high end where the ear has poor frequency resolution, expands the speech-critical 100 Hz–4 kHz region. Reveals formant trajectories and consonant bursts cleanly.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Smooth, organically-warped spectrogram; the standard 'pretty' ML spectrogram look. Less physiologically exact than ERB but visually clean and universally recognized.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: M (filter count) sets vertical resolution and auto-fills the display; mel range is a fixed perceptual ruler (0 → ~3923 mel at 20 kHz). f_low/f_high can auto-clamp to the signal's Nyquist. Filter centers self-place by the mel inverse with zero tuning.
techniques.implementation_notes: Most-documented option; reference implementations everywhere (librosa, HTK). Same constant-matrix GEMV per frame. M≈40–128. The only gotcha is normalization (area vs peak normalize the triangles) which changes brightness balance — pick area-norm for energy honesty.

references.title: Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficient (MFCC) tutorial — Practical Cryptography
Frequency Scales: Semitones, Mels, ERBs — UIUC ECE417 Lec 6

techniques.name: Equal-loudness-weighted spectrogram (ISO 226 phon-honest brightness)
techniques.one_line: Reweight every spectral bin by the ISO 226 equal-loudness contour so a 20 Hz rumble and a 3 kHz hiss at the same dB SPL get the screen brightness the ear assigns them.
techniques.the_math: ISO 226:2023 maps SPL L_f at frequency f to loudness level L_N (phon) per pure-tone curves. Parametric form: A_f = 4.47e−3·(10^(0.025·L_N) − 1.15) + (0.4·10^((T_f+L_U)/10 − 9))^a_f, then L_f = (10/a_f)·log10(A_f) − L_U + 94 dB, where a_f, L_U (transfer), T_f (threshold) are tabulated per frequency. Practical display weight: gain(f) = L_N(f, L_f) − L_f in phon, applied as a per-bin dB offset before colour mapping. The contour is most sensitive ~2–5 kHz (ear-canal resonance dip) and rolls off steeply below 100 Hz, flattening as level rises.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Which energy actually contributes to perceived loudness vs. which is physically present but nearly inaudible. A subwoofer-heavy mix that looks blazing on a linear spectrogram dims correctly; the presence band lights up. Reveals the ear's mid-frequency bias directly on the image.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The picture suddenly agrees with your ears — bass stops dominating the frame, vocals and cymbals glow. It is the single most 'honest' fix you can apply to brightness.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Contours are level-dependent, so the weighting auto-adapts to each bin's instantaneous SPL — louder passages flatten (less low-end suppression), quiet passages emphasize mids more, exactly like real hearing. This is automatic content-adaptive brightness with no user knob. Estimate per-frame SPL from a calibration or relative reference to pick the right contour family.
techniques.implementation_notes: Feasible. Precompute the 89-phon family of contours as a 2D lookup (frequency × reference-level) → per-bin gain via bilinear interpolation. The catch: absolute SPL is unknown without calibration, so use a relative anchor (e.g. assume program peak ≈ 80–90 phon, or pin to LUFS). A-weighting (IEC 61672) is a cheap fixed approximation of the 40-phon contour if level-adaptivity is overkill: A(f) in dB via the standard rational magnitude formula.

references.title: Revision of ISO 226 Normal Equal-Loudness-Level Contours 2003→2023 (Acoust. Sci. Tech.)
ISO 226:2023 sample (formula 2, parameters a_f, L_U, T_f)

techniques.name: Stevens-power-law (sone) brightness mapping
techniques.one_line: Map magnitude to screen brightness through the cube-root-ish loudness compression, not raw dB or linear amplitude — equal perceptual loudness steps become equal brightness steps.
techniques.the_math: Stevens' power law: L = k·I^0.3 (sones), I = intensity ∝ |X|². Anchor: 1 sone ≡ 40 phon (1 kHz, 40 dB SPL); +10 phon ≈ ×2 sones. Phon→sone: N = 2^((L_N − 40)/10) for L_N ≥ 40; below 40 phon a steeper law N = (L_N/40)^2.642. Specific loudness (Zwicker/Moore) from excitation E vs threshold E_thr: N'(z) = c·[(E/E_thr)^α − 1]^... with α≈0.2–0.3, integrated to total loudness N = ∫ N'(z) dz over the Bark/ERB axis. Display: brightness ∝ N (sones) per band, optionally gamma-corrected for the monitor (V_out = V_in^(1/2.2)).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Makes the *area*/intensity of color on screen proportional to perceived loudness, so the visual 'energy' of the image tracks how loud it actually sounds. Reveals dynamic range as the ear feels it — quiet detail lifts, screaming peaks compress, matching the compressive loudness function.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Images stop being washed-out (linear) or muddy-flat (raw dB); the perceptual cube-root gives a luminous, well-balanced tonality where soft and loud both read. Composes with relative-luminance-correct colormaps (Y = 0.2126R+0.7152G+0.0722B) for double perceptual honesty.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The compression exponent is fixed by perception (~0.3), but the *anchor* (what maps to full brightness) can auto-calibrate to the clip's integrated loudness (LUFS) or running max, so the brightness scale self-normalizes per file without clipping or crushing. Loudness in sones is an absolute perceptual quantity → the brightness ruler is content-independent once anchored.
techniques.implementation_notes: Extremely cheap: a per-pixel pow(x, 0.3) (or a 256-entry LUT) replacing the usual log/linear mapping. Combine with the ERB/Bark band energies. Gotcha: pure intensity^0.3 ignores frequency-dependent threshold; for full honesty feed it post-equal-loudness-weighted, per-band excitation (then it becomes the Zwicker specific-loudness display). Realtime-trivial on CPU or as a wgpu fragment-shader transfer function.

references.title: Stevens's power law — Wikipedia (loudness exponent 0.3, sone)
Loudness Spectrogram Examples — sones, phons, compressive nonlinearity (J.O. Smith)
Relative luminance — Wikipedia (Y coefficients for perceptual brightness)

techniques.name: Simultaneous-masking suppression overlay (psychoacoustic model 1 spreading)
techniques.one_line: Compute the masking threshold per critical band and visibly dim/hide everything below it — the screen shows only what survives masking, i.e. what you can actually hear.
techniques.the_math: On the Bark axis, each masker at band z_m with level L_m spreads via the two-slope spreading function SF(Δz): rising side ≈ +27 dB/Bark (toward lower frequencies); falling side ≈ −(24 + 0.23/f_kHz − 0.2·L_m) dB/Bark (toward higher), commonly simplified to the MPEG values: −17 dB/Bark for 0<Δz<1, then level-dependent −(−6 + slope) onward; e.g. −5 dB/Bark at 80 dB, −8 dB/Bark at 60 dB. Masking contribution: LT(z) = L_m + SF(z − z_m) − offset, with tonal offset Δ_t = 14.5 + z dB and noise offset Δ_n ≈ 5.5 dB. Global threshold = power-sum over maskers and the absolute threshold in quiet T_q(f) = 3.64·(f/1000)^−0.8 − 6.5·e^(−0.6·(f/1000−3.3)²) + 1e−3·(f/1000)^4 dB SPL. Display rule: brightness(z) = max(0, E(z) − Threshold(z)).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Why a busy mix can hide a track entirely; which partials are perceptually 'free' and which are wasted (masked) energy. Mixing-engineer X-ray: shows exactly where a vocal is being buried by a guitar in the same critical band. This is the literal MP3 model.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The image dramatically simplifies to just the audible skeleton — sparse, glowing islands of survivor energy on a dark field, which is both beautiful and uncannily true to perception.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Thresholds are computed from the signal itself each frame, so the suppression auto-adapts to instantaneous content — loud sections raise the floor, sparse sections reveal fine detail. The absolute-threshold curve T_q self-floors the bottom of the dynamic range to true audibility. Fully content-driven, no knobs.
techniques.implementation_notes: Moderately feasible. Per frame: Bark band energies → spread (cheap triangular/two-slope convolution over 24 bands) → power-sum maskers + T_q → subtract. ~24-tap operations per frame, negligible cost. Gotcha: tonal vs noise discrimination (spectral flatness measure) needed for correct offsets; a simplified single-offset model still looks great. This is the MPEG-1 psychoacoustic model 1, well-documented.

references.title: Psychoacoustic Models for Perceptual Audio Coding — A Tutorial Review (MDPI Appl. Sci. 2019)
Psychoacoustic Masking — spreading function, slopes, thresholds

techniques.name: MFCC timbre embedding → low-D color/scatter mapping
techniques.one_line: Decorrelate the log-mel spectrum with a DCT to get ~13 orthogonal 'timbre axes', then drive color/position/scatter from them.
techniques.the_math: Pipeline: power spectrum |X(f)|² → mel filterbank energies S_m (M≈40) → log: L_m = ln(S_m) → DCT-II: c_n = Σ_{m=1}^{M} L_m·cos(π·n·(m−0.5)/M), keep n=0..12. c_0 = overall log-energy (loudness), c_1 = spectral tilt (dark↔bright), higher c_n = finer spectral ripple/formant detail. Optionally liftering w_n = 1 + (D/2)·sin(π·n/D). Deltas: Δc_n(t) = [Σ_{k} k·(c_n(t+k) − c_n(t−k))]/(2Σk²) capture timbral motion.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Timbre as geometry: the DCT makes the axes perceptually near-independent, so c_1 cleanly separates bright from dark sounds, c_2 separates peaky from flat, etc. A 2D/3D scatter of (c_1, c_2, c_3) clusters instruments/phonemes into visible blobs — timbral fingerprint you cannot hear as separable dimensions.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Turns a sound into a moving constellation in timbre-space; trails and clusters trace a song's textural journey. Excellent for an autonomous, abstract 'shape of the sound' view distinct from any spectrogram.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The DCT is itself a self-adapting dimensionality reducer: energy compacts into the first few coefficients, so you can auto-select how many axes to display by an energy threshold (keep coefficients capturing 95% of log-spectral variance). Color channels (e.g. map c_1→hue, c_2→saturation, c_0→value) auto-cover the perceptual gamut. Per-feature running min/max auto-scales the scatter bounds to the file.
techniques.implementation_notes: Very feasible. mel matrix + log + a small DCT (M→13, a constant 13×M matrix → GEMV). All CPU, microseconds per frame. Gotcha: MFCCs discard absolute pitch (good for timbre, useless for melody — pair with CQT/chroma for that). Normalize (CMVN: subtract running mean, divide running std) for stable color mapping.

references.title: MFCC tutorial — full filterbank + DCT pipeline (Practical Cryptography)
Mel-frequency Cepstral Coefficients — overview (ScienceDirect)

techniques.name: LUFS / K-weighted real-time loudness meter (BS.1770)
techniques.one_line: The broadcast-standard perceptual loudness number, rendered as momentary/short-term/integrated needles plus a loudness-range histogram.
techniques.the_math: Two-stage K-weighting at 48 kHz (biquad cascade): Stage 1 high-shelf (+~4 dB above ~1.5 kHz) b=[1.53512485958697, −2.69169618940638, 1.19839281085285], a=[1, −1.69065929318241, 0.73248077421585]; Stage 2 RLB high-pass (~38 Hz) b=[1.0, −2.0, 1.0], a=[1, −1.99004745483398, 0.99007225036621]. Per channel mean square z_i = (1/N)Σ y_i[n]². Loudness L = −0.691 + 10·log10(Σ_i G_i·z_i) LKFS, with channel gains G = 1.0 (L,R,C) and 1.41 (≈+1.5 dB, Ls,Rs).
