Gating: 400 ms blocks, 75% overlap; absolute gate Γ_a = −70 LKFS, relative gate Γ_r = (gated mean) − 10 LU. Momentary = 400 ms window, short-term = 3 s.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Perceived program loudness as the whole industry defines it — the K-weighting bakes in the ear's mid-frequency emphasis and bass de-emphasis, so the meter tracks subjective loudness far better than RMS/peak. Loudness Range (LRA, 10th–95th percentile of gated short-term) reveals dynamic-range character.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Less a 'wow' visual, more a trustworthy gauge; but a flowing momentary/short-term/integrated needle stack with a target band and an LRA histogram is a clean, professional, perceptually-grounded readout that anchors all the prettier views to a real number.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The gating auto-excludes silence/quiet from the integrated figure (the −70 LKFS absolute gate and −10 LU relative gate self-select the 'program' portion), so the metric self-scopes to the meaningful audio. The meter's range can auto-zoom around the running integrated value.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivially feasible and standard — two biquads per channel + block accumulation. Coefficients above are the canonical 48 kHz values; for other sample rates re-derive via bilinear transform to preserve the frequency response (do NOT reuse 48 kHz coefficients at 44.1 kHz). True-peak (BS.1770) needs ≥4× oversampling. This is the right 'ground-truth loudness' backbone to anchor the brightness mappings of the other techniques.

references.title: Recommendation ITU-R BS.1770-5 (11/2023) — K-weighting, gating, channel gains
LUFS — Wikipedia (pipeline: K-weight, square, gate, integrate)

techniques.name: Temporal-masking-honest time axis (forward/backward masking decay)
techniques.one_line: Smear the time dimension with the ear's actual masking time-constants so the visual persistence matches perceptual persistence — and pre-echo/transient masking becomes visible.
techniques.the_math: Backward (pre-) masking: short transient, ~20 ms reach, fast exponential. Forward (post-) masking dominates: threshold elevation decays roughly linearly in log-time, ΔL(Δt) ≈ a − b·log10(Δt) for ~5–200 ms after masker offset, with a longer-term component time constant τ ≈ 75 ms (offset attenuation effective to ~100–200 ms). Implement as an asymmetric per-band temporal filter on excitation E(z,t): rise instantly, decay E_disp = max(E, E_prev·e^(−Δt/τ_release)) with τ_release(z) ≈ 50–150 ms (band-dependent). Optionally subtract a forward-masking threshold so post-masker quiet sounds vanish on screen.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Why a loud transient hides a soft sound 50 ms later (and ~10 ms before); makes pre-echo artifacts of codecs visible; shows attack/decay envelopes the way the ear smears them rather than the FFT's sharp truth. Reveals perceptual 'shadowing' behind drum hits.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Adds organic, comet-tail persistence — transients leave perceptually-correct glows that fade on the ear's own clock, far more lifelike than a fixed decay or none.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Time constants are frequency/level/band-dependent and (in the full model) shorten with increasing masker level — so the temporal smear auto-adapts: punchy loud passages decay faster, quiet passages linger, mirroring hearing. The release constant per band self-selects from the running level.
techniques.implementation_notes: Feasible and cheap — a one-pole asymmetric (fast-attack/slow-release) envelope per display band, τ from a small per-Bark table. Sits right after the excitation/specific-loudness stage. Gotcha: backward masking is genuinely non-causal (future masks past) so true pre-masking needs a short look-ahead buffer (~20 ms); easy in offline/file playback, needs a small latency in realtime. Forward masking is causal and free.

references.title: Auditory masking — Wikipedia (forward/backward masking, time reach)
Auditory Time-Frequency Masking: Psychoacoustical Data and Application to Audio Representations

techniques.name: Zwicker sound-quality glyph (loudness / sharpness / roughness / fluctuation)
techniques.one_line: Reduce the whole sound to four perceptually-meaningful scalars and render them as a live radar/glyph — the ear's high-level descriptors.
techniques.the_math: Loudness N = ∫_0^24 N'(z) dz sones (Zwicker specific loudness over Bark). Sharpness S = k·∫_0^24 N'(z)·g(z)·z dz / ∫_0^24 N'(z) dz acum, weighting g(z)≈1 below 16 Bark, rising ~e^(0.171z) above (≈1 acum for 60 dB narrowband noise at 1 kHz). Roughness R = c·Σ_z (∂N'/∂t modulation depth)·f_mod, peaking at modulation rate ~70 Hz (1 asper ≡ 60 dB 1 kHz tone 100% AM at 70 Hz). Fluctuation strength F peaks at ~4 Hz modulation (1 vacil ≡ same tone AM at 4 Hz). Psychoacoustic annoyance PA = N·(1 + sqrt(γ1·(S−1.75)²·(S>1.75) + γ2·(2.18/N^0.4)·(0.4F + 0.6R))).
techniques.what_it_reveals: High-level perceptual character — brightness/sharpness, harshness/roughness, throb/fluctuation — that no spectrogram exposes directly. Turns 'this synth sounds harsh' into a measured roughness value; tracks tonal-quality drift over a song.
techniques.why_breathtaking: An animated four-axis radar or morphing glyph that pulses with the sound's perceptual personality — a compact, abstract 'soul' of the timbre that's striking precisely because it's so reduced.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Each metric is in absolute perceptual units (sones, acum, asper, vacil) tied to fixed reference sounds, so the glyph axes are pre-calibrated rulers — no per-file scaling. The modulation analysis auto-tunes to the dominant fluctuation/roughness rates present.
techniques.implementation_notes: Moderately feasible — loudness/sharpness are cheap once you have the Bark specific-loudness (reuse the masking/excitation stage). Roughness & fluctuation need per-band temporal modulation spectra (envelope FFT per Bark band, bandpass around 70 Hz and 4 Hz) — a bit more compute but still realtime for 24 bands. ISO 532-1 (Zwicker) gives the loudness reference; full sharpness per DIN 45692. Gotcha: exact ISO compliance is fiddly; an approximate but stable implementation is fine for a visual.

references.title: Automotive Sound Quality for EVs: Psychoacoustic Metrics (arXiv 2509.16901) — sharpness/roughness/fluctuation formulas
ISO 532-1:2017 Zwicker loudness method

key_references.title: Equivalent Rectangular Bandwidth — J.O. Smith, CCRMA (ERB & ERB-rate formulas)
key_references.why: Authoritative, concise statement of the Glasberg-Moore ERB(f)=0.108f+24.7 and ERB-rate ERBS(f)=21.4·log10(0.00437f+1) — the backbone of the most perceptually-honest frequency axis.

key_references.title: Auditory scales of frequency representation — Hartmut Traunmüller (Stockholm)
key_references.why: Source of the canonical Traunmüller Bark formula z=26.81f/(1960+f)−0.53 with its two corrections, plus mel and ERB cross-references; one page that unifies all three frequency warps.

key_references.title: Psychoacoustic Models for Perceptual Audio Coding — A Tutorial Review (MDPI Appl. Sci. 9(14):2854, 2019)
key_references.why: Complete, modern derivation of simultaneous masking: spreading-function slopes, tonal/noise offsets, absolute threshold in quiet, and global threshold power-summation — exactly the math for masking-honest visuals.

key_references.title: Recommendation ITU-R BS.1770-5 (11/2023)
key_references.why: The normative LUFS specification: K-weighting biquads, channel gains, −0.691 offset, and the two-stage gating — the ground-truth perceptual loudness number to anchor every brightness mapping.

key_references.title: Loudness Spectrogram Examples — J.O. Smith, Spectral Audio Signal Processing
key_references.why: Lays out the full FFT→ERB-resample→excitation→specific-loudness(sones) pipeline that fuses frequency warp, equal-loudness weighting, and Stevens compression into one honest auditory spectrogram.

key_references.title: Revision of ISO 226 Equal-Loudness-Level Contours 2003→2023 (Acoust. Sci. Tech. 45(1))
key_references.why: Gives the parametric ISO 226 phon equation (A_f, a_f, L_U, T_f) and the latest tabulated constants needed for level-dependent equal-loudness brightness weighting.

key_references.title: MFCC tutorial — Practical Cryptography
key_references.why: Step-by-step mel triangular filterbank construction and the log+DCT decorrelation, the recipe for both mel spectrograms and the MFCC timbre-embedding scatter.

cross_links: Spectrogram / STFT facet: every perceptual technique here is a post-warp of the same STFT — the perceptual filterbank is just a constant K_fft×N weighting matrix applied after rustfft, so it composes with any windowing/multiresolution work in that facet.

Constant-Q / chroma / pitch facet: CQT's geometric (octave-log) spacing is the music-theoretic cousin of these auditory warps; MFCCs deliberately discard pitch, so pair MFCC timbre views with CQT/chroma melody views for a complete picture.

Color / colormap facet: the Stevens-power-law brightness mapping and equal-loudness weighting only pay off if the colormap is itself perceptually uniform (CIELAB / relative-luminance Y=0.2126R+0.7152G+0.0722B) — perceptual honesty must extend through the color stage.

Autonomous-dimensioning meta-theme: perceptual scales (ERB ~0–42 Cam, Bark ~0–24, sones, LUFS, acum/asper) are FIXED perceptual rulers, so axes self-calibrate to perception not to the file — the cleanest source of content-independent auto-scaling in the whole project.

Masking + temporal-decay techniques feed the GPU/particle facets: the surviving-energy field after masking is naturally sparse, an ideal driver for particle/glow systems that should ignite only on audible energy.

Loudness/LUFS facet ↔ envelope/dynamics facet: K-weighted gated loudness and Zwicker sone-loudness are the perceptual upgrades of RMS/peak envelopes used elsewhere; share the biquad and block-accumulation machinery.

facet: Music-theoretic structure: chroma, Tonnetz/tonal-space geometry, self-similarity & form, tempo/beat, and key/chord detection visualization
summary: This facet is where a spectrum becomes music. Rather than showing energy-vs-frequency, these techniques fold the audio onto the 12-fold pitch-class circle (chroma), onto curved tonal manifolds (the toroidal Tonnetz, Harte's 6-D hypertorus, Chew's nested helices), or onto the time x time plane (self-similarity matrices) where a song's verse-chorus-bridge architecture appears as glowing blocks and diagonal stripes you literally cannot hear as a single percept. The unifying mathematical trick is octave-folding / equivalence-class projection: collapse a dimension the ear treats as "the same" (octave, tempo-octave, key transposition) so that harmony, form, and tempo become low-dimensional, stable, and beautiful. The most breathtaking-and-insightful members are the self-similarity matrix with Foote's checkerboard-novelty (reveals song form at a glance), the animated Tonnetz/tonal-centroid trajectory (reveals chord motion as smooth geometric flow on a torus), and the chroma-driven circle-of-fifths bloom (reveals key and harmonic color in real time). All are realtime-feasible in Rust+egui: they are O(12) to O(N^2) per frame on top of an FFT you already compute, and the SSM/Tonnetz are GPU-trivial as textures.

techniques.name: Chromagram (pitch-class spectrogram / log-frequency fold)
techniques.one_line: Fold the spectrum's octave dimension away: a rolling 12-bin pitch-class energy profile that paints harmony as 12 horizontal color bands robust to timbre.
techniques.the_math: Map STFT frequency f to MIDI pitch via p = round(69 + 12*log2(f/440)); the center frequency of pitch p is F_pitch(p) = 440 * 2^((p-69)/12). Build a log-frequency spectrogram Y_LF(n,p) = sum_{k: round(69+12 log2(F_coef(k)/440)) = p} |X(n,k)|^2 with F_coef(k) = k*Fs/N. Fold to 12 chroma bins: C(n,b) = sum_{p: p mod 12 = b} Y_LF(n,p), b in {0..11}. Normalize each frame to unit L2: c(n,b) = C(n,b)/sqrt(sum_b C(n,b)^2 + eps). A CQT can replace the STFT pooling: X_CQT(k) uses geometrically spaced center freqs f_k = f_min*2^(k/B) (B bins/octave) with per-bin window length proportional to 1/f_k (constant Q = f_k/Delta f_k).
techniques.what_it_reveals: The harmonic/melodic content stripped of instrumentation and dynamics. Two recordings of the same chord on piano vs guitar yield nearly identical chroma, so you SEE harmony where the raw spectrogram only shows timbre. Octave errors, chord changes, and tonal shifts become visible as band activations that the ear blends into a single 'color'.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It is the bridge from physics to music: a 12-lane river of color where chords light up as chords. Stacked as a chromagram it has the legibility of sheet music with the smoothness of a heat map, and it is the substrate every other technique in this facet builds on.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The 12-bin output is content-independent by construction, so the y-axis never needs rescaling. Tuning deviation is auto-estimated (cross-correlate the chroma against an idealized comb and shift the binning by the peak, handling A=435..445 Hz instruments). Per-frame L2 normalization auto-scales color intensity to loudness-invariant range. Octave span of the analysis adapts to detected pitch range (only pool pitches with non-negligible energy).
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivial on top of rustfft: one STFT per frame, a precomputed K->pitch pooling table (sparse matrix), a 12-way modulo sum, an L2 normalize. ~O(N) per frame, sub-millisecond. CQT is heavier (precompute kernel as sparse complex matrix, ~O(N log N)); for realtime, an STFT+pooling approximation is fine. Render as an egui texture (scrolling ring buffer). Gotcha: pool energy not magnitude, and add a small spectral whitening / log compression (log(1+gamma*|X|)) before pooling or bass dominates.

references.title: Chroma feature (Wikipedia)
FMP Notebook C3S1: Log-Frequency Spectrogram & Chromagram (Müller)

techniques.name: Harmonic Pitch-Class Profile (HPCP) with harmonic summation
techniques.one_line: A tuning-robust, peak-based chromagram that adds each spectral peak's energy into not just its own pitch class but its overtone-implied root, sharpening chords.
techniques.the_math: Detect local spectral peaks (f_i, a_i). For peak frequency f_i, its continuous pitch-class position is l_i = 12*log2(f_i/f_ref) mod 12 (f_ref the estimated reference tuning). Contribute to bin n (of M bins, M in {12,24,36}) with a cosine-squared weight: w(n,f_i) = cos^2( (pi/2)*(d/(0.5*M/12)) ) for |d| <= 0.5*M/12 else 0, where d = (l_i*M/12 - n) wrapped to nearest. Harmonic summation: for each peak also add its sub-harmonic contributions sum_{h=1..H} (s^(h-1)) * a_i^2 * w(...) where harmonic h of a note at f_i/h implies a fundamental; s~0.6 is a harmonic decay, H~8. Final HPCP(n) = sum over peaks/harmonics, then normalize to max=1.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The implied root/harmony even when the fundamental is weak or missing (overtone-rich or band-limited audio). It cleans the chromagram's 'smear' from harmonics so triads read as compact 3-bin clusters instead of fuzzy clouds, exposing chord quality (major/minor/dim) more sharply than plain chroma.
techniques.why_breathtaking: With 36 bins it becomes a high-resolution tonal fingerprint: you can watch a vibrato wobble a bin, a guitar bend slide across bins, and tuning drift shift the whole field — micro-tonality made visible.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Reference tuning f_ref is auto-estimated from the peak histogram (the algorithm self-calibrates to the instrument's actual A). Bin resolution M can adapt: 12 for chord-only views, 36 to surface microtonal/intonation detail when peaks are sharp. Peak count adapts to spectral sparsity. The cosine window width scales with M automatically.
techniques.implementation_notes: Needs a peak picker (parabolic interpolation on FFT magnitude for sub-bin frequency) + harmonic-summation loop over ~8 harmonics x detected peaks. Still cheap (peaks are tens, not thousands). All on top of the same FFT. Render identically to chroma. Gotcha: weighting window and harmonic decay s are the tuning knobs; get the reference-frequency estimation right or everything is rotated by a fraction of a semitone.

references.title: Harmonic pitch class profiles (Wikipedia)
Gómez, Tonal Description of Music Audio Signals (HPCP, PhD thesis)

techniques.name: Circle-of-fifths / pitch-class clock bloom (chroma on the fifths ring)
techniques.one_line: Plot the live chroma vector as petals on the circle of fifths, where consonant keys sit adjacent — a glowing harmonic compass that points at the current key.
techniques.the_math: Place pitch class b at angle theta(b) = 2*pi * (7*b mod 12)/12 (multiply by 7 = perfect-fifth reordering so neighbors are a fifth apart). Petal length/brightness = c(n,b) (current chroma). The harmonic 'center of mass' is the complex sum z(n) = sum_b c(n,b) * exp(i*theta(b)); |z| measures tonal focus (peaks high for a clear key, near 0 for atonal/percussive), arg(z) points toward the tonal center. Animate the resultant vector. For key estimate overlay, rotate so arg(z) aligns with the best Krumhansl key.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Tonal centricity and key as geometry: a strongly tonal passage collapses the bloom toward one side of the wheel; a modulation rotates it; a diminished/whole-tone passage spreads it symmetrically (|z| ~ 0). You see 'how tonal' and 'which key' in one glance — invisible to the ear as a single number.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The fifths reordering makes consonance spatial: neighboring petals are always harmonically close, so chords form contiguous arcs of light and dissonance scatters. A modulation is a literal rotation of a glowing flower. Color-by-pitch-class (hue = 2*pi*b/12) makes it iconic.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The resultant magnitude |z| auto-scales the visual 'tightness' indicator without any manual threshold. Hue mapping is fixed to pitch class so it self-labels. The wheel can auto-rotate to keep the detected tonic at 12-o'clock (key-relative view) — the visualization re-centers on the content's key. Petal brightness normalized per frame to current dynamics.
techniques.implementation_notes: Pure 2-D egui painter on top of chroma: 12 line/wedge primitives + one resultant arrow. Negligible cost. The reordering and the complex sum are 12-term loops. Looks best with additive blending and a short temporal smoothing (EMA on chroma, alpha ~0.2) so petals breathe rather than flicker. Optional inner ring for relative minors.

references.title: Circle of fifths (Wikipedia)
Color-based circle-of-fifths representation (ResearchGate figure)

techniques.name: Tonnetz lattice with live triad triangles (neo-Riemannian)
techniques.one_line: Pitch classes on a triangular lattice where fifths/major-thirds/minor-thirds are the three axes; the current chord lights up as a triangle that flips along shared edges as harmony moves.
techniques.the_math: Lay pitch class on an integer lattice: position(pc) = a*(7) + b*(4) mod 12 — i.e. axis u = +7 semitones (perfect fifth), axis v = +4 (major third); the third edge is +3 (minor third) = u - v direction. In 2-D screen coords place node (a,b) at x = a + 0.5*b, y = (sqrt(3)/2)*b (hex packing). A major triad {root, root+4, root+7} forms an UP triangle; a minor triad {root, root+3, root+7} a DOWN triangle; they share edges. Neo-Riemannian moves are reflections: P (parallel) flips the third, L (leading-tone exchange) and R (relative) flip across the other two edges — each shares 2 of 3 vertices (minimal voice-leading). Under enharmonic+octave equivalence (mod 12 both axes) the plane wraps to a torus; embed on a torus surface as (theta1,theta2) = (2*pi*a/12, 2*pi*b/12) for a 3-D ribbon.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Voice-leading parsimony made visible: a I-vi-IV-V or a Wagnerian P/L/R chain becomes a walk across adjacent triangles, exposing that 'distant' chords are often one edge-flip apart. You see the harmonic geometry composers exploit — common-tone retention — which is utterly hidden in a waveform.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It is a living crystal lattice of harmony. Watching a triad triangle pivot edge-to-edge through a progression, optionally on a slowly rotating torus, is mesmerizing and mathematically exact. The toroidal wrap is genuinely beautiful 3-D.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The active triad is found by argmax over the 24 chord templates against current chroma, so the highlighted triangle adapts to content automatically. Lattice extent can auto-tile to keep the active region centered (infinite-plane scroll) or wrap to the torus when enharmonic equivalence is assumed. Node brightness can be driven directly by chroma c(n,b), turning the whole lattice into a soft harmonic heat field that self-scales to dynamics.
techniques.implementation_notes: 2-D version is pure egui line/triangle drawing on a fixed hex grid + chord pick (24 dot-products). Torus version wants wgpu: a parametric torus mesh with the 12x12 lattice as a texture/vertex colors, trivial. Per-frame cost dominated by the FFT, not this. Gotcha: chord detection flicker — smooth chroma and add hysteresis or an HMM/Viterbi over chord labels so the triangle doesn't jitter.

references.title: Tonnetz (Wikipedia)
Neo-Riemannian theory (Wikipedia)
FMP C5S2: Template-based chord recognition (Müller)

techniques.name: Harte 6-D tonal centroid trajectory on the hypertorus (HCDF)
techniques.one_line: Project the 12-D chroma onto three circles (fifths, minor-thirds, major-thirds) giving a point on a 6-D hypertorus; its speed is a chord-change detector.
techniques.the_math: Define a 6x12 projection where pitch class l (0..11) maps to phi(l) = [ r1*sin(l*7*pi/6), r1*cos(l*7*pi/6), r2*sin(l*3*pi/2), r2*cos(l*3*pi/2), r3*sin(l*2*pi/3), r3*cos(l*2*pi/3) ]^T. The three angular rates 7pi/6, 3pi/2, 2pi/3 traverse the circle of fifths, the minor-third circle, and the major-third circle respectively as l increments. Radii (librosa/Harte): r1=1 (fifths), r2=1 (minor thirds), r3=0.5 (major thirds). Tonal centroid: zeta(n) = (1/||c(n)||_1) * Phi * c(n), Phi = [phi(0)...phi(11)] (6x12). Smooth zeta over time (e.g. Gaussian, ~1 s). Harmonic Change Detection Function: HCDF(n) = || zeta(n+1) - zeta(n-1) ||_2 ; peaks = chord boundaries.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Harmonic change rate as a 1-D curve, and harmonic 'location' as a smooth 6-D trajectory. Chord boundaries that you might not consciously parse appear as sharp HCDF peaks; a sustained chord is a still point, a turnaround is a tight loop. It separates harmonic rhythm from surface rhythm.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Reduced to 2 or 3 dimensions (the fifths circle pair, plus PCA of the other four), the trajectory is a luminous Lissajous-like ribbon that loops and knots with the harmony — a phase portrait of tonality. Projected onto its native three circles it's three coupled rotating dials.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The L1 normalization makes the centroid scale-invariant to loudness automatically. The 6-D point can be auto-projected to 2/3-D via online PCA on a sliding window so the displayed axes follow whichever tonal dimensions the current music actually uses. HCDF peak-picking threshold can be set adaptively (median + k*MAD over a window), so boundary sensitivity self-tunes to the piece's harmonic density.
techniques.implementation_notes: Phi is a constant 6x12 matrix: one matrix-vector product per frame (72 mults). HCDF is a difference + norm. All trivial on top of chroma. The interesting cost is the 6-D->2/3-D embedding for display (incremental PCA, cheap). Render the trail with fading alpha in egui (2-D) or a wgpu line strip (3-D). Gotcha: smoothing length sets the harmonic time-scale; too short = noisy HCDF, too long = misses fast changes.

references.title: Harte, Sandler, Gasser — Detecting Harmonic Change in Musical Audio (2006)
librosa.feature.tonnetz documentation

techniques.name: Chew Spiral Array — center-of-effect key/chord locator
techniques.one_line: Stack pitches on a 3-D helix (a fifth = a quarter-turn rise); chords and keys live as weighted centroids inside the spiral, and the current key is the nearest key-point to the music's center of effect.
techniques.the_math: Pitch class k steps along the line of fifths: P(k) = [ r*sin(k*pi/2), r*cos(k*pi/2), k*h ] (a perfect fifth = +1 in k = 90-degree turn + vertical rise h; four fifths up a major third stacks one full turn). Major triad center: C_M(k) = w1*P(k) + w2*P(k+1) + w3*P(k+4), w1>=w2>=w3>0, sum=1 (root, fifth, third). Minor: C_m(k) = u1*P(k) + u2*P(k+1) + u3*P(k-3). Major key center: T_M(k) = o1*C_M(k) + o2*C_M(k+1) + o3*C_M(k-1) (tonic, dominant, subdominant). For an audio window, build the Center of Effect: ce = sum_i d_i * P(pc_i) (d_i = duration/energy weight, from chroma). Estimated key = argmin over the 24 key points T of ||ce - T||.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Key and its stability over time as a continuous 3-D path, plus a principled distance ('tonal tension') between the music's current centroid and the home key. Modulations are visible as the center-of-effect drifting toward and settling at a new key point; tonal ambiguity is the centroid hovering between two.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A nested spiral of helices (pitches outside, chords within, keys at the core) with a glowing marker spiraling through it is architecturally gorgeous — it literally renders music theory as a 3-D sculpture, and the marker's wandering is the piece's tonal narrative.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Center-of-effect uses a decaying memory window whose length can adapt to harmonic rhythm (longer for slow harmony). Key distance gives a built-in confidence/tension scalar that self-scales. The spiral radius/height (r,h) are free aspect parameters that can be tuned so the major-third vertical alignment reads cleanly; the camera can auto-orbit to keep the ce marker facing the viewer.
techniques.implementation_notes: Pitch/chord/key points are precomputed constants. Per frame: weight the 12 P(k) by chroma, sum (12 vec3 adds), then 24 distance comparisons — negligible. Native 3-D, so wgpu line strips + point sprites; or a 2-D top-down projection in egui. Gotcha: choosing weights (w,u,o) and h affects which keys win at boundaries; Chew's published values are a good default. It assumes a line-of-fifths (spelled) input — from chroma you use the 12 pitch classes mod 12, an acceptable approximation.

references.title: Spiral array model (Wikipedia)
Chew, Mathematical and Computational Modeling of Tonality (Springer)

techniques.name: Krumhansl–Schmuckler key-detection overlay (template correlation)
techniques.one_line: Pearson-correlate the accumulated chroma against 24 empirically-measured key profiles; the best of 24 is the key, and the full correlation vector is a glowing key-likelihood ring.
techniques.the_math: Major profile (Krumhansl–Kessler, rooted at C): K_maj = [6.35,2.23,3.48,2.33,4.38,4.09,2.52,5.19,2.39,3.66,2.29,2.88]; minor: K_min = [6.33,2.68,3.52,5.38,2.60,3.53,2.54,4.75,3.98,2.69,3.34,3.17]. For key with tonic t, rotate the profile by t. Accumulate a windowed chroma histogram x(b). Score each of 24 keys by Pearson r: r(K_t, x) = sum_b (K_t(b)-meanK)(x(b)-meanx) / sqrt( sum(K_t-meanK)^2 * sum(x-meanx)^2 ). Estimated key = argmax_t r. Visualize all 24 r-values as brightness around a double circle-of-fifths (outer=major, inner=relative minor).
techniques.what_it_reveals: The piece's key, and crucially the RUNNER-UP keys: the relative minor, the dominant, the subdominant typically glow second-brightest, exposing the tonal neighborhood. Watching the bright spot migrate over time visualizes modulation directly; competing peaks reveal ambiguous or modal passages.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It turns a perceptual judgment ('this is in A minor') into a luminous landscape over all 24 keys, where the second- and third-place glows tell a richer story than a single label — the harmonic 'gravity well' of the music.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The accumulation window can be local (instantaneous key, scrolls with modulations) or global (whole-piece key), a user/auto-selected time-scale. Because Pearson correlation is mean- and scale-invariant, no normalization tuning is needed — it self-adapts to chroma magnitude. The display ranks all 24 so the visualization needs no manual key labeling.
techniques.implementation_notes: 24 Pearson correlations of length-12 vectors per update window — utterly trivial. The profiles are constants. Best layered as an overlay on the circle-of-fifths bloom or as a small 24-bar meter. Gotcha: Pearson can be replaced by cosine or by the Temperley–Kostka–Payne refined profiles for better pop/classical accuracy; for live audio, smooth the chroma accumulation or the winner flickers at boundaries.

references.title: Key-finding algorithm — Robert Hart (profiles & worked example)
Krumhansl, Cognitive Foundations of Musical Pitch (probe-tone profiles)

techniques.name: Self-Similarity Matrix (SSM) with Foote checkerboard novelty
techniques.one_line: Compare every frame to every other frame; the whole song's verse/chorus architecture appears as a glowing time x time map, and a checkerboard kernel slid down the diagonal pops out section boundaries.
techniques.the_math: From normalized features x_n (chroma, MFCC, or tonal centroid), SSM S = X^T X, i.e. S(n,m) = <x_n,x_m> = cos similarity in [-1,1] (==1 on diagonal). Homogeneous sections => bright square BLOCKS; repetitions (verse 1 ~ verse 2) => off-diagonal diagonal STRIPES (paths). Foote novelty: slide a 2L x 2L Gaussian-tapered checkerboard kernel C(m,n) = sign(m)*sign(n)*exp(-(m^2+n^2)/(2*sigma^2)) (so +/+ on the two diagonal quadrants, -/- on the anti-diagonal quadrants; Foote used 64x64, sigma=delta=32) along the main diagonal: N(i) = sum_{m=-L}^{L} sum_{n=-L}^{L} C(m,n) * S(i+m, i+n). Peaks in N(i) = section boundaries. Path/repetition enhancement: convolve S with a short averaging filter ALONG the diagonal direction (and over a few tempo-shear angles to handle tempo drift) to denoise stripes.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The macro-FORM of a piece in a single image — something no spectrogram and no amount of listening-in-one-pass gives you. AABA, verse-chorus, sonata recapitulations, loop points, and even the exact bar where the drop hits all become geometric. The novelty curve gives objective segment boundaries.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A finished SSM is a self-portrait of the song: symmetric, fractal-feeling, with shimmering diagonals marking every repeat. It is the single most 'insightful' image in MIR and is also strikingly beautiful as a heat map. Transposed repeats can be made visible with transposition-invariant SSMs (take min distance over all 12 cyclic chroma shifts).
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The matrix is inherently N x N — it auto-sizes to the song length; for realtime it grows as a scrolling lower-triangle. Kernel width L sets the boundary time-scale and can be swept to build a hierarchical (multi-scale) segmentation automatically. Novelty peak threshold adapts via local statistics. Path enhancement can auto-search a small set of tempo-shear angles (e.g. +/-10%) and keep the strongest, adapting to tempo differences between repeats. Transposition invariance auto-handles key-shifted repeats.
techniques.implementation_notes: S = X^T X is one matrix multiply; for N up to a few thousand frames it is instant, and for streaming you append a column per frame (O(N) per frame). This is the prime wgpu candidate: store S as an R32F texture, compute novelty in a compute/fragment pass, render with a perceptual colormap (magma/viridis). egui can blit the texture directly. Gotcha: feature normalization is essential (diagonal must be 1); use cosine. Memory O(N^2) — for very long files downsample frames (1-2 Hz feature rate is plenty for form).

references.title: Foote — Automatic Audio Segmentation Using a Measure of Audio Novelty (2000)
FMP C4S4: Novelty-based Segmentation (checkerboard kernel, Müller)
FMP C4S2: SSM — Path Enhancement & Transposition Invariance

techniques.name: Arc diagram of repeated sections
techniques.one_line: Lay the timeline as a horizontal axis and connect every repeated passage with a translucent semicircular arc — a Schenker-like map of the song's repetition skeleton.
techniques.the_math: From the SSM's enhanced path structure (or from substring matching on a quantized chroma/chord string), extract maximal repeating segment pairs (start_a..end_a) ~ (start_b..end_b). For each pair draw a semicircle on the time axis spanning [center_a, center_b]: radius = (t_b - t_a)/2, center at (t_a+t_b)/2, height = radius. Encode arc thickness ∝ segment length (match duration) and opacity ∝ similarity score. Nesting of arcs reveals hierarchical structure (a long arc spanning two short nested arcs = a repeated section that itself contains a repeat).
techniques.what_it_reveals: The repetition grammar of the piece as a clean line drawing rather than a dense matrix — easier to read than an SSM for 'what repeats where, and how the repeats nest'. Long-range form (chorus returns 3 minutes apart) jumps out as big arcs.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Elegant, minimal, almost calligraphic — overlapping translucent arcs of varying radius produce a rainbow-bridge aesthetic that reads instantly as structure. It is the legible, presentation-ready cousin of the SSM.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Arc set is derived from detected repeats, so it auto-populates to the song's actual structure with no fixed track count. A minimum-match-length threshold can be set as a fraction of song length (self-scaling). Arc height/opacity normalize to the longest/strongest match found. The horizontal axis auto-fits the duration.
techniques.implementation_notes: Lightweight egui painter (bezier/arc primitives) once repeats are known. The cost is upstream: extracting repeat pairs from the SSM (path/stripe detection, e.g. greedy diagonal matching) which is offline-ish but fine for a loaded file; not ideal for hard-realtime streaming. Gotcha: arc-pair extraction quality depends entirely on path enhancement; noisy SSM => spurious arcs. Best as a 'whole song loaded' view rather than live.

references.title: Wattenberg — Arc Diagrams: Visualizing Structure in Strings (IEEE InfoVis 2002)
Interactive Arc Diagrams (CCRMA)

techniques.name: Fourier / autocorrelation / cyclic Tempogram
techniques.one_line: A spectrogram of rhythm: analyze the onset-novelty curve for local periodicity to paint tempo (BPM) over time, and fold tempo-octaves to a stable cyclic tempo color.
techniques.the_math: Build an onset novelty curve Delta(m) (e.g. spectral flux: Delta(m) = sum_k max(0, |X(m,k)| - |X(m-1,k)|), log-compressed, mean-subtracted, half-wave rectified; feature rate Fs ~ 100 Hz). Fourier tempogram: F(n,omega) = sum_m Delta(m) w(m-n) exp(-2 pi i omega m); T_F(n,tau) = |F(n, tau/60)| for tempo tau (BPM), since tempo tau BPM = frequency tau/60 Hz. Autocorrelation tempogram: A(n,lag) = sum_m Delta(m) Delta(m+lag) w(...); convert lag (sec) to tau = 60/lag — this emphasizes the true beat and its sub-harmonics while suppressing harmonics (complementary to the Fourier one, which does the opposite). Cyclic tempogram (tempo-chroma): identify tempi an octave apart, C(n,[tau]) = sum_{k in Z} T(n, 2^k * tau_0 * s), a periodic, pulse-level-invariant tempo profile (s in [1,2) the scaling param, M bins/octave). PLP (predominant local pulse) = sinusoidal kernel at the dominant tempo, phase from F, half-wave rectified => a refined beat curve.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Tempo and its fluctuations (ritardando, rubato, drift), metrical ambiguity (is it 70 or 140 BPM? both light up an octave apart), and beat phase. The autocorrelation-vs-Fourier pair disambiguates the true tactus from its harmonics. Cyclic tempogram gives a tempo 'color' as stable as a key.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A tempogram is a heat map of the music's pulse — you watch tempo shimmer and bend. The cyclic version is a clean rotating tempo-hue, and a PLP/beat overlay pulsing exactly on the tactus is viscerally satisfying.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Tempo axis is naturally log-spaced (BPM is perceived multiplicatively); range Theta=[30:600] BPM with M bins/octave auto-covers content. The cyclic fold removes the octave ambiguity automatically (self-normalizing tempo). Window length sets tempo-vs-time resolution and can adapt to detected tempo stability. The dominant-tempo ridge can be auto-tracked (Viterbi over the tempogram) so a beat marker follows tempo changes without manual BPM entry.
techniques.implementation_notes: Novelty curve = cheap (per-frame spectral flux from the FFT you have). Tempogram = compute Fourier coefficients only at the ~tempi of interest (a few hundred), not a full FFT — a small matrix multiply per frame; or rustfft on the windowed novelty. Autocorrelation = one more cheap pass. All realtime. Render as a scrolling egui texture, tempo on a log y-axis. Gotcha: novelty-curve quality is everything; log-compress and normalize it. Beat phase from PLP needs careful unwrapping.

references.title: Grosche, Müller, Kurth — Cyclic Tempogram (ICASSP 2010)
FMP C6S2: Fourier Tempogram (Müller)
librosa.feature.tempogram documentation

key_references.title: FMP — Fundamentals of Music Processing notebooks (Meinard Müller, AudioLabs Erlangen)
key_references.why: The single most authoritative, equation-complete, openly-runnable source for every technique in this facet: chroma (C3), SSM/novelty/path-enhancement/transposition-invariance (C4), chord recognition (C5), tempogram/beat (C6). Copy the math straight from here.

key_references.title: Foote — Automatic Audio Segmentation Using a Measure of Audio Novelty (ICME 2000)
key_references.why: Origin of the self-similarity-matrix + Gaussian-tapered checkerboard-kernel novelty curve — the highest-insight technique in the facet; gives exact kernel (64x64, sigma=32) and N(i) formula.

key_references.title: Harte, Sandler, Gasser — Detecting Harmonic Change in Musical Audio (ACM AMCMM 2006)
key_references.why: The 6-D tonal-centroid / hypertorus projection (exact phi(l) angular formula, radii r1=r2=1, r3=0.5) and the HCDF chord-change detector — the most breathtaking geometric harmony visualization that is also a real algorithm.

key_references.title: librosa documentation (chroma_cqt, tonnetz, tempogram)
key_references.why: Reference implementation parameters to validate a Rust port against (tonnetz radii, CQT chroma, Fourier/autocorrelation tempogram), with stable numeric defaults.

key_references.title: Krumhansl & Kessler probe-tone key profiles / Robert Hart worked example
key_references.why: Exact 24 key-profile weight vectors and the Pearson-correlation key-finding procedure — drop-in constants for the key-detection overlay and circle-of-fifths bloom.

key_references.title: Chew — Spiral Array model (Wikipedia + Springer book)
key_references.why: Parametric helix equations and center-of-effect formulas for the 3-D tonal-space sculpture and a principled key/chord locator with built-in tonal-tension distance.

cross_links: Chromagram is the shared input to nearly every other facet's harmonic analysis and is itself a folded version of the log-frequency spectrogram from the spectral/time-frequency facet (CQT/STFT) — share the FFT and pooling matrix.

Self-similarity matrices and tempograms reuse the spectral-flux onset/novelty curve from the rhythm/onset-detection facet; the SSM can be built from MFCC (timbre facet) instead of chroma to reveal timbral rather than harmonic form.

The Tonnetz torus, Harte hypertorus, and Chew spiral are 3-D manifold embeddings — they belong to the same GPU/wgpu rendering pipeline as any 3-D point-cloud/manifold facet (phase-space, UMAP/t-SNE of audio features).
Circle-of-fifths bloom + Krumhansl key overlay + Tonnetz triad highlight should compose into one unified 'harmony HUD' layer; they all consume the same per-frame normalized chroma vector.
Beat/tempo tracking (PLP from the tempogram) provides the phase clock that can drive beat-synchronized animation and beat-quantized SSM/feature averaging used across all rhythmic visualizations.
Color-by-pitch-class (hue = 2*pi*pc/12) is a shared palette convention that ties chroma, circle-of-fifths, and Tonnetz together visually and should be a global theme primitive.

facet: Nonlinear dynamics & chaos: Takens delay embedding, recurrence plots/RQA, audio-forced strange attractors, Poincaré sections, fractal/correlation dimension
summary: This facet treats the audio waveform not as a spectrum but as a trajectory through a reconstructed state space. Takens' delay-embedding theorem is the keystone: a single scalar audio channel x(t) is lifted into an m-dimensional cloud whose geometry is diffeomorphic to the (unknown) attractor that generated the sound — so a flute note, a vowel, a snare, and white noise each carve out visibly distinct shapes that no spectrogram shows. The strongest techniques here are (1) delay-coordinate phase portraits with auto-chosen lag tau (first minimum of mutual information) and dimension m (false-nearest-neighbors), (2) recurrence plots and Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA), which expose repetition, determinism, and laminar/intermittent structure as glowing diagonal/vertical texture, and (3) audio-forced/parameter-modulated strange attractors (Lorenz/Rössler/Chua) that turn the sound into a living sculpture. Crucially for this research's autonomous-dimensioning interest, the whole pipeline is self-scaling: tau, m, and the recurrence threshold epsilon are all estimated from the signal itself (mutual information, FNN, fixed-recurrence-rate), so the visualization re-dimensions its own axes, neighborhood radius, and even embedding dimensionality as the audio changes. All of this is realtime-feasible in Rust+egui: the embeddings are O(N) gather operations, recurrence matrices are an O(N^2) GPU-friendly outer product, and attractor integration is a handful of multiply-adds per sample — the heavy O(N^2)/O(N log N) parts (RQA, correlation dimension) map cleanly to wgpu compute or windowed CPU work.

techniques.name: Delay-coordinate phase portrait with autonomous (tau, m) estimation
techniques.one_line: Lift the mono waveform into an m-D delay embedding whose lag and dimension are chosen automatically from the signal, then render the resulting attractor cloud/curve.
techniques.the_math: State vector (Takens): v(i) = [ x(i), x(i+tau), x(i+2tau), ..., x(i+(m-1)tau) ] in R^m. Takens' theorem: if m > 2d (d = box-counting dim of the true attractor) the delay map phi(x) = (h(x), h(Tx), ..., h(T^{m-1}x)) is generically an embedding — diffeomorphic to the original attractor. AUTONOMOUS tau via average mutual information: I(tau) = sum_{i,j} p_{ij}(tau) log2( p_{ij}(tau) / (p_i p_j) ), where p_i is the probability x lands in amplitude-bin i and p_{ij}(tau) the joint probability x(t) in i AND x(t+tau) in j; choose tau* = first local minimum of I(tau) (decorrelates coordinates while keeping nonlinear dependence). AUTONOMOUS m via false nearest neighbors (Kennel): for each point find nearest neighbor at dim m; it is FALSE if going to m+1 the extra coordinate separation makes |x_{i+m*tau} - x_{j+m*tau}| / R_m(i) > R_tol (~15), or if the new distance exceeds A_tol * sigma_data; increase m until FNN fraction < ~1%. Render: for 2D scope plot (x(i), x(i+tau)); for 3D use (x(i), x(i+tau), x(i+2tau)) rotated to screen.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Geometry the ear and the spectrogram both miss: a pure tone is an ellipse (a Lissajous loop), a quasi-periodic dyad is a torus/Lissajous knot, a sustained vowel is a thick closed band whose thickness IS the harmonic richness, a chaotic/breathy/noisy sound fills space (high embedding dimension), and amplitude/timbre changes warp the loop in real time. The FNN curve itself is a readout of how many degrees of freedom the sound has — a single number that says 'this is a 2-oscillator system' vs 'this is broadband noise'.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A single channel of audio becomes a luminous 3D ribbon that breathes, twists, and thickens with the music — visually it reads as living calligraphy, and because tau auto-tunes, the loop stays beautifully 'open' (never collapsing to the diagonal or exploding) no matter the pitch.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This is the canonical self-dimensioning visual: tau is recomputed from mutual information per analysis window, m is set by FNN so the render literally adds/removes a spatial dimension as the sound's complexity changes (2D for a pure tone, 3D+ projection for chaos), and axis scaling follows the running min/max of the embedded cloud. The visualization chooses its own coordinates.
techniques.implementation_notes: Embedding is a zero-cost strided gather over the existing sample buffer. AMI: 16-32 amplitude bins, 2D histogram per candidate tau (say tau=1..512) — a few hundred K adds per window, trivial on CPU; recompute every 50-100 ms, not per frame. FNN: kd-tree (kiddo crate) or a coarse grid hash on a few thousand windowed samples — keep N small (2-4k) so it is sub-millisecond. Render with egui painter for 2D, or wgpu line-strip/point-sprites for the 3D ribbon (tens of thousands of vertices, instanced, easy 60fps). Temporal smoothing of tau/m is essential or the loop pops; EMA them. Gotcha: at very low pitch tau can exceed the window — clamp and fall back to autocorrelation lag.

references.title: Attractor reconstruction (Scholarpedia)
Fraser & Swinney mutual information (TISEAN docs)
False nearest neighbors (Kennel, TISEAN docs)
AMI & FNN for embedding parameter estimation (Frontiers/PMC)

techniques.name: Recurrence plot + GPU Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA)
techniques.one_line: Plot R_ij = 1 wherever two embedded states are within epsilon, producing a glowing texture whose diagonals/verticals are quantified into determinism, laminarity, and entropy.
techniques.the_math: Recurrence matrix: R_{i,j}(eps) = Theta( eps - || v(i) - v(j) || ), Theta = Heaviside step, v = delay-embedded states, ||.|| usually Euclidean (or max-norm). The plot is the N x N binary image (black where R=1). Quantification from the line structure: Recurrence Rate RR = (1/N^2) sum_{i,j} R_{i,j}; Determinism DET = ( sum_{l>=l_min} l P(l) ) / ( sum_{l>=1} l P(l) ), P(l)=histogram of diagonal-line lengths; mean diagonal L = sum l P(l) / sum P(l) over l>=l_min; Divergence DIV = 1/L_max (estimator of the largest Lyapunov exponent); Diagonal entropy ENTR = - sum_{l>=l_min} p(l) ln p(l) with p(l)=P(l)/sum P(l); Laminarity LAM = ( sum_{v>=v_min} v P(v) ) / ( sum_{v>=1} v P(v) ), P(v)=vertical-line lengths; Trapping Time TT = sum v P(v)/ sum P(v) over v>=v_min. AUTONOMOUS epsilon: choose eps so RR hits a fixed target (~1-5%), i.e. eps = quantile of the pairwise-distance distribution — or fixed-amount-of-nearest-neighbors (FAN) for a per-column adaptive threshold.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Time-structure invisible in a waveform or even a spectrogram: exact repeats appear as off-diagonal diagonal lines (a riff returning at bar 17 lights up the (t1,t2) cell), tempo drift bends those lines, sustained/laminar tones make vertical-and-horizontal blocks, and pure noise gives a structureless speckle. DET separates deterministic (tonal/periodic) from stochastic (noisy) audio numerically; LAM/TT flag intermittency and held notes; DIV is a direct chaos meter; the recurrence plot of a whole song is essentially a fingerprint of its form (verse/chorus block structure).
techniques.why_breathtaking: The plot is a self-symmetric, fractal-textured tapestry — diagonals shimmer as motifs recur, and for music it produces unmistakably gorgeous checkerboard/block mosaics that look like woven cloth. It is simultaneously an art object and an X-ray of song form.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Strongly self-scaling: epsilon is not a fixed constant but solved each window to hold the recurrence rate at a target density, so the plot keeps the same visual contrast whether the audio is loud, soft, dense, or sparse. The same RR-targeting auto-normalizes across instruments and dynamics; FAN makes the threshold adapt per-column to local density. l_min/v_min can scale with sample rate.
techniques.implementation_notes: The full N x N matrix is the cost center: for a windowed live view use N=256-1024 embedded states -> 64k-1M cells, an embarrassingly parallel outer product perfect for a wgpu compute shader (one thread per (i,j), write 1 bit / 1 texel) rendered straight to a texture egui samples. CPU fallback fine at N<=512. RQA line-length histograms are a cheap scan over the binary image (diagonal and vertical run-length encoding), microseconds. epsilon-for-target-RR: sample a few thousand pairwise distances, take the desired quantile — O(N) per window. Gotcha: Euclidean norm needs the embedding done first; reuse the delay-embedding buffer from technique 1. Whole-song SSM (thousands x thousands) is offline/downsampled, not per-frame.

references.title: Recurrence quantification analysis (Wikipedia)
Marwan et al., Recurrence plots for the analysis of complex systems
Recurrence threshold selection / fixed RR & FAN (pyunicorn docs)
Recurrence Plots 25 years later (arXiv 1306.0688)

techniques.name: Audio-forced / parameter-modulated strange attractor (Lorenz, Rössler, Chua) beyond a fixed Lorenz
techniques.one_line: Drive a chaotic ODE's parameters and forcing terms from live audio features so the strange attractor morphs, bifurcates, and pulses in lockstep with the sound.
techniques.the_math: Lorenz: x' = sigma(y-x); y' = x(rho-z)-y; z' = xy - beta z (classic chaos at sigma=10, rho=28, beta=8/3). Rössler (single-scroll, minimal): x' = -y - z; y' = x + a y; z' = b + z(x - c) (chaos at a=b=0.2, c=5.7). Chua (double-scroll): x' = alpha( y - x - g(x) ); y' = x - y + z; z' = -beta y, with piecewise-linear diode g(x) = m1 x + 0.5(m0-m1)(|x+1| - |x-1|). AUDIO COUPLING (go beyond a passive Lorenz): (a) additive forcing — add A*s(t) to one equation, e.g. x' = sigma(y-x) + A*s(t) where s(t) is the band-limited sample; (b) parameter modulation — rho(t) = rho0 + k * RMS_envelope(t), or Chua's alpha(t) driven by spectral centroid, sweeping the system through period-doubling bifurcations as loudness/brightness change; (c) map low/mid/high band energies to (sigma, rho, beta). Integrate with RK4 at audio rate (or an oversampled control rate): k1..k4, x_{n+1} = x_n + (h/6)(k1+2k2+2k3+4... ) standard Runge-Kutta.
techniques.what_it_reveals: It externalizes the dynamical 'temperature' of the sound: a quiet passage keeps rho below the chaos threshold so the attractor is a tidy limit cycle; a loud or bright hit pushes rho past the Hopf/period-doubling cascade and the single loop blossoms into the full butterfly. So you literally SEE bifurcation — the transition from order to chaos — driven by musical dynamics. Band-to-parameter mapping makes the attractor's wing-span, scroll count, and folding encode the spectral balance.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The Lorenz butterfly / Chua double-scroll is already iconic; making it breathe, sprout wings, and cascade into chaos on the beat is mesmerizing — a continuously redrawn 3D sculpture with motion blur trails. With audio forcing it never repeats, giving an organic, alive quality basic visualizers lack.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The control mapping auto-ranges: normalize each audio feature (RMS, centroid, band energy) by a running percentile so the parameters always traverse the interesting bifurcation interval regardless of input gain. The system can even auto-select WHICH attractor (Rössler's single scroll for simple tonal input, Chua's double-scroll for rich input) based on the measured embedding dimension/FNN from technique 1 — re-dimensioning the dynamical model to match the sound's complexity.
techniques.implementation_notes: Cheapest technique here: 3 state variables, RK4 is ~40 flops/step, run a few hundred steps per frame -> nothing. Keep an integration substep smaller than audio frame to stay stable when forcing is strong; clamp parameters to keep the system bounded (a runaway Lorenz with huge rho diverges — saturate rho, and detect/blow-up reset if |state| explodes). Render as a fading point/line trail (ring buffer of a few thousand states) via egui or wgpu line strip; additive blending + per-vertex age->alpha gives the glow. The app already has a basic Lorenz — the upgrade is the audio->parameter mapping, RK4 (vs Euler) for clean curves, auto-ranged features, and attractor auto-selection. Gotcha: forcing at full audio rate can alias the visual; low-pass the control signal.

references.title: Lorenz system (data-driven modeling lecture)
Rössler attractor (Wikipedia)
Circuits of Chaos: Lorenz, Chua, Rössler (Electronic Design)
Encryption of Audio Signals Using the Lorenz Chaotic System (arXiv 2409.14092)

techniques.name: Poincaré section / first-return map of the embedded audio trajectory
techniques.one_line: Slice the reconstructed attractor with a hyperplane and plot only the crossings, collapsing the 3D ribbon to a sparse 2D constellation or a 1D return map that exposes periodicity and routes to chaos.
techniques.the_math: Given the embedded flow v(t) in R^m, pick an oriented hypersurface Sigma = { v : <v - v0, n> = 0 } (e.g. the plane x(i+tau) = mean, crossed with positive velocity). Record P = { v(t_k) : trajectory crosses Sigma transversally, same direction }. This defines the Poincaré map P: Sigma -> Sigma, dim(Sigma)=m-1. For a 3D embedding the section is a 2D point cloud; reduce further to the FIRST-RETURN MAP by taking one scalar coordinate r_k at successive crossings and plotting r_{k+1} vs r_k. Interpolate the crossing precisely between bracketing samples: if g(v)=<v-v0,n> changes sign between t and t+1, t_cross = t + g(v_t)/(g(v_t)-g(v_{t+1})), and linearly interpolate the recorded coordinates there.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The deepest structural read of all: a periodic sound yields a FINITE set of dots (n dots = period-n); a quasi-periodic dyad yields a closed loop (a 1-torus section); a chaotic/noisy sound yields a fractal curve or a fuzzy cloud. The first-return map r_{k+1}=f(r_k) reveals the unimodal 'tent/logistic' shape of a period-doubling route to chaos — you can literally watch dots split 1->2->4->8 as a tone becomes rough/multiphonic. It separates 'sounds rough' (chaos) from 'sounds beating' (quasi-periodic torus), which the ear conflates.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Sparse, jewel-like — a scatter of points that snap into crisp geometric arrangements (a clean pentagon for a period-5 lock, a smooth parabola for the return map) then dissolve into fractal dust. The sudden crystallization/dissolution as the sound locks and unlocks is visually dramatic and rare in audio visualizers.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The section plane is auto-placed at the running centroid of the embedded cloud with normal along the direction of maximum variance (first PCA axis of the recent trajectory), so the slice always cuts the attractor where it is densest — self-orienting. The return-map axes auto-scale to the crossing-value range. Crossing direction and the choice of which coordinate to plot are derived from the data, not hand-set.
techniques.implementation_notes: Very cheap: detect sign changes of g(v) along the same ring buffer used by techniques 1/3, interpolate the crossing, push into a fading 2D scatter. A few crossings per cycle -> tiny point count, render trivially with egui circles or wgpu points. PCA for the auto-plane is a 3x3 covariance eigen-decompose over a few thousand recent points — microseconds with nalgebra. Gotcha: noise causes spurious double-crossings (chatter) near the plane; add a small hysteresis band around Sigma and require a minimum dwell between crossings. Works best downstream of techniques 1 or 3 since it consumes their embedded/integrated trajectory.

references.title: Poincaré map overview (ScienceDirect Topics)
Manifold learning of Poincaré sections (arXiv 2105.09888)
Is one dimensional return map sufficient... (arXiv 1409.6738)

techniques.name: Live correlation/fractal dimension & largest-Lyapunov readout as a self-scaling complexity gauge
techniques.one_line: Continuously estimate the attractor's correlation dimension D2 and the largest Lyapunov exponent, and feed them back to drive color, dimension count, and bloom — a numeric 'complexity meter' that also auto-tunes the other visuals.
techniques.the_math: Correlation sum (Grassberger-Procaccia): C(r) = (2 / (N(N-1))) sum_{i<j} Theta( r - ||v(i)-v(j)|| ). For small r, C(r) ~ r^{D2}, so the correlation dimension D2 = lim_{r->0} d(log C(r)) / d(log r) — estimated as the slope of log C(r) vs log r over the scaling (linear) region. Largest Lyapunov exponent (Rosenstein): for each embedded point find nearest neighbor, track mean log divergence <ln d_j(k)> of neighbor pairs over k steps; lambda_1 = slope of (1/dt)<ln d_j(k)> vs k. A cheaper proxy already available from RQA is DIV = 1/L_max (inverse longest diagonal) which scales with lambda_1.
techniques.what_it_reveals: A continuously updated scalar 'how chaotic / how many degrees of freedom' is the sound right now: D2 near 1 = a pure periodic tone, near 2 = quasi-periodic, fractional (2.05, 3.7...) = deterministic chaos, and growing toward the embedding dimension = noise. lambda_1 > 0 confirms genuine sensitive-dependence chaos vs mere complexity. Together they distinguish a flute (low D, lambda<=0) from a cymbal/breath (high D, lambda>0) — a quantitative timbre axis orthogonal to spectrum.
techniques.why_breathtaking: On its own it is a number, so its beauty comes from being the CONDUCTOR: map D2 to how many spatial dimensions/scrolls the attractor visual uses, map lambda_1 to trail length and chromatic spread, and the whole scene visibly 'heats up' (more wings, more color, more bloom) exactly when the math says the sound got chaotic. The log C(r)-vs-log r plot itself is an elegant straight-line-with-a-knee diagnostic overlay.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This is the autonomous-dimensioning engine for the entire facet: D2 measured from the data decides the rendering dimensionality and which attractor model to instantiate (technique 3's auto-select), the scaling region for the slope is detected automatically (find the longest stretch of near-constant local slope in log-log space), and lambda_1 sets temporal parameters (trail decay). The visualization measures its own subject's dimension and reconfigures accordingly.
techniques.implementation_notes: The correlation sum is O(N^2); keep N small (1-2k windowed embedded points) and evaluate C(r) on a log-spaced set of ~20 radii — a single GPU compute pass (pairwise Theta count) or a CPU spatial-grid box-count makes it realtime at a 5-10 Hz update, not per-frame. Slope/scaling-region detection: robust least-squares over the middle decade of log r. Rosenstein lambda needs a kd-tree neighbor search + short forward tracking — also keep N small and update at a few Hz. Cheapest path: skip full GP/Rosenstein and drive visuals from RQA's DET (order) and DIV=1/L_max (Lyapunov proxy), which you already computed in technique 2 for near-zero extra cost. Gotcha: D2 estimates are noisy on short windows — heavily temporally smooth before using them to switch dimensionality, or the scene will flicker between models.

references.title: Grassberger-Procaccia algorithm (Scholarpedia)
Grassberger & Procaccia, Measuring the Strangeness of Strange Attractors
Rosenstein et al., A practical method for calculating largest Lyapunov exponents (PhysioNet)
Selection procedures for the largest Lyapunov exponent (PMC6438190)

techniques.name: Stereo goniometer / Lissajous as a degenerate 2-channel phase portrait (with cross-recurrence extension)
techniques.one_line: Treat L and R channels as a genuine 2D phase space (no delay needed), giving the classic goniometer, and extend to a cross/joint recurrence plot to expose inter-channel coupling and delay.
techniques.the_math: Goniometer point cloud: plot (L(t), R(t)) directly — a delay-free embedding using two real observables. Common 45-degree-rotated form: u = (L+R)/sqrt2 (mid), w = (R-L)/sqrt2 (side); a mono signal -> vertical line, perfectly anti-phase -> horizontal line, decorrelated stereo -> circular blob. Cross-recurrence plot of the two channels' embeddings: CR_{i,j} = Theta( eps - || vL(i) - vR(j) || ), with vL, vR the delay embeddings of each channel; the 'line of synchronization' (LOS) — the longest near-diagonal black curve — and its OFFSET from the main diagonal measures the L/R time delay / phase lead, while its slope tracks relative tempo. Joint recurrence: JR_{i,j} = RL_{i,j} * RR_{i,j} (Hadamard product) marks simultaneous recurrence in both channels.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Stereo field and phase coherence at a glance: width (mono vs wide), polarity problems (energy in the anti-phase quadrant warns of mono-summing cancellation), and per-frequency imaging. The cross-recurrence LOS offset reveals an inter-channel delay (e.g. a Haas/comb mispatch) down to the sample, and its curvature reveals time-varying delay/pitch drift between channels — information no level meter shows.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The goniometer's drifting, flame-like point cloud is a beloved studio visual; rotating, persistence-trailed, color-by-frequency-band it is genuinely hypnotic, and the cross-recurrence plot adds an interpretable, glowing diagonal that literally bends when the channels desynchronize.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Auto-gains to the running RMS so the cloud fills the scope at any level; the correlation coefficient rho = E[LR]/sqrt(E[L^2]E[R^2]) auto-sets a 'width' readout and can drive the aspect/zoom. Cross-recurrence epsilon uses the same fixed-RR auto-threshold as technique 2, so coupling contrast stays constant across material. The technique gracefully degrades to a single-channel delay embedding (technique 1) when the input is mono — self-selecting its dimensioning source.
techniques.implementation_notes: Goniometer is the cheapest visual in the whole facet — one (x,y) per sample, draw a fading point cloud (egui or wgpu points), auto-gain via running RMS. The cross/joint recurrence matrix is the same O(N^2) GPU outer product as technique 2 but with two different embeddings; reuse that shader. LOS extraction = find the longest connected near-diagonal run (cheap image scan) and report its mean offset. Gotcha: per-sample point spam can overdraw — decimate or accumulate into a density texture with additive blend and decay; color-by-band requires a cheap 2-3 band split (biquad) before plotting.

references.title: Lissajous curve (Wikipedia)
Goniometers are a Powerful Acoustic Feature for MIR (arXiv 2302.01090)
Cross & joint recurrence plots (recurrence-plot.tk)
Cross-recurrence plot based synchronization of time series
