key_references.title: klustr: dimensionality reduction & visualization of large audio datasets
key_references.why: Shows the production-grade recipe (STFT → first 14 PCA components → UMAP→2D, colored by sample type) plus quality ranking via silhouette/roundness, and the t-SNE 45-min vs UMAP 2-min speed reality that decides realtime feasibility.

key_references.title: Self-organizing map — Wikipedia
key_references.why: Exact SOM math (BMU argmin, weight update with neighborhood θ and learning rate α, Gaussian kernel, decay schedules) and the U-matrix concept — the most realtime-friendly DR for a live navigable timbre map.

key_references.title: RAVE: A variational autoencoder for fast and high-quality neural audio synthesis (arXiv:2111.05011)
key_references.why: The realtime audio VAE: faster-than-realtime encoder, disentangled morphable latent, invertible embedding — the bridge from DR-as-visualization to DR-as-resynthesizable-timbre-space, and a deployable pretrained encoder for the app.

key_references.title: Oja learning rule — Scholarpedia
key_references.why: The streaming-PCA primitive: Hebbian update w += η y (x − y w) converges to the top eigenvector with no covariance matrix stored — the cheapest possible realtime self-scaling axis tracker for the audio thread.

key_references.title: A proposal and evaluation of new timbre visualisation methods for audio sample browsers (arXiv:2011.15096)
key_references.why: Audio-specific comparison of DR methods for timbre layout with neighborhood-preservation evaluation — grounds the trustworthiness/continuity metrics used for autonomous dimension selection.

cross_links: Feature extraction facet: every DR method here consumes a feature vector (MFCC, chroma, spectral centroid/spread/flatness, STFT magnitude) — the quality of the embedding is bounded by the feature set, and delta/delta-delta features add the temporal dimension that makes trajectories smooth.

Spectrogram/STFT facet: STFT magnitude frames are the raw high-dim input (klustr uses STFT→PCA→UMAP); DR is literally a learned nonlinear re-projection of the same time-frequency data a spectrogram shows linearly.

Clustering & segmentation facet: t-SNE/UMAP cluster structure and the SOM U-matrix are visual clustering; k-means/silhouette scores rank embedding quality and can color the points by discovered cluster.

Particle-system / point-cloud rendering facet: every embedding output is a set of 2D/3D points — GPU instanced point rendering (wgpu) is the shared substrate, and the UMAP 'galaxy' / latent trajectory are particle clouds colored by time or pitch.

Trajectory & manifold facet: projecting a feature time-series gives a path through the embedding; tangent/velocity along that path encodes timbral change rate, linking DR to phase-space and recurrence visualizations.
Neural-audio / resynthesis facet: the VAE latent is both a visualization axis and a generator — the only embedding here that is invertible, connecting DR to timbre morphing and resynthesis.

facet: Spatial / stereo / ambisonics sound visualization (vectorscope/goniometer geometry, inter-channel correlation, mid/side, ambisonics & spherical-harmonic soundfield encoding, HRTF/ITD/ILD, sound-intensity & DOA maps, beamforming heatmaps, 3D spatial-audio displays)
summary: This facet covers how to *see* where sound lives in space, from the cheapest possible 2-channel display to full 3D soundfield reconstruction. The spine of the whole facet is a single idea expressed at increasing dimensionality: the **mid/side rotation** of the stereo pair is the 1st-order, 2-channel special case of the **spherical-harmonic (SH) encoding** Y_l^m, and the **inter-channel correlation coefficient** is the 2-channel special case of the **diffuseness** scalar in DirAC. The most *breathtaking* members are the classic Lissajous goniometer (a phosphor point-cloud that draws the stereo image as living geometry) and the **active-intensity / DirAC soundfield arrow map** (a per-time-frequency-bin field of direction-of-arrival vectors colored by diffuseness — it literally shows the direction every frequency is flying from). The deepest *insight* members are the **per-band stereo-correlation / panorama spectrogram** (reveals phase cancellation and mono-incompatibility invisible to the ear and to mono meters) and **SRP-PHAT beamforming heatmaps**. The richest *autonomous-dimensioning* members are SH balloon plots (the visualization's dimensionality literally equals the ambisonic order N of the file — a 1st-order file gets a 4-lobe display, a 3rd-order file auto-grows to 16 lobes) and intensity-vector fields whose arrow density/length self-scale to energy. All are realtime-feasible in Rust+egui/wgpu; the goniometer and correlation meter are trivial CPU work, the spectrograms are one rustfft per channel per hop, and SH balloons/intensity fields are small GPU mesh draws.

techniques.name: Lissajous goniometer / stereo vectorscope (mid-side rotated phosphor point cloud)
techniques.one_line: Plot the stereo pair as a 2D parametric curve rotated 45 deg onto mid/side axes, with phosphor-style decay, so the stereo image becomes a living geometric shape.
techniques.the_math:
For each sample n with left L[n], right R[n], the raw Lissajous point is (R[n], L[n]). Rotate CCW by 45 deg onto the mid/side basis so vertical = mono content and horizontal = stereo difference:
  Mid  M[n] = (L[n]+R[n])/sqrt(2)
  Side S[n] = (L[n]-R[n])/sqrt(2)
Plotted screen coords: x = S[n] (horizontal), y = M[n] (vertical). (Equivalently a 2D rotation matrix [[cos45,-sin45],[sin45,cos45]] applied to (R,L).) Optional polar form: radius r[n]=sqrt(L^2+R^2), angle phi[n]=atan2(L[n],R[n]); a pure-mono signal sits at phi=45deg (vertical line), pure anti-phase at phi=-45deg (horizontal line), hard-left/right at 0/90deg diagonals. Phosphor persistence: accumulate into an intensity buffer A with exponential decay A <- A*exp(-dt/tau) + splat(x,y). For the MIR feature form (Ziemer/Schuller), the point cloud is binned into a 2D (or polar) histogram H(i,j) and log-compressed log(1+H) to make a fixed-size descriptor.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Mono-compatibility and phase problems you cannot hear on stereo speakers but that collapse on a mono system: anti-correlated material shows as a horizontal smear (will vanish in mono). Reveals stereo width (vertical=narrow, round ball=wide), panning bias (cloud tilts toward the louder side), and 'too wide' masters. The shape is a direct fingerprint of mixing decisions — genres and even individual albums cluster by goniometer shape.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It is the original audio-as-light display: a glowing green Lissajous figure that breathes and morphs with the music, instantly readable as a shape rather than a number. The phosphor-decay point cloud is gorgeous and iconic.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Auto-scale the plot radius to a running RMS or peak (AGC) so quiet passages still fill the scope; the decay constant tau can adapt to tempo/onset density so fast material doesn't smear. The histogram-feature version self-normalizes by total point count, giving a content-independent descriptor.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivial in Rust+egui. ~1-2k points/frame is plenty (decimate the audio buffer). The mid/side rotation is 2 adds + 2 scales per sample. Phosphor buffer: render to an egui texture (or wgpu) and multiply by a decay each frame, or additively blend small Gaussian splats. Pure CPU at 60fps; GPU only needed for very dense persistence. Gotcha: scale by 1/sqrt(2) to keep mono at unit radius and avoid clipping the display.

references.title: Goniometer (audio) — Wikipedia
Goniometers are a Powerful Acoustic Feature for MIR Tasks (Ziemer & Schuller, arXiv:2302.01090)
RTW — Focus: The Audio Vectorscope

techniques.name: Inter-channel correlation / phase-correlation meter (normalized zero-lag cross-correlation)
techniques.one_line: A single sliding-window scalar in [-1,+1] that measures how in-phase the two channels are — the 2-channel ancestor of diffuseness.
techniques.the_math:
Zero-lag normalized cross-correlation (Pearson coefficient) over a short window, using exponential or boxcar short-time averages E{.}:
  r = E{L*R} / sqrt( E{L^2} * E{R^2} )
With exponential averaging at coefficient alpha (per sample): update accumulators
  P_LR <- alpha*P_LR + (1-alpha)*L*R ; P_LL <- alpha*P_LL + (1-alpha)*L*L ; P_RR <- alpha*P_RR + (1-alpha)*R*R ; then r = P_LR/sqrt(P_LL*P_RR).
Normalizing by the per-channel variances/RMS decouples the meter from absolute level. Interpretation: r=+1 in-phase mono, r=0 fully decorrelated (max width / independent channels), r=-1 anti-phase (mono-cancelling). A per-frequency-bin version uses STFT coherence (see panorama spectrogram).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Mono-compatibility at a glance and over time; sustained negative readings warn that a master will partially disappear in mono (clubs, phones, broadcast). The eye cannot integrate phase relationships across thousands of samples — this number does.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Modest alone (a needle/bar), but as a colored time-history strip (green=+1 -> red=-1) under a spectrogram it becomes a striking 'phase weather' band.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The averaging time-constant alpha can auto-adapt to the signal's autocorrelation time (longer for sustained pads, shorter for transient/percussive material) so the meter is neither jittery nor sluggish.
techniques.implementation_notes: Essentially free: 3 multiply-accumulates per sample plus one sqrt+divide per display frame. Guard against divide-by-zero on silence (clamp denominator). Pure CPU.

references.title: False and Correct Audio Correlation Measurements (Beis)
Q. What are my phase-correlation meters telling me? — Sound on Sound

techniques.name: Per-band stereo panorama / correlation spectrogram (STFT mid-side + coherence)
techniques.one_line: A spectrogram whose color/position encodes WHERE each frequency sits in the stereo field and how phase-coherent it is — a 2D map of stereo image vs frequency vs time.
techniques.the_math:
Take STFTs of both channels: L(t,f), R(t,f) (one rustfft per channel per hop). Per time-frequency bin:
  Mid M=(L+R)/sqrt2, Side S=(L-R)/sqrt2.
  Panning index (signed, -1=hard left .. +1=hard right):
    pan(t,f) = (|R|^2 - |L|^2) / (|R|^2 + |L|^2)   [energy form]
  Inter-channel coherence (magnitude-squared coherence over a short time average <.>):
    gamma^2(t,f) = |<L R*>|^2 / ( <|L|^2> <|R|^2> )
  Inter-channel phase difference: dphi(t,f) = arg( L(t,f) R*(t,f) ).
Render as an image: x=time, y=log-frequency, hue=pan(t,f), brightness=magnitude, saturation or a second layer=coherence gamma^2 (or use HSL where lightness=|M|, hue=pan).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Exactly which frequency regions are wide vs mono, which are panned where, and which have phase cancellation — none of which a normal (summed) spectrogram or the ear can isolate. Instantly exposes a bass that should be mono but isn't, or a reverb tail that's anti-phase in the highs.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A spectrogram that glows in stereo color — left-panned content washes blue, right washes red, mono center stays neutral, phase-trouble bins flash. Far more beautiful and information-dense than a grayscale spectrogram.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Log-frequency axis and dynamic dB floor auto-fit to content (per-column adaptive normalization). FFT size can auto-select from a transient/tonal estimate (short windows for percussive, long for tonal). The coherence averaging window self-scales with bin frequency (longer averages at low f).
techniques.implementation_notes: Two rustfft calls per hop + cheap per-bin arithmetic; build an RGBA texture and blit in egui. ~2048-pt FFT, 50% overlap is realtime on one core. Color mapping is the main design choice. Gotcha: coherence needs averaging across a few frames or neighboring bins (a single bin's |<LR*>|^2/(<|L|^2><|R|^2>) is identically 1 without averaging).

references.title: Phase spectrogram and frequency spectrogram as new diagnostic tools — ScienceDirect
iZotope Insight 2 — Sound Field (stereo spectrogram metering)

techniques.name: DirAC / active-intensity soundfield vector map (B-format DOA + diffuseness field)
techniques.one_line: From a first-order (B-format) signal, compute a per-time-frequency direction-of-arrival arrow colored by diffuseness — a field that shows the direction every frequency is arriving from.
techniques.the_math:
B-format channels: W (pressure, omni), and X,Y,Z (figure-8 velocity components). Pressure P=W; particle velocity proportional to U=(X,Y,Z)/(rho c) (up to the 1/sqrt2 SN3D scaling on W). Instantaneous active intensity (per time-frequency bin, STFT domain):
  I(t,f) = Re{ P*(t,f) * U(t,f) } = Re{ W*(t,f) * [X,Y,Z](t,f) }   (a 3-vector)
Direction of arrival is OPPOSITE the intensity flow:
  azimuth(t,f)  = atan2( -I_y , -I_x )
  elevation(t,f)= atan2( -I_z , sqrt(I_x^2 + I_y^2) )
Energy density E(t,f) = ( |P|^2 + |rho c U|^2 ) / 2 (i.e. (|W|^2 + |X|^2+|Y|^2+|Z|^2)/2 in SN3D scaling).
Diffuseness (0=point source, 1=fully diffuse):
  psi(t,f) = 1 - || <I(t,f)> || / ( c * <E(t,f)> )
where <.> is a short-time/freq average. For plain stereo (no B-format) you can synthesize a pseudo-W=M and pseudo-Y=S to get a 2D version (azimuth-only).
techniques.what_it_reveals: The literal geometry of the recorded space: where each frequency component is coming from and whether the field is a clean point source or a diffuse reverberant wash. Separates direct sound from reverberation per band — impossible by ear in real time.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A swirling field of little arrows, one per band, each pointing at where its sound is in the room, fading to a diffuse cloud as reverb builds. It looks like a wind/flow visualization of the soundfield itself.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Arrow length auto-scales to per-bin energy; arrow color/opacity to diffuseness psi; the number of arrows = number of frequency bands chosen, which can auto-thin to only energetically significant bins. Works for stereo (2D, azimuth only) and auto-upgrades to full 3D hemisphere when fed B-format/ambisonic input.
techniques.implementation_notes: STFT of each channel (rustfft), then per-bin Re{W* X}, Re{W* Y}, Re{W* Z}, two atan2s, one norm/divide. Render arrows as instanced line meshes (egui painter for 2D, wgpu instancing for 3D). Realtime for hundreds of bins. Gotcha: needs B-format input for true 3D; for ordinary stereo it degrades to a 2D azimuth-only pseudo-intensity (W=mid, Y=side). Smooth psi over time to avoid flicker.

references.title: Pulkki et al. — Applications of Directional Audio Coding (DirAC)
Generalized intensity vector and energy density in the spherical-harmonic domain

techniques.name: Spherical-harmonic soundfield balloon / hemisphere energy map (ambisonic order = display dimensionality)
techniques.one_line: Reconstruct and render the ambisonic soundfield's angular energy as a deforming sphere whose lobe count literally equals the file's ambisonic order N.
techniques.the_math:
Real SH basis (AmbiX: ACN ordering, SN3D normalization). ACN index of degree l, order m: ACN = l^2 + l + m. SN3D normalization:
  N_l^m = sqrt( (2 - delta_m) * (l-|m|)! / (l+|m|)! ),  delta_m = 1 if m=0 else 0.
Real SH: Y_l^m(theta,phi) = N_l^m * P_l^|m|(sin phi) * {cos(|m| theta) if m>=0, sin(|m| theta) if m<0}.
First order (Cartesian, on unit dir (x,y,z)): Y00=1; Y1,-1=sqrt3 y; Y1,0=sqrt3 z; Y1,1=sqrt3 x. Second order: Y2,-2=sqrt15 xy; Y2,-1=sqrt15 yz; Y2,0=sqrt5/2(3z^2-1); Y2,1=sqrt15 xz; Y2,2=sqrt15/2(x^2-y^2).
Soundfield power as a function of direction (beamform/plane-wave decomposition): for ambisonic signal vector b(t,f) with components b_ACN, the directional energy map is
  D(theta,phi) = | sum_{l,m} w_l * b_{ACN(l,m)} * Y_l^m(theta,phi) |^2
with max-rE or in-phase weights w_l for a clean beam. Render r(theta,phi)=D as a radius -> a deforming 'balloon'.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The full angular distribution of energy on the sphere — where sound sits all around (and above/below) the listener, and how sharply localized it is (sharper lobes at higher order). Directly visualizes the spatial resolution the recording actually contains.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A glowing 3D blob that pulses and reaches toward sound sources in every direction, sprouting more, sharper lobes for higher-order content. It is the canonical 'shape of a soundfield'.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: THE flagship autonomous-dimensioning case: the visualization's complexity self-determines from the file. A 1st-order (4-channel) file yields 4 SH lobes; a 3rd-order (16-channel) file auto-expands to 16, with finer angular detail and a denser render mesh — the display's effective dimensionality = (N+1)^2 channels with no user input. Mesh tessellation and lobe sharpness auto-scale to N; radius auto-normalizes to peak directional energy.
techniques.implementation_notes: Precompute Y_l^m on a fixed sphere mesh (e.g. icosphere, a few thousand verts) once; per frame compute D(vertex)=|sum b_ACN Y|^2 (a small matrix-vector per vertex) — easily a wgpu compute/vertex shader, or CPU for low orders. egui can host the wgpu view. Gotcha: watch normalization convention (SN3D vs N3D differ by sqrt(2l+1)); pick max-rE weights to avoid ugly side-lobes; needs ambisonic/B-format input (stereo only gives a degenerate 1st-order-ish balloon via W=mid,Y=side).

references.title: Ambisonic data exchange formats (ACN/SN3D) — Wikipedia
Zotter & Frank — All-Round Ambisonic Panning and Decoding (max-rE weights)
IEM DirPat / balloon_holo directivity visualization

techniques.name: SRP-PHAT / delay-and-sum beamforming heatmap (acoustic-camera DOA map)
techniques.one_line: Sweep a steered beamformer over candidate directions and paint the steered power as a heatmap — where the array 'looks', the sound lights up.
techniques.the_math:
For M channels with signals s_m and STFTs S_m(omega), steered-response power at candidate location/direction x:
  P(x) = sum_{m1,m2} R_{m1,m2}( tau_{m1,m2}(x) )
where the generalized cross-correlation is
  R_{m1,m2}(tau) = (1/2pi) integral Phi_{m1,m2}(omega) S_{m1}(omega) S_{m2}*(omega) e^{j omega tau} d omega.
PHAT weighting (whitens to phase only, robust to reverb):
  Phi_{m1,m2}(omega) = 1 / | S_{m1}(omega) S_{m2}*(omega) |.
Steering delay (TDOA) for candidate x: tau_{m1,m2}(x) = round( f_s * ( ||x - x_{m1}|| - ||x - x_{m2}|| ) / c ).
Plain delay-and-sum (no whitening): P0(x) = sum_n | sum_m s_m(n - tau_m(x)) |^2. Render P(x) over an azimuth-elevation grid as a heatmap; peak = DOA.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The physical location/direction of sound sources from a mic array — a true acoustic camera. Resolves multiple simultaneous sources and rejects reverberation (PHAT) in a way no single-channel display can.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A heatmap of 'sound hotspots' overlaid on space — the acoustic-camera look (think the colorful blobs over a machine/orchestra). Very high wow, especially animated.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The search grid resolution can auto-refine around emerging peaks (coarse-to-fine); the dB color range auto-fits to the current peak/noise floor; the dimensionality (1D azimuth line vs 2D az-el map vs 3D volume) adapts to array geometry (linear=1D, planar=2D, volumetric=3D).
techniques.implementation_notes: Requires a multichannel (>=3-4 mic) input — niche for a typical stereo studio app, so feasibility is content-limited, not compute-limited. GCC-PHAT via rustfft + IFFT per mic pair; grid evaluation is embarrassingly parallel (rayon or a wgpu compute shader sampling the GCC tables). For 2 channels it collapses to a single ITD/azimuth estimate, not a full map. Gotcha: PHAT denominator needs an epsilon floor; grid steering delays should be interpolated (fractional) for smooth maps.

references.title: Steered-response power — Wikipedia (SRP, GCC-PHAT, TDOA formulas)
A Steered Response Power Method for Sound Source Localization (arXiv:2509.15702)

techniques.name: HRTF binaural cue display (ITD/ILD spherical-head model + interaural cross-correlation)
techniques.one_line: Map the interaural time and level differences of a binaural signal onto a perceived-azimuth arc, optionally driven by the spherical-head Woodworth model.
techniques.the_math:
Spherical-head Woodworth/Schlosberg ITD for a source at azimuth theta (head radius a, sound speed c):
  ITD(theta) = (a/c) * ( theta + sin theta )   (seconds; theta in radians, lateral angle from median plane)
Interaural level difference is the dominant high-frequency / near-field cue: ILD(f,theta) = 20 log10( |H_R(f,theta)| / |H_L(f,theta)| ) dB, with a rigid-sphere diffraction model for |H|. Estimate cues from a binaural signal by the running interaural cross-correlation function (per critical band):
  IACC(tau) = <L(t) R(t+tau)> / sqrt( <L^2> <R^2> ),  for tau in [-1ms, +1ms];
  estimated ITD = argmax_tau IACC(tau);  perceived width/spaciousness ~ 1 - max_tau IACC (the binaural cousin of diffuseness). Map (ITD,ILD) -> azimuth via the model inverse and plot a glowing arc/dot on a head-centered azimuth display, per band.
techniques.what_it_reveals: How a binaural/HRTF-processed mix will actually localize for a listener — the perceived azimuth and apparent source width per frequency band, including front/back and elevation cues encoded in the spectral notches. Predicts spaciousness (IACC) the way concert-hall acousticians measure it.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A head-centered radar where each band's source glides around an arc as you pan it, with apparent-source-width blooming as a halo. Elegant and immediately intuitive.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Per-band lag search window auto-scales (low bands allow larger ITD); the azimuth mapping auto-calibrates head radius a from the signal's max observed ITD; number of band-dots adapts to energetic bands. Degrades gracefully: with plain stereo it shows the ITD/ILD a panner imposes; with true binaural it shows full HRTF localization.
techniques.implementation_notes: IACC is a short normalized cross-correlation over +/-~40 samples at 44.1kHz per band — cheap (or via rustfft cross-spectrum). ILD is per-band RMS ratio. Spherical-head model is closed-form. egui painter draws the head/arc/dots. Pure CPU, realtime. Gotcha: ITD argmax is ambiguous for periodic/tonal content (multiple correlation peaks) — band-limit and pick the peak nearest zero, or weight by band importance (500-4kHz).

references.title: Woodworth spherical-head ITD model — testing/correcting/extending (ResearchGate)

cross_links: Spectrogram/STFT facet: the per-band panorama spectrogram and DirAC arrow map both ride on the same windowed STFT (rustfft per channel per hop) used by ordinary spectrograms — share the FFT pipeline and just add the L/R cross terms.
Color/perceptual-mapping facet: panorama spectrogram and SRP heatmap depend entirely on perceptually-uniform colormaps (HSL pan-hue, viridis/turbo energy) and adaptive dB normalization.
3D rendering / GPU facet: SH balloons and 3D intensity fields are wgpu mesh/instancing problems — overlap with any waveform-mesh or particle-system facet.

Dimensionality-reduction facet: the goniometer histogram feature and SH energy maps are both low-rank projections of a high-dimensional soundfield, conceptually adjacent to PCA/t-SNE timbre embeddings.
Pitch/harmony facet: inter-channel correlation and IACC reuse the same normalized cross-correlation machinery as autocorrelation pitch detection — one correlator engine serves both.
Loudness/metering facet: the correlation meter and ILD/balance displays pair naturally with LUFS/true-peak meters as a unified mastering metering panel.

facet: Generative / particle / fluid aesthetics — audio-reactive particle systems, reaction-diffusion, stable-fluids, curl-noise, and the demoscene/shader-art tradition modulated by spectrum/onsets, with the Mandala mode as the aesthetic frontier.
summary: This facet covers the family of generative simulations whose internal forces, birth rates, and diffusion parameters are continuously modulated by audio features (band energies, onsets/spectral flux, RMS). The strongest techniques are: curl-noise flow fields (divergence-free turbulence that is the cheapest way to get "fluid-looking" particle motion without a solver), Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion (Turing patterns whose feed/kill point can be steered by the spectrum to morph between spots/stripes/mazes/mitosis live), Stam's Stable Fluids (a true Navier-Stokes grid solver driven by injecting onset-located force splats), and kaleidoscopic domain-folding (the literal math behind a "Mandala" mode, which turns any of the above into N-fold radial symmetry essentially for free). The deepest INSIGHT lever is autonomous dimensioning: instead of a fixed band→parameter map, you let the audio's own statistics (spectral centroid, flux distribution, running percentiles) place the simulation at the edge-of-chaos operating point — Gray-Scott near its bifurcation boundary, fluids near the onset of turbulence — where small audio changes cause large, legible visual change. All four are realtime-feasible in Rust+egui: curl-noise and kaleidoscope fold are trivial on CPU or in a fragment shader; Gray-Scott and Stable Fluids want a wgpu compute/ping-pong texture pass but run at 60fps on modest grids (256²–512²). The breathtaking ceiling is high because these are the exact techniques behind award-winning demoscene and TouchDesigner audio-reactive work.

techniques.name: Curl-noise flow field (divergence-free turbulence) advecting a particle system, band-modulated
techniques.one_line: Advect particles along v = ∇×ψ where ψ is band-modulated Perlin/Simplex noise — incompressible 'fluid' motion with zero solver cost.
techniques.the_math: Velocity is the curl of a vector potential ψ so it is automatically divergence-free (∇·(∇×ψ)=0), meaning particles never clump or vanish. 2D (stream function, scalar ψ): u=∂ψ/∂y, v=−∂ψ/∂x, i.e. velocity = 90°-rotated gradient of the noise: v(x,y)=( ∂ψ/∂y, −∂ψ/∂x ). Compute ∂ψ/∂y ≈ (ψ(x,y+ε)−ψ(x,y−ε))/(2ε) with ε≈1px and ψ = noise(x/L, y/L, t). 3D: v=∇×Ψ with Ψ=(ψx,ψy,ψz) three independent noise fields, u=∂ψz/∂y−∂ψy/∂z, v=∂ψx/∂z−∂ψz/∂x, w=∂ψy/∂x−∂ψx/∂y. Multi-octave turbulence: v=Σ_i A_i · curl(noise(x/L_i, t·S_i)) with length scales L_i=L_0/2^i. Particle update: x ← x + Δt·v(x,t). AUDIO COUPLING: amplitude A_i := band_energy[i] (low band → big slow scrolls, high band → fine fast eddies), time-warp S_i := 1 + α·onset_env, and inject a radial/swirl term scaled by RMS.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Maps the spectral envelope onto *spatial scale*: bass becomes large-scale laminar drift, treble becomes fine vorticity, so you literally see the timbre's roughness as the granularity of the flow. The incompressibility constraint means the eye reads coherent 'streamlines' — you perceive the spectral balance as a fluid texture rather than as bars.
techniques.why_breathtaking: This is the look behind most high-end music-video particle work: silk-smooth, never-clumping, organically turbulent ribbons of millions of particles. It reads as living smoke/ink reacting to the music with no uncanny rigidity.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Octave count and base length scale L_0 self-scale to the framebuffer size and particle count (L_0 ≈ √(area/N)). Per-octave amplitudes A_i are driven directly by the live band-energy vector, so the *effective dimensionality of motion* (how many scales are active) expands and contracts with spectral richness — a pure sine collapses to one octave, a dense mix lights all octaves. Normalize band energies by a running percentile so the field auto-gain-stages to any track's loudness.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivial and cheap. CPU: a few Simplex-noise evaluations per particle per frame (use a fast simplex crate; rustfft already gives you the bands). GPU: do curl in a fragment/compute shader, store particles in a texture or SSBO and advect on-GPU — 1M+ particles at 60fps on wgpu. Gotchas: use Simplex not classic Perlin in ≥3D (O(n²) vs O(n·2ⁿ)); compute curl by *analytic or central-difference* of noise, never by differentiating an already-curled field; keep ε small but >0 to avoid noise quantization. No boundary handling needed unless you want walls (then modulate ψ by a ramp near boundaries, Bridson §).

references.title: Bridson, Hourihan, Nordenstam — Curl-Noise for Procedural Fluid Flow (SIGGRAPH 2007)
Curl-Flow: Boundary-Respecting Incompressible Velocity Interpolation (arXiv 2104.00867, ar5iv)
CaffeineViking/cnpf — Curl-Noise Particle Flow (OpenCL reference impl)

techniques.name: Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion with audio-steered feed/kill operating point
techniques.one_line: Run two coupled reaction-diffusion PDEs and move the (feed, kill) point along a curated path through the Turing phase diagram with the spectrum — spots, stripes, mazes, mitosis morphing to the music.
techniques.the_math: Two coupled reaction-diffusion PDEs on a grid: ∂u/∂t = Du·∇²u − u·v² + F·(1−u);  ∂v/∂t = Dv·∇²v + u·v² − (F+k)·v. Here u,v are chemical concentrations, Du,Dv diffusion coefficients (canonical Du=0.2 or 1.0, Dv=0.1 or 0.5, Dv≈Du/2), F=feed, k=kill, and u·v² is the autocatalytic reaction A+2B→3B. Explicit Euler update with discrete Laplacian (3×3 convolution kernel, center −1, edge 0.2, corner 0.05): u' = u + Δt·(Du·Lap(u) − u·v² + F(1−u)); v' = v + Δt·(Dv·Lap(v) + u·v² − (F+k)v). The (F,k) plane is a phase diagram: F≈0.034,k≈0.095 → worms/mazes; k→0.097 → spots; F=0.032,k=0.059 → stripes; F=0.0367,k=0.0649 → mitosis/cell-division; F=0.0545,k=0.062 → fingerprints. AUDIO COUPLING: (F,k) ← morph along a curated path through this zoo driven by spectral centroid & low/high band ratio; seed v with onset 'drops' (paint a disc of v=1 at random/beat-locked points on each onset); modulate Δt or Du by RMS for tempo of growth.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals *sustained spectral state* vs *transients* separately: the slowly-morphing (F,k) point shows the track's tonal center evolving over seconds, while onset-seeded v-splats show every percussive hit as a new colony that then grows according to the current chemistry. You see the song's macro-structure as pattern-class (spots vs mazes) and its rhythm as nucleation events.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Turing patterns are mesmerizingly organic — coral, leopard, fingerprint, dividing cells. Watching them *morph between regimes* in time to music (spots dissolving into labyrinths as a filter sweeps up) is a rare, hypnotic effect almost no consumer visualizer does.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The key autonomous move is to keep (F,k) *near the bifurcation boundary* between pattern classes, where the system is maximally sensitive: map the audio not to absolute (F,k) but to a 1-D arc-length along a precomputed 'interesting' curve through the F-k phase diagram, with the audio's own dynamic range (running min/max of the controlling feature) rescaled to the full arc. Grid resolution auto-scales to window size; Δt auto-clamps to the explicit-stability limit Δt < 0.5·min(dx²/Du, dx²/Dv).
techniques.implementation_notes: Wants GPU ping-pong (two RG/RGBA textures, swap each step, 1–4 substeps/frame for stability) in wgpu — a ~40-line compute or fragment shader; 256²–512² runs trivially at 60fps. CPU-only is fine at 128²–256² with a separable/explicit Laplacian. Gotchas: explicit Euler is conditionally stable — enforce the Δt bound or it 'blows up'; keep Du>Dv (short-range activator, long-range inhibitor) or no patterns form; clamp u,v to [0,1]; smooth (F,k) changes (low-pass the audio control) or the pattern shatters. Color via a palette LUT on v, or on |∇v| for crisp edges.

references.title: Gray-Scott Model: A Turing Pattern Cellular Automaton — Biological Modeling (exact update eqns & params)
Reaction Diffusion: The Gray-Scott Algorithm — Algosome (Laplacian kernel & code)
Karl Sims — Reaction-Diffusion Tutorial (canonical F/k pattern atlas)

techniques.name: Stam Stable Fluids (Navier-Stokes grid solver) driven by onset-located force splats
techniques.one_line: Run the unconditionally-stable force/advect/diffuse/project Navier-Stokes solver and inject a force+dye splat on every onset — real vortices that persist and interact to the beat.
techniques.the_math: Incompressible Navier-Stokes: ∂u/∂t = −(u·∇)u + ν∇²u + f, with ∇·u=0. Stam's unconditionally-stable operator splitting runs four steps per frame: (1) add force f; (2) advect by semi-Lagrangian backtrace: u_new(x)=u_old(x−Δt·u(x)) — trace each cell backward and bilinearly sample, stable for any Δt; (3) diffuse by implicit solve (I−νΔt∇²)u_new=u_old via Gauss-Seidel/Jacobi iterations; (4) project to divergence-free by solving Poisson ∇²q=∇·u then u←u−∇q (Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition). A passive dye/density field d is advected by the same semi-Lagrangian step for visualization. AUDIO COUPLING: on each detected onset inject a force splat f = G·(direction)·gain at a beat-locked or band-mapped location and a dye blob; set swirl/vorticity-confinement strength ∝ high-band energy; set buoyancy/global force ∝ bass; viscosity ν ∝ inverse of spectral flatness (noisy→thin, tonal→syrupy).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Unlike curl-noise (kinematic), this is *dynamic* — momentum is conserved and advected, so a single bass hit produces a vortex that persists and interacts with later hits. You see the *temporal correlation* of the music: how a groove's hits reinforce or cancel as real fluid momentum, revealing rhythmic phrasing as constructive/destructive vortex interaction.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Real smoke/ink with real vortices curling off every drum hit. The persistence and interaction give it a physicality and 'aliveness' that noise fields can't fake — this is the gold standard for premium reactive fluid visuals.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Grid resolution and iteration count auto-scale to a frame-time budget (drop from 512² to 256² if a frame exceeds ~14ms). Onset force gain is normalized by the running flux percentile so quiet and loud passages both produce well-scaled splats. Vorticity-confinement ε and viscosity ν are set from live spectral descriptors, so the fluid's *character* (wispy vs thick, turbulent vs laminar) tracks timbre automatically rather than via fixed sliders.
techniques.implementation_notes: The most expensive of the four but very doable: the projection Poisson solve is the cost. On wgpu, do all four steps as compute passes with ~20–40 Jacobi iterations for pressure at 256²–512², 60fps achievable. The Stam reference is famously ~100 lines. Gotchas: semi-Lagrangian advection is numerically diffusive (smears dye) — add vorticity confinement f_vc = ε·h·(N×ω) to re-inject curl; boundary conditions on the pressure solve matter (reflect velocity at walls, set ∂p/∂n=0); Jacobi needs enough iters or fluid looks 'gummy'. rustfft enables an alternative *spectral* Poisson solve (FFT-based projection) which is exact and fast on a periodic torus domain — a nice fit since you already have rustfft.

references.title: Jos Stam — Stable Fluids (SIGGRAPH 1999); see Dan Morris's annotated notes
Andrew Chan — Simulating Fluids, Fire, and Smoke in Real-Time (GPU walkthrough)
Solving Navier-Stokes with the Stable Fluids Algorithm (step-by-step, force/advect/diffuse/project)

techniques.name: Kaleidoscopic domain folding — the 'Mandala' mode (N-fold radial symmetry post-process)
techniques.one_line: Fold screen space into one wedge via polar mod+abs so any of the above sims becomes an N-fold symmetric mandala — symmetry order driven by a musical feature.
techniques.the_math: Convert pixel coord p=(x,y) (centered) to polar: r=|p|, a=atan2(y,x). Fold the angle into one of N mirrored wedges: segment=π/N; a=mod(a, 2·segment); a=abs(a−segment) (this gives N kaleidoscope mirrors). Optionally rotate first: a += φ(t). Map back to Cartesian to sample the source field: p' = r·(cos a, sin a). For a true mandala add a radial mirror (r → |mod(r,2R)−R|) and feed the folded coordinate into curl-noise/Gray-Scott/fluid above so the *simulation itself* is sampled symmetrically. Recursive/fractal mandala: apply the fold k times with shrinking radius p ← fold(p·scale)/scale. AUDIO COUPLING: symmetry order N := quantize(beat phase) or N := 3..12 mapped from chroma/key; rotation speed dφ/dt ∝ tempo; radial mirror spacing R ∝ bass; per-petal hue from band energies.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Symmetry is a *perceptual amplifier of structure*: folding multiplies every feature N times so subtle asymmetric detail in the underlying field (a single vortex, one onset splat) becomes a bold N-petal bloom. Changing N on harmonic/key changes makes the *tonality* of the music legible as the rotational symmetry group of the image.
techniques.why_breathtaking: This is the explicit aesthetic frontier of a 'Mandala' mode — sacred-geometry, stained-glass, mandala blooms that pulse and rotate to the beat. Symmetry triggers strong innate 'beauty' response; combined with a fluid/RD source it's reliably jaw-dropping.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The symmetry order N is itself an autonomously-chosen *dimension of the output*: derive it from the music (chroma → N∈{3,4,5,6,8,12}) so the mandala's geometry self-selects to the song's harmony. Fold count k (recursion depth) scales with available GPU headroom and with onset density (busier passages → deeper fractal folding). Rotation and radial-mirror spacing auto-scale to tempo and bass envelope, so the whole symmetry apparatus reparametrizes per track with no manual tuning.
techniques.implementation_notes: Essentially free — it's a coordinate transform applied in the sampling step of whatever you already render, ~5 lines of GLSL/WGSL (mod, abs, atan2, length). Pairs with every other technique in this facet as a pure post/pre-process. Gotchas: atan2 has a seam at ±π — folding hides it, but watch for shimmer at petal boundaries (use smoothstep or supersample near r→0 where angular resolution explodes); quantize/low-pass N changes to whole numbers on beat to avoid tearing; for the recursive form watch precision at deep k. Pure fragment shader, no compute needed.

references.title: Daniel Ilett — Crazy Kaleidoscopes (polar fold + mirror math)
LYGIA Shader Library — kaleidoscope() (multi-language reference impl)
Inigo Quilez — domain repetition / folding (mod-based symmetry foundations)

techniques.name: SPH metaball fluid — particle-based Navier-Stokes with audio-modulated pressure/buoyancy
techniques.one_line: Simulate a few thousand SPH particles (poly6 density, spiky pressure-gradient kernel) and render as a metaball surface; bass = gravity flips, onsets = momentum injections.
techniques.the_math: SPH approximates fields as kernel-weighted sums over neighbors j within smoothing length h. Density: ρ_i=Σ_j m_j·W_poly6(r_ij,h), W_poly6=315/(64πh⁹)·(h²−r²)³ for 0≤r≤h. Pressure (Tait/ideal gas): p_i=κ(ρ_i−ρ0). Pressure force uses the spiky-kernel gradient (non-zero at r→0, prevents clumping): f_i^press=−Σ_j m_j·(p_i+p_j)/(2ρ_j)·∇W_spiky, W_spiky=15/(πh⁶)·(h−r)³, ∇W_spiky=−45/(πh⁶)·(h−r)²·r̂. Viscosity: f_i^visc=μΣ_j m_j·(u_j−u_i)/ρ_j·∇²W_visc, ∇²W_visc=45/(πh⁶)·(h−r). Integrate: a_i=(f_i^press+f_i^visc+f_ext)/ρ_i, then leapfrog/symplectic-Euler. AUDIO COUPLING: external force f_ext = gravity·g(bass) (invert/zero gravity on drops for floaty bursts), inject impulse Δu on onsets, set rest density ρ0 or stiffness κ from a band so the fluid 'tightens'/'loosens' with the music; map particle color to local speed |u| or to the band that birthed it.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Shows *mass and conservation* explicitly: blobs merge, splash, and settle, so you read the music as gravity and impulse. Sloshing reveals the beat's downbeat (gravity flips), and the surface tension/metaball merging makes spectral 'thickness' (how tonal vs noisy) visible as how cohesive the blob stays.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Liquid-metal / mercury blobs that splash and coalesce to the beat — the metaball iso-surface gives a glossy, tactile, premium look. Fewer particles than curl-noise but each one carries real fluid behavior.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Smoothing length h auto-derives from particle count and domain volume (h ∝ (V·target_neighbors/N)^{1/d}) so the simulation stays well-resolved at any N. Stiffness κ and viscosity μ are set from live spectral flatness so the fluid's phase (runny vs viscous) tracks timbre. Neighbor grid cell size = h is recomputed when h changes, so spatial hashing self-tunes.
techniques.implementation_notes: Moderate cost: O(N·neighbors) with a uniform-grid/spatial-hash neighbor search; ~5–20k particles realtime on CPU, far more on GPU (build the hash and do density/force passes as compute). Render the surface via screen-space metaballs or marching-squares on a density splat. Gotchas: poly6 for density but spiky for pressure gradient is mandatory (poly6's vanishing center gradient causes clumping — the exact bug Müller's split kernels fix); pick Δt by CFL (Δt<λ·h/|u|max); use Tait/weakly-compressible or position-based-fluids (PBF) for stiffer incompressibility and bigger steps. Heavier than curl-noise; only choose it when you specifically want splashing mass.

references.title: Müller, Charypar, Gross — Particle-Based Fluid Simulation for Interactive Applications (SCA 2003)
Lucas V. Schuermann — Particle-Based Fluid Simulation with SPH (poly6/spiky/viscosity derivations)
CMU 15-467 — Position-Based Fluids assignment (stiffer realtime alternative to weakly-compressible SPH)

techniques.name: Onset/spectral-flux 'force-event' layer — the autonomous coupling engine shared by all the above
techniques.one_line: A half-wave-rectified spectral-flux novelty function with adaptive thresholding converts the audio into discrete force/birth events and continuous band drives that steer every simulation.
techniques.the_math: Per STFT frame n with magnitude spectrum |X(n,k)|: spectral flux SF(n)=Σ_k H(|X(n,k)|−|X(n−1,k)|), H(x)=max(x,0) (half-wave rectifier; only energy *increases* count). Optionally log-compress: use log(1+γ|X|) (γ≈1) before differencing for perceptual scaling. Adaptive peak-pick: an onset is flagged when SF(n) > median(SF[n−w..n+w]) + δ AND SF(n) is a local max — the moving-median makes the threshold self-scaling. Continuous drives: band energies E_b(n)=Σ_{k∈band_b}|X(n,k)|² (log-spaced/Bark bands), each smoothed by a one-pole envelope env←max(E_b, λ·env), λ≈0.9. Spectral centroid C=Σ_k f_k|X_k| / Σ_k|X_k| (brightness), spectral flatness = geomean/mean (tonal vs noisy). These scalars are exactly the {A_i, F, k, N, ν, gravity, gain} knobs referenced by the other techniques. Superflux variant: max-filter the previous frame's spectrum before differencing to suppress vibrato false-positives.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Separates the audio into the two things a generative visual needs: *impulses* (onsets → discrete births/forces) and *fields* (smoothed band/centroid/flatness → continuous parameters). It exposes structure invisible to the ear-as-meter: e.g. spectral flatness cleanly distinguishes a snare (noisy) from a tom (tonal) and routes them to different visual responses.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Not breathtaking itself — it's the nervous system that makes everything else *feel* tightly locked to the music instead of vaguely pulsing. Good onset coupling is the single biggest factor in whether a reactive visual reads as 'alive.'
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This is the autonomous-dimensioning core of the whole facet: the moving-median threshold auto-scales sensitivity to the track's loudness; running percentiles on each band auto-gain-stage the drives so a quiet ambient piece and a loud EDM track both fully exploit the visual's parameter range; band layout can be Bark/log so resolution follows perceptual frequency. Every downstream parameter range is set relative to these live statistics rather than hard-coded.
techniques.implementation_notes: Cheap and already half-built: rustfft gives |X(n,k)| each hop (hop≈512–1024 @ 44.1k → ~43–86 novelty fps, interpolate to frame rate). Maintain ring buffers for the moving median (or a fast streaming quantile) and one-pole envelopes per band. Gotchas: choose hop/window for onset time resolution (~10ms) vs frequency resolution tradeoff; the half-wave rectifier and median threshold are essential to avoid double-triggers; smooth continuous drives (one-pole) before feeding RD/fluid params or patterns shatter; align audio analysis latency to the render so hits land on-frame.

references.title: Spectral flux onset detection — Music Information Retrieval (formula & rectifier)
Böck & Widmer — Maximum Filter Vibrato Suppression for Onset Detection (SuperFlux)
A Basic Tutorial on Novelty and Activation Functions for Music Signal Processing (TISMIR)

key_references.title: Bridson, Hourihan, Nordenstam — Curl-Noise for Procedural Fluid Flow (SIGGRAPH 2007)
key_references.why: Canonical source for divergence-free turbulence from noise — the cheapest path to 'fluid-looking' particle motion and the backbone technique of this facet.

key_references.title: Jos Stam — Stable Fluids (SIGGRAPH 1999), annotated notes by Dan Morris
key_references.why: The unconditionally-stable force/advect/diffuse/project Navier-Stokes solver; the reference implementation any realtime fluid visual is built on.

key_references.title: Gray-Scott Model — Biological Modeling (exact PDEs, params, update rule)
key_references.why: Gives the precise reaction-diffusion equations and the feed/kill parameter atlas needed to steer Turing patterns with audio.

key_references.title: Müller, Charypar, Gross — Particle-Based Fluid Simulation for Interactive Applications (2003)
key_references.why: Defines the poly6/spiky/viscosity SPH kernels with constants — the foundation for metaball/liquid-blob audio fluids.

key_references.title: Spectral flux onset detection — Music Information Retrieval
key_references.why: The half-wave-rectified spectral-flux novelty function with adaptive thresholding — the autonomous coupling engine that maps audio to forces, births, and diffusion params.

key_references.title: Daniel Ilett — Crazy Kaleidoscopes / LYGIA kaleidoscope()
key_references.why: The polar mod+abs fold math that literally implements the 'Mandala' mode as an N-fold-symmetry coordinate transform over any source field.

cross_links: Spectrogram/STFT facet: the band energies, spectral flux, centroid, and flatness that drive every simulation here come straight from the same rustfft STFT — share one analysis buffer.
Geometry/symmetry facet: the kaleidoscope domain-fold (mod+abs in polar) is the same math as SDF domain-repetition and ties directly into any 'Mandala' raymarching mode.
Color/palette facet: all field outputs (curl speed |v|, Gray-Scott v, fluid dye density, SPH speed) are scalar fields that need a palette LUT — the mapping from scalar→color is shared.
Beat/tempo facet: onset events and tempo estimate gate force splats, gravity flips, and the quantized symmetry order N; a shared beat tracker improves lock for all techniques.
GPU pipeline facet: Gray-Scott, Stable Fluids, and SPH all want wgpu ping-pong textures / compute SSBOs — they can share one compute-pass scaffold and double-buffer pattern.
Chromagram/key facet: harmonic content (chroma) is the natural autonomous driver for mandala symmetry order N and Gray-Scott palette, linking musical key to image geometry.

facet: Adaptive / autonomous display techniques — auto-ranging, AGC, multi-resolution STFT, perceptual normalization, salience-driven emphasis, and self-tuning smoothing that dimension the display to the signal
summary: This facet is the engineering of a display that measures itself against the signal and re-dimensions on the fly: it decides its own dynamic range, its own time-frequency resolution per band, its own color limits, and its own idea of what is "interesting" frame to frame. The strongest ideas split into four families. (1) Auto-ranging / AGC and meter ballistics: one-pole exponential smoothers with asymmetric attack/release time constants (alpha = exp(-1/(tau*fs))) and percentile-based color limits estimated online with the P-square streaming-quantile algorithm — cheap, robust, and the literal embodiment of "self-dimensioning." (2) Adaptive / multi-resolution time-frequency: choosing window length per band or per frame from a concentration measure (Renyi entropy / PSD spread), constant-Q geometric band spacing, and reassignment/synchrosqueezing which lets the data relocate itself to its true coordinates with no fixed grid. (3) Perceptual normalization: K-weighting/LUFS (ITU-R BS.1770), mel/log-frequency warping, and dB compression so the axes match human hearing rather than raw DFT bins. (4) Salience / attention: Itti-Koch center-surround normalization N(.) and adaptive-whitening / median-thresholded spectral flux to decide which structure to brighten. The most "breathtaking + insightful" combination for a Rust/egui app is a reassigned constant-Q spectrogram with percentile auto-ranged perceptual colormap and a salience-modulated brightness channel — every one of those four words is a self-dimensioning operator.

techniques.name: Percentile auto-ranging colormap via streaming quantiles (P-square)
techniques.one_line: Set the color/intensity limits to running low/high percentiles of the live magnitude distribution instead of fixed dB floors, estimated online with O(1) memory.
techniques.the_math: Map each pixel value v to color t in [0,1] by t = clamp((v - q_lo)/(q_hi - q_lo), 0, 1), where q_lo = Q(p_lo) and q_hi = Q(p_hi) are e.g. the 2nd and 98th percentiles of the magnitude (or dB) histogram of the current spectrogram window. Estimate Q(p) online with the Jain-Chlamtac P-square algorithm: maintain 5 markers (positions n_i and heights q_i) per tracked quantile; on each new sample x increment marker counts, compute desired positions n'_i = 1 + 2p(N-1) for the p-marker, and when a marker drifts >=1 from its desired position adjust its height by the piecewise-parabolic (P2) predictor q_i += (d/(n_{i+1}-n_{i-1}))[(n_i-n_{i-1}+d)(q_{i+1}-q_i)/(n_{i+1}-n_i) + (n_{i+1}-n_i-d)(q_i-q_{i-1})/(n_i-n_{i-1})], falling back to linear if that breaks monotonicity. Per quantile this is O(1) time and O(1) memory, no sample storage.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals faint structure that a fixed -80 dB floor either clips to black or washes out: reverb tails, room tone, breath noise, the harmonic 'skirt' around partials. Because the limits track the data, a whisper and a wall of distortion both fill the colormap's full perceptual range — the display always shows maximum visible contrast for whatever is actually present.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The image stays vivid through huge loudness swings without the user touching a gain slider — quiet passages bloom into detail, loud passages don't smear to a solid block. It looks 'alive' and self-correcting, like the picture is breathing with the music.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This IS autonomous dimensioning of the value axis: the color limits are continuously recomputed from the signal's own distribution (percentiles), so the dynamic-range mapping self-scales to content with zero manual calibration. Smoothing the q_lo/q_hi over time with a one-pole filter prevents flicker.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivial in Rust: a 5-marker P-square struct per quantile, fed the per-frame magnitude bins. ~50 lines, no allocation in the hot loop. Apply EMA to the two limits (tau ~ 0.3-1 s) to avoid jitter. Gotcha: do percentiles in dB domain, not linear, or q_lo collapses to ~0 and the stretch is dominated by noise floor. Combine with a hard absolute floor only as a safety clamp.

references.title: R. Jain & I. Chlamtac, The P-square algorithm for dynamic calculation of quantiles and histograms without storing observations (CACM 1985)
HIPR2: Contrast Stretching (percentile-based point operation)
Online Percentile Estimation (extended P-square)

techniques.name: Automatic Gain Control with asymmetric attack/release one-pole ballistics
techniques.one_line: Drive overall display brightness/scale toward a target perceptual level via a smoothed level estimate with fast attack, slow release — the same loop a compressor or VU meter uses.
techniques.the_math: Track a smoothed level L[n] from instantaneous energy e[n]=x[n]^2 (or per-band power). Use a one-pole filter with coefficient depending on direction: L[n] = a*L[n-1] + (1-a)*e[n], where a = exp(-1/(tau*fs)). Asymmetric ballistics: tau = tau_attack when e[n] > L[n-1] (e.g. 1-50 ms), tau = tau_release otherwise (e.g. 100-1000 ms, ~10x attack). Time constant <-> 63.2% step-response time. Gain steering toward target T (in dB or linear): g[n] = T / (sqrt(L[n]) + eps), optionally rate-limited so |20log10(g[n]/g[n-1])| <= R dB/frame. For perceptual targets use K-weighted energy (ITU-R BS.1770): e_K[n] from the BS.1770 two-stage shelf+highpass filter, mean-squared, then loudness LK = -0.691 + 10*log10(mean(e_K)). For energy/power domain remember the effective averaging time is ~2*tau in the linear-amplitude domain.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Makes relative dynamics legible: because the AGC removes the slow loudness trend, what remains visible is the *shape* of the dynamics — accents, transients, micro-crescendos — rather than the absolute level. You see the gesture, not the volume knob.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The classic 'pumping/breathing' aesthetic when release is slow: the whole display swells and settles with the groove, which is hypnotic and musical. Fast attack keeps transients crisp; slow release keeps the motion smooth and organic.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-scales the amplitude/brightness axis to keep the display centered on a target regardless of input level. The attack/release asymmetry is itself a content-adaptive choice: the loop reacts instantly to onsets but ignores brief dips, matching how loudness should be tracked.
techniques.implementation_notes: Almost free: two multiplies per sample (or per frame for block AGC). In Rust, precompute a_attack/a_release; branch on direction. Gotchas: (1) attack too fast on the brightness channel causes flicker — keep display AGC slower than audio AGC; (2) avoid divide-by-zero with eps and a gain ceiling; (3) for true loudness use BS.1770 K-weighting (two biquads) not raw RMS. K-weighting biquads are well-specified and cheap.

references.title: Automatic gain control — Wikipedia (attack/release, pumping/breathing)
Recommendation ITU-R BS.1770-5 (K-weighting, gating, mean-square loudness)
EMA filter time constant alpha = exp(-1/(tau*fs)) (mbedded.ninja)

techniques.name: Adaptive multi-resolution STFT (per-band / per-frame window length from a concentration measure)
techniques.one_line: Choose the analysis window length locally to maximize time-frequency concentration — long windows where the signal is tonal, short windows where it's transient.
techniques.the_math: Compute several STFTs S_w(t,f) with window lengths w in a set {N_1..N_K}. For each (t,f) region pick the window that maximizes energy concentration, measured by the (normalized) Renyi entropy of the local TF density: R_alpha(S) = (1/(1-alpha)) * log2( sum_{t,f} (|S(t,f)|^2 / sum|S|^2)^alpha ), alpha typically 3; lower entropy = more concentrated = better window. Select w*(region) = argmin_w R_alpha(S_w | region), then stitch a composite spectrogram from the best regions. A cheaper global variant (Nisar et al.): compute PSD, its mean mu and std sigma; if sigma > beta (~1500) the signal is wideband -> use CQT-style short windows, else narrowband -> window length W = 3*B_s*F_s/mu where B_s is the window main-lobe factor (4 for Hamming) and mu the spectral centroid. Constant-Q is the structured limit: center freqs f_k = f_0 * 2^(k/B) (B bins/octave), window length N_k = Q*F_s/f_k with quality factor Q = 1/(2^(1/B) - 1), giving constant resolution in octaves.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Resolves the fundamental Gabor/Heisenberg tradeoff *adaptively*: you simultaneously see sharp vertical transients (drum hits, consonants) and sharp horizontal tones (sustained notes, vowels, bass) in one image, which a single fixed FFT size can never do. CQT specifically makes musical intervals equal-spaced — octaves become uniform vertical distances, so harmony is geometrically visible.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Edges everywhere look crisp — the spectrogram stops being a blurry smear and gains an almost photographic sharpness, with transients as clean knife-edges and tones as clean filaments in the same frame.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The textbook case of self-dimensioning the *resolution* axis: window length (hence time vs frequency resolution) is chosen by the signal's own local concentration, region by region. CQT auto-dimensions the frequency axis to a perceptual log/octave grid.
techniques.implementation_notes: Multi-window then entropy-pick is K full STFTs per frame — heavy but parallelizable; with rustfft, K in {3,4} of sizes {256,1024,4096} is realtime on a few cores. CQT can be precomputed as a sparse kernel matrix (Brown-Puckette) and applied as one sparse matmul per hop — very efficient and the recommended path for a music app. Gotcha: stitching multi-window maps needs consistent magnitude normalization across window lengths (divide by sqrt(N) or by window sum) or seams appear.

references.title: Nisar et al., An Efficient Adaptive Window Size Selection Method for Improving Spectrogram Visualization (Comput. Intell. Neurosci. 2016)
Bridging the gap between STFT, wavelets, the constant-Q transform and multi-resolution STFT (SIViP 2020)
Learnable Adaptive Time-Frequency Representation via Differentiable STFT (arXiv 2025)

techniques.name: Time-frequency reassignment / synchrosqueezing (self-relocating energy)
techniques.one_line: Move each spectrogram value from its bin center to the signal's true local time-frequency coordinate, computed from the STFT phase gradient — a grid-free, self-sharpening display.
techniques.the_math: Compute three STFTs of x with windows h, T_h(t)=t*h(t), and D_h(t)=h'(t): X, X_Th, X_Dh. Reassigned coordinates (Auger-Flandrin efficient form): t_hat(t,w) = t + Re{ X_Th(t,w) * conj(X(t,w)) / |X(t,w)|^2 } (note sign convention), w_hat(t,w) = w - Im{ X_Dh(t,w) * conj(X(t,w)) / |X(t,w)|^2 }. Equivalently t_hat = t - d(phase)/dw, w_hat = w + d(phase)/dt. Deposit energy |X(t,w)|^2 at (t_hat, w_hat) instead of (t,w). Synchrosqueezing is the invertible variant that reassigns only in frequency: w_hat(t,w) = Im{ (d/dt) Wf(t,w) / Wf(t,w) } (instantaneous frequency), then squeezes coefficients onto the w_hat curve, allowing signal reconstruction.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Turns blurry ridges into hairline curves at the exact instantaneous frequency. You can read precise pitch glides, vibrato depth/rate, formant trajectories, and closely-spaced partials that overlap in an ordinary spectrogram. Synchrosqueezing additionally separates and lets you resynthesize individual components.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The signature 'wow': a fuzzy spectrogram snaps into crisp filaments, like pulling a photo into focus. Chirps and vibrato render as razor-thin glowing threads on black — extremely cinematic.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Maximally autonomous: there is no fixed display grid at all — every energy quantum reports its own true coordinate, so the representation dimensions itself to the signal's instantaneous structure rather than to chosen bin sizes. Resolution is effectively set by the data, not the window.
techniques.implementation_notes: Three FFTs per frame (rustfft handles this), then a scatter/accumulate into the output buffer at (t_hat,w_hat) — the scatter is the only non-trivial part; round to nearest output pixel or splat with a small kernel. Realtime-feasible at moderate FFT sizes. Gotchas: (1) reassignment is noisy in low-energy regions — mask where |X|^2 below an adaptive floor (ties to percentile auto-range); (2) sign conventions differ across references — validate on a known chirp; (3) plain reassignment isn't invertible (use synchrosqueezing if you need resynthesis).

references.title: Reassignment method — Wikipedia (Auger-Flandrin efficient equations)
Daubechies, Lu, Wu, Synchrosqueezed wavelet transforms (arXiv 1105.0010)
A Fast and Robust Spectrogram Reassignment Method (Mathematics 2019)

techniques.name: Salience-driven emphasis via Itti-Koch center-surround normalization
techniques.one_line: Compute an auditory/visual saliency map from center-surround feature contrasts and use it to modulate brightness/opacity, so the display auto-emphasizes what stands out from its surround.
techniques.the_math: Build a multi-scale pyramid of the spectrogram (and derived feature maps: intensity, spectral contrast, temporal contrast/flux, pitch-strength). Center-surround feature map: F(c,s) = | M_c (-) M_s |, the across-scale difference (interpolate fine scale c to coarse scale s and subtract) — this is a Difference-of-Gaussians band-pass. Normalize each map with the operator N(.): (1) rescale values to a fixed range [0,M]; (2) find the global max M and the mean m_bar of all *other* local maxima; (3) multiply the whole map by (M - m_bar)^2. This globally promotes maps with one dominant peak and suppresses maps with many comparable peaks. Combine: S = (1/n) * sum_k N(F_k). For audio (Kayser/Kaya-Elhilali) the same machinery runs over rate-scale-frequency feature maps; saliency = how much an event differs from its temporal/spectral surround.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals *what the ear would notice*: the model brightens events that pop out against their background (a sudden onset in silence, a lone high partial, a timbral change) and dims steady texture. It surfaces structure that is statistically distinctive rather than merely loud — a quiet but novel sound can outshine a loud steady drone.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Produces a display with depth and intentionality — salient moments glow while background recedes, so the picture has natural focus/attention, like the visualization is 'listening' and pointing at the interesting bits.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-dimensions the *emphasis/attention* channel: the (M - m_bar)^2 normalization adaptively weights each feature by how peaky it is right now, so the mix of cues that drives brightness re-balances itself to the content frame by frame.
techniques.implementation_notes: Pyramids + DoG + the N(.) normalization are cheap convolutions; fully realtime on CPU for moderate maps, ideal on GPU (wgpu) as mipmap + subtract passes. Gotcha: full Itti uses iterative DoG normalization (10 iterations) which is heavier — the single (M-m_bar)^2 operator is a good realtime approximation. Temporal feature maps need a short history buffer. Tune the feature set to audio (intensity, spectral flux, harmonicity) rather than color/orientation.

references.title: Itti, Koch, Niebur, A Model of Saliency-Based Visual Attention (normalization operator N(.))
Kayser et al., Mechanisms for allocating auditory attention: an auditory saliency map (PubMed)
Kaya & Elhilali, A temporal saliency map for modeling auditory attention (CISS 2012)

techniques.name: Adaptive whitening + median-adaptive thresholding (per-bin self-equalization and self-tuning detection)
techniques.one_line: Normalize each frequency bin by its own recent peak so every band gets equal display dynamic range, and gate events with a local-median adaptive threshold.
techniques.the_math: Adaptive whitening (Stowell-Plumbley): keep a per-bin running peak P[n,k] = max(|X[n,k]|, r, m*P[n-1,k]) where m in (0,1) is a memory/decay factor (e.g. 0.9970 for 1 s at ~344 fps) and r a noise floor; whitened spectrum X_w[n,k] = X[n,k] / P[n,k]. Spectral-flux novelty: SF[n] = sum_k H(|X_w[n,k]| - |X_w[n-1,k]|), H(x)=max(x,0) (half-wave rectify, positive changes only). Adaptive peak-picking threshold: an onset at n requires SF[n] = max over [n-w, n+w], SF[n] >= mean over [n-m_a, n+m_a] + delta, and SF[n] >= lambda * median over [n-m_b, n+m_b] + delta. The median multiplier lambda makes the threshold robust and self-tuning to local activity.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Whitening exposes high-frequency and low-energy detail that a raw magnitude display buries under the bass-heavy 1/f spectral tilt — cymbals, sibilance, and upper partials become as visible as the kick drum. The adaptive threshold cleanly marks onsets/beats regardless of whether the passage is loud or soft.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The spectrogram suddenly shows a full, balanced spread of energy across the whole frequency range instead of a bright bass blob fading to black on top — it looks richer and more textured, and overlaid onset markers pulse exactly with the rhythm.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Two independent self-dimensioning loops: (1) per-bin whitening auto-scales the *frequency axis gain* so each band fills its dynamic range; (2) the median/mean adaptive threshold auto-scales the *detection sensitivity* to local context. Both adapt continuously with no global knob.
techniques.implementation_notes: Both are cheap streaming ops: one max+multiply per bin per frame for whitening, a sliding window stats buffer for thresholding. Pure Rust, no GPU needed. Gotcha: whitening amplifies noise in silent bins — the floor r and decay m must be tuned (too aggressive m -> flicker, too slow -> sluggish). The three-criterion peak-picker needs a short look-ahead (w frames), so it's near-realtime with a small latency.

references.title: Stowell & Plumbley, Adaptive whitening for improved real-time audio onset detection (ICMC 2007)
Bock et al., Evaluating the online capabilities of onset detection methods (ISMIR 2012)
Spectral flux onset novelty + median adaptive threshold (ResearchGate diagram)

techniques.name: Perceptual axis warping: mel/log-frequency + dB/loudness compression as a self-normalizing transform
techniques.one_line: Remap the frequency and amplitude axes onto perceptual scales (mel/log Hz, dB / loudness exponent) so the display's geometry matches hearing rather than the DFT.
techniques.the_math: Frequency: mel m = 2595*log10(1 + f/700) (or log2 octaves), so equal vertical pixels = equal perceptual pitch steps; bin energies pooled by a triangular mel filterbank H_k(f). Amplitude: dB compression D = 20*log10(|X|/X_ref) implements the Weber-Fechner log law (JND proportional to level). Optional Stevens power-law loudness: perceived = k*I^0.3 (linear domain) ~ sone scaling. Perceptual weighting toward equal-loudness: multiply bins by ISO 226 equal-loudness-contour weights, or use ITU-R BS.1770 K-weighting (high-shelf +4 dB above ~1.7 kHz cascaded with a ~38 Hz highpass) before display. Per-file/global mean-variance normalization (z-score) of the log-mel then standardizes the color mapping.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Puts musically/linguistically meaningful structure where the eye expects it: octaves equally spaced, formants and harmonics aligned to pitch, and dB compression unfolds the 100+ dB range into a band the eye can actually resolve. It reveals fine low-level detail (reverb, noise) that linear amplitude hides, and matches what is actually *audible* (K-weighting downweights inaudible sub-bass/ultra-treble).
techniques.why_breathtaking: The image becomes legible and 'correct' to a musician — harmonic stacks march up in even rows, melodies are straight ladders, and the brightness corresponds to loudness you'd actually hear, which makes the visualization feel honest and immediately readable.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-dimensions both axes to a perceptual coordinate system that is invariant to the raw sample rate / FFT size: the same content always lands in the same perceptual place. dB + z-score normalization auto-centers the color mapping on the data's statistics.
techniques.implementation_notes: Cheapest of all: mel filterbank is a fixed sparse matrix multiply per frame (precompute once); dB and power-law are pointwise. K-weighting is two biquads on the input. All trivially realtime in Rust. Gotcha: log/dB needs a floor (e.g. clamp to -80 dB or add eps) to avoid log(0); mel pooling loses fine pitch resolution at high freq (acceptable for display, combine with CQT if precise partials matter). Best used as the substrate under the percentile auto-range and salience layers.

ITU-R BS.1770-5 K-weighting (perceptual pre-display weighting)
Equal-loudness contour (ISO 226) discussion — librosa issue #463

key_references.title: R. Jain & I. Chlamtac, The P-square algorithm for dynamic calculation of quantiles without storing observations (CACM 28(10), 1985)
key_references.why: The streaming-quantile engine behind percentile auto-ranging colormaps — O(1) memory makes it perfect for a realtime egui display that re-dimensions its color limits every frame.

key_references.why: Gives the exact, implementable t_hat/omega_hat equations for the single most breathtaking + insightful self-dimensioning representation (grid-free sharpened spectrogram).
