key_references.why: Concrete, cheap formula (W = 3*B_s*F_s/mu, sigma>beta switch to CQT) for choosing window length from signal statistics — the core of autonomous time-frequency resolution.

key_references.title: Itti, Koch, Niebur, A Model of Saliency-Based Visual Attention (Vision Research 1998/2000)
key_references.why: Defines the center-surround feature maps and the (M - m_bar)^2 normalization operator N(.) that lets the display adaptively decide what to emphasize frame by frame.

key_references.title: Stowell & Plumbley, Adaptive whitening for improved real-time audio onset detection (ICMC 2007)
key_references.why: Per-bin running-peak normalization is a dead-simple, realtime self-equalizer that flattens spectral tilt so every frequency band fills its display range.

key_references.title: Recommendation ITU-R BS.1770-5 (loudness, K-weighting, gating)
key_references.why: The standard perceptual energy weighting and gated mean-square loudness — the principled target signal for a perceptual AGC / brightness loop.

cross_links: Perceptual axis warping (mel/log/dB/K-weighting) is the substrate that the FFT/STFT-spectrogram facet should render onto — share the filterbank and dB-compression code.

Percentile auto-ranging and AGC ballistics belong on top of ANY 2D representation (spectrogram, scalogram, reassigned map, CQT) — they are display-layer operators independent of the transform that produces the values.

Reassignment/synchrosqueezing and constant-Q overlap heavily with the wavelet / time-frequency-transform facet; this facet contributes the *adaptive selection* logic (entropy-based window choice) rather than the transforms themselves.
Salience/center-surround normalization connects to any color/brightness-mapping facet — it supplies a per-pixel emphasis weight that multiplies into the final color.

One-pole asymmetric smoothing (alpha = exp(-1/(tau*fs))) is the shared self-tuning temporal filter used by AGC, percentile-limit smoothing, peak-following meters, and onset thresholds — a single reusable primitive across facets.
Adaptive-whitening per-bin normalization and the K-weighting front-end both feed loudness/level facets and the onset/beat-detection facet (spectral flux).

facet: Information-theoretic & insight-generation: spectral descriptors, novelty/flux, entropy, structure & anomaly surfacing, and the cognitive science of what makes a sound visualization reveal a problem at a glance.
summary: This facet treats the spectrogram not as something to look pretty, but as a probability distribution to be characterized and a field to be differentiated. The strongest ideas reduce a high-dimensional STFT frame to a handful of "shape" scalars (centroid = brightness, flatness/entropy = noisiness vs. tonality, rolloff = high-frequency edge) whose time-series overlays make tonal/noise transitions, harmonic clashes, and timbral drift visible as line movement you literally cannot hear apart. Novelty/flux functions (spectral flux, complex-domain, KL/Itakura-Saito divergence, and Foote's self-similarity checkerboard) differentiate the spectrogram in time to surface onsets and structural boundaries — turning "where does the section change?" into a peak-picking problem. The deepest insight payoff comes from pairing these descriptors with the cognitive science of preattentive features (Healey/Ware), Cleveland-McGill's encoding-accuracy hierarchy, and Tufte's data-ink discipline: a visualization is "insightful" precisely when the problem (phase cancellation, dynamic-range collapse, harmonic clash) maps to a preattentive channel (a goniometer line collapsing to horizontal, a crest factor flattening, an entropy spike) detectable in under 200 ms without serial search. Autonomous dimensioning is natural here because every descriptor is already a normalized, content-relative ratio — flatness is dimensionless 0..1, entropy auto-normalizes by log K, and novelty thresholds adapt via local-average subtraction.

techniques.name: Spectral entropy field (Shannon entropy of the normalized power spectrum, per-frame)
techniques.one_line: Collapse each STFT frame to one number measuring how 'spread' vs. 'peaky' its energy is — a tonality/noisiness meter that auto-normalizes to [0,1].
techniques.the_math: For frame n with power spectrum S_n(k)=|X(n,k)|^2, form a probability distribution p_n(k)=S_n(k)/Σ_j S_n(j). Shannon entropy H(n) = -Σ_{k} p_n(k) log2 p_n(k). Normalize by the max attainable entropy log2(K) over K bins: H̃(n)=H(n)/log2(K) ∈ [0,1]. A pure tone → p concentrated on one bin → H̃→0; white noise → uniform p → H̃→1. Render H̃(n) as a color-mapped strip under the spectrogram or as an overlaid line. Sub-band variant: split into B bands, compute per-band entropy for a 2D entropy 'texture map'.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Tonal-vs-noise character and its time evolution — invisible to the ear when masked by loud tonal content. A dynamic-range/over-compression problem shows as entropy rising toward 1 and flattening (everything becomes noise-like); a feedback/resonance ring shows as a sharp entropy DROP (energy collapsing into one bin). Reveals 'spectral monotony' (a mix where every frame looks the same) at a glance.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A single scalar field rippling beneath the spectrogram reads like a heat signature of the sound's 'organization'; the [0,1] normalization makes it a clean, glowing gauge that the eye locks onto instantly.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-scaling is intrinsic: dividing by log2(K) makes the range exactly [0,1] regardless of FFT size, sample rate, or content. No manual gain/range knob ever needed — the axis IS the information bound.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivial on top of an existing rustfft magnitude spectrogram: O(K) per frame after the FFT. Add a tiny epsilon (e.g. 1e-12) before log to avoid -inf; skip zero-probability bins (0·log0=0). Realtime-trivial even at 60fps; the only cost is the normalization sum. Color-map via a perceptually uniform LUT (viridis/magma) in egui or a wgpu fragment shader.

references.title: Spectral entropy — power spectral entropy as an information-theoretic correlate (JASA)
Spectral Entropy overview — ScienceDirect Topics
OpenAE standards: Spectral entropy

techniques.name: Spectral flatness (Wiener entropy / tonality coefficient) overlay
techniques.one_line: Ratio of geometric to arithmetic mean of the spectrum — a dimensionless 0..1 'how white-noise-like is this?' meter, the exponential cousin of spectral entropy.
techniques.the_math: For frame power spectrum S(k), k=1..K: SFM = (geometric mean)/(arithmetic mean) = exp((1/K)Σ_k ln S(k)) / ((1/K)Σ_k S(k)). Equivalently SFM = (Π_k S(k))^{1/K} / ((1/K)Σ_k S(k)). Range (0,1]: 1 ⇒ perfectly flat (white noise), →0 ⇒ tonal/peaky. Often reported in dB: SFM_dB = 10·log10(SFM), max 0 dB, min −∞. Note the geometric mean = exp(mean of log) is exactly the entropy of the LOG spectrum, so flatness and entropy are duals; flatness is sub-banded (per critical band) to get a tonality-vs-frequency profile.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Hiss, noise floor, breathiness, and de-tuned/clustered harmonics that read as 'noise.' Per-band flatness exposes a specific harmonic clash (two near-but-not-equal partials beating) as a localized flatness bump in one band while the rest stays tonal — something an entropy-of-whole-frame number averages away. Mastering use: rising broadband flatness = the loudness-war 'wall of noise.'
techniques.why_breathtaking: As a stacked per-band ribbon it paints a 'tonality spectrogram' — clean tonal regions glow dark/saturated, noisy regions wash to white, giving an instantly legible map of where the sound is 'clean' vs 'gritty.'
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Geometric/arithmetic ratio is scale-invariant by construction (multiply the whole spectrum by any gain and SFM is unchanged) — it auto-normalizes against level, so no gain calibration is ever required; the meter means the same thing for a whisper and a kick.
techniques.implementation_notes: One pass: accumulate Σ ln S(k) and Σ S(k). Use log-domain accumulation to avoid the underflow you'd get from an actual Π of thousands of small numbers. Guard S(k)=0 with epsilon. MPEG-7 standardizes this as AudioSpectralFlatness, so it's a known-good, citable descriptor. O(K)/frame, realtime-trivial.

references.title: Spectral flatness — Wikipedia
Spectral flatness — HandWiki (formula + dB)
Spectral Flatness overview — ScienceDirect

techniques.name: Spectral 'shape' descriptor bundle: centroid, spread/bandwidth, skewness, rolloff
techniques.one_line: Treat the spectrum as a 1-D mass distribution and read its statistical moments — centroid (brightness), spread, skew, and rolloff edge — as four overlaid lines that summarize timbre evolution.
techniques.the_math: Treat normalized magnitude p(k)=|X(k)|/Σ_j|X(j)| with bin center frequency f(k). 1st moment (centroid, brightness): μ = Σ_k f(k)·p(k). 2nd central moment (spread/bandwidth): σ = sqrt(Σ_k (f(k)-μ)^2 p(k)). 3rd standardized moment (skewness): γ = Σ_k (f(k)-μ)^3 p(k) / σ^3 (asymmetry of energy). Rolloff R_κ: smallest frequency such that Σ_{f(k)≤R_κ} |X(k)| ≥ κ·Σ_k |X(k)|, with κ=0.85 or 0.95 (the high-frequency 'edge'). These give a compact 4-number 'timbre fingerprint' per frame.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Brightness drift, timbral 'thinning' or 'darkening' across a take, and EQ problems: a centroid that drifts upward over a mix = building harshness; a rolloff ceiling that suddenly drops = a low-pass/bandwidth loss (e.g., a codec or a clogged mic). Spread distinguishes a focused tone from a smeared one. Skewness flags energy imbalance (boomy low-heavy vs thin high-heavy).
techniques.why_breathtaking: Four smooth, color-coded curves gliding over the spectrogram act like the sound's vital-signs trace; the centroid line especially reads as the spectrogram's 'horizon,' and watching it rise and fall is hypnotic and immediately meaningful.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: All moments are computed in physical Hz against the actual sample-rate-derived bin grid, so axes self-fit to Nyquist automatically; rolloff is a content-relative percentile, so it adapts its absolute Hz value to whatever the material actually contains rather than to a fixed threshold.
techniques.implementation_notes: All O(K)/frame, shares the same magnitude buffer as entropy/flatness — compute them in one fused loop. Centroid/spread are numerically robust; skewness needs σ>epsilon guard. These are standard MIR features (MATLAB spectralCentroid etc.), so behavior is well-validated. Realtime-trivial; the only design work is choosing a shared, legible overlay color scheme.

references.title: Spectral Descriptors — MATLAB (centroid, spread, skewness, rolloff, flatness formulas)
Spectral Centroid overview — ScienceDirect

techniques.name: Spectral-flux novelty function with log-compression and adaptive local-average thresholding
techniques.one_line: Differentiate the (log-compressed) spectrogram in time and half-wave rectify — a 1-D 'novelty' curve whose peaks are onsets and section changes.
techniques.the_math: Compress: Y(n,k)=log(1+γ·|X(n,k)|), γ>0 (γ≈1–1000 boosts low-energy detail). Discrete time derivative, keep only increases (half-wave rectify |x|_{≥0}=max(x,0)): Δ(n)=Σ_{k=0}^{K-1} |Y(n+1,k)−Y(n,k)|_{≥0}. Adaptive normalization via local average μ(n)=(1/(2M+1))Σ_{m=−M}^{M} Δ(n+m), enhanced novelty Δ̄(n)=|Δ(n)−μ(n)|_{≥0}. Peaks of Δ̄ are onset/change candidates. (Plain L2 flux variant: SF(n)=sqrt(Σ_k max(|X(n,k)|−|X(n−1,k)|,0)^2).)
techniques.what_it_reveals: Rhythmic structure and transients you'd miss in a static spectrogram: every drum hit, note attack, and edit point becomes a spike. The log compression is the magic — it equalizes loud and quiet events so a soft hi-hat onset is as visible as a kick, surfacing rhythmic detail buried under dynamics. Reveals timing problems (sloppy/early onsets) as misaligned spikes against a grid.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A crisp, spiky waveform that beats in time with the music; aligned under the spectrogram it visually 'pulses,' and the local-average baseline crawling beneath the spikes is elegant.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The local-average subtraction Δ̄ IS autonomous thresholding: the detection floor self-adapts to local energy, so a quiet passage and a loud chorus both yield clean, comparably-sized peaks without a global threshold knob. γ can also be auto-tuned from the signal's dynamic range.
techniques.implementation_notes: O(K)/frame for the diff plus a cheap sliding-window mean (use a running sum, O(1) update). Needs the previous frame buffered. Peak-picking adds a small windowed local-max + threshold step. This is THE canonical, heavily-validated MIR novelty pipeline (ISMIR tutorial). Realtime-trivial; the main tuning is γ and window M.

references.title: A Basic Tutorial on Novelty and Activation Functions (TISMIR)
FMP Notebooks — Novelty (spectral flux), AudioLabs Erlangen

techniques.name: Complex-domain novelty (combined magnitude + phase prediction error)
techniques.one_line: Predict each bin's next complex value assuming steady-state (constant magnitude, constant phase-advance) and sum the prediction error — catches soft tonal onsets that energy-only flux misses.
techniques.the_math: From frame n−1 predict the steady-state target: X_S(n,k) = |X(n−1,k)| · exp( j·( φ(n−1,k) + Δφ(n−1,k) ) ), where the phase advance Δφ(n−1,k)=princarg(φ(n−1,k)−φ(n−2,k)) (principal-argument-wrapped to (−π,π]). Novelty = total complex deviation: CD(n) = Σ_k | X(n,k) − X_S(n,k) |. Large where either magnitude jumps (percussive onset) OR phase breaks its linear trend (pitched/tonal onset). Bello & Duxbury, DAFx 2003.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Onsets of soft, pitched notes (legato strings, bowed attacks) that produce little energy change but a clear phase discontinuity — invisible to pure spectral flux. It surfaces the 'gentle' note starts and is far better at separating two close notes. Also exposes phase incoherence/smearing as elevated baseline CD.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Encodes both 'how loud did it get?' and 'did the oscillation break its rhythm?' into one curve — a richer, more musical novelty trace that responds to subtleties the magnitude-only version is deaf to.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The steady-state predictor is itself adaptive: the expected value is recomputed per-bin per-frame from the signal's own recent trajectory, so the 'baseline of no-change' continuously self-calibrates to the current tempo of spectral evolution rather than to any fixed reference.
techniques.implementation_notes: Needs complex STFT (rustfft gives this directly) plus 2 frames of phase history. princarg wrapping = atan2(sin θ, cos θ) or a modulo trick. O(K)/frame, a few extra trig calls per bin — still realtime at 60fps for K≤4096. Slightly more state than spectral flux but a strict insight upgrade for tonal material.

references.title: Bello & Duxbury et al., Complex-domain onset detection (DAFx 2003 PDF)
On the use of phase and energy for musical onset detection in the complex domain (Semantic Scholar)

techniques.name: Foote self-similarity matrix + checkerboard-kernel novelty (structure surfacing)
techniques.one_line: Build an N×N frame-vs-frame similarity image, then correlate a Gaussian-tapered checkerboard along its diagonal to find where the music changes section.
techniques.the_math: Feature vectors v_n (MFCC/chroma/spectral). Self-similarity matrix S(n,m)=cos(v_n,v_m)=(v_n·v_m)/(||v_n||·||v_m||) ∈ [−1,1] (or [0,1] for non-negative features). Checkerboard kernel of half-width L: sign pattern C(l,m)=sign(l)·sign(m) for l,m ∈ [−L,L], radially Gaussian-tapered: K(l,m)=sign(l)·sign(m)·exp(−(l^2+m^2)/(2σ^2)) with σ≈L/2. Novelty by correlating the kernel along the diagonal: Δ(n)=Σ_{l=−L}^{L} Σ_{m=−L}^{L} K(l,m)·S(n+l, n+m). Peaks ⇒ boundaries between homogeneous blocks (a verse/chorus edge sits at the corner where four checkerboard quadrants meet).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Global song STRUCTURE — verse/chorus/bridge boundaries, repetitions (off-diagonal stripes = repeated sections), and rhythmic periodicity — none of which a moment-to-moment spectrogram conveys. It is the macro-scale 'map' of a piece: you see the architecture, spot a missing repeat, or catch an arrangement that never develops (a uniform, structureless SSM = a 'flat' track).
techniques.why_breathtaking: The SSM itself is one of the most striking images in all of audio: glowing diagonal blocks and ghostly off-diagonal echoes form a self-portrait of the music's form — genuinely gallery-worthy, and the novelty curve riding its diagonal is a beautiful derived layer.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Kernel half-width L sets the time-scale of structure detected — and can be swept to produce a multi-scale novelty (small L = beats, large L = sections), letting the visualization auto-select the structural resolution that yields the cleanest peaks. The matrix axes self-fit to track length N.
techniques.implementation_notes: S is O(N^2) memory/compute — fine for offline/whole-file (a 4-min track at 2 fps frames ≈ 480×480), borderline for long realtime streaming (use a banded/Toeplitz windowed SSM around the diagonal to bound cost). The diagonal correlation is only O(N·L^2). GPU-friendly: render S as a texture in wgpu, compute the kernel correlation in a compute/fragment shader. Best as an 'analysis view' rather than per-frame realtime.

references.title: Foote, Automatic Audio Segmentation Using a Measure of Audio Novelty (CCRMA PDF)
FMP — Novelty-based segmentation, checkerboard kernel (AudioLabs)
Self-similarity matrix — Wikipedia

techniques.name: Information-divergence frame distance (KL / Itakura-Saito) as a spectral-change meter
techniques.one_line: Measure the statistical distance between consecutive spectral 'distributions' with KL or Itakura-Saito divergence — an information-theoretic novelty that weights changes the way perception does.
techniques.the_math: Treat normalized frame spectra as distributions p_n, p_{n-1}. Symmetric KL (Jeffreys): D(n)=Σ_k [ p_n(k) log(p_n(k)/p_{n-1}(k)) + p_{n-1}(k) log(p_{n-1}(k)/p_n(k)) ]. Itakura-Saito (on power spectra S, scale-invariant, perceptually motivated for speech): D_IS(S_n||S_{n-1}) = Σ_k [ S_n(k)/S_{n-1}(k) − log(S_n(k)/S_{n-1}(k)) − 1 ]. KL-divergence rate corresponds to Itakura-Saito up to a constant. Peaks of D(n) = spectral change points; the running D(n) is a perceptually-weighted novelty curve.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Change that matters perceptually: IS divergence penalizes a ratio mismatch the same in dB regardless of absolute level, so it flags a quiet-but-significant timbral shift that an L2 flux (dominated by loud bins) would miss. Surfaces the moment a sound's spectral 'fingerprint' genuinely changes vs. mere level change — ideal for catching dropouts, codec artifacts, and identity shifts.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Less of a showpiece on its own (it's a 1-D curve) but conceptually elegant: it visualizes 'how surprised are we, in bits, by this frame given the last?' — surprise itself rendered as a line.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Itakura-Saito's scale invariance (D_IS(λS||λS')=D_IS(S||S')) makes it auto-normalize to level — the meter is calibrated in information units, not amplitude, so it needs no gain reference and adapts its sensitivity to relative rather than absolute change.
techniques.implementation_notes: O(K)/frame; needs epsilon-flooring of the denominator/log (zero or near-zero bins explode the ratio) — clamp S to a noise floor first. KL needs both spectra normalized to sum 1; IS works on raw power. Numerically touchier than spectral flux (watch divide-by-zero and log of tiny numbers) but cheap and realtime. Best paired with smoothing.

references.title: KL & Rényi divergence rate for Gaussian ARMA processes (ScienceDirect)
Itakura-Saito divergence in information theory of speech (ResearchGate)

techniques.name: Goniometer / vectorscope + correlation meter (stereo phase-problem surfacing)
techniques.one_line: Plot the L/R sample pair as a Lissajous point cloud rotated 45°, with a running Pearson correlation bar — phase/mono-compatibility problems become a single glanceable shape.
techniques.the_math: Per sample pair (L,R), plot the rotated point: x = (L − R)/√2 (side/difference axis, horizontal), y = (L + R)/√2 (mid/sum axis, vertical). Mono (L=R) ⇒ all points on the vertical y-axis (thin vertical line). Out-of-polarity (L=−R) ⇒ thin horizontal line. Pan/width spreads the cloud. Correlation coefficient over a window: ρ = Σ(L_i R_i) / sqrt(Σ L_i^2 · Σ R_i^2) ∈ [−1,1] (zero-mean Pearson). ρ→+1 mono-compatible/in-phase, ρ→0 wide/decorrelated, ρ<0 phase-cancellation risk (mono sum will hollow out).
techniques.what_it_reveals: THE classic 'reveal a problem at a glance' tool: a stereo phase issue (which sounds fine on headphones but collapses to silence in mono) is invisible in a waveform or spectrogram but unmistakable as a goniometer cloud tilting horizontal or a correlation bar diving negative. Also surfaces excessive width, off-center balance, and a mono signal masquerading as stereo (a perfect vertical line).
techniques.why_breathtaking: The Lissajous cloud is mesmerizing — a living, breathing organic blob that pulses, leans, and rotates with the music; it's the single most iconic 'analog scope' visual and people watch it like a fire.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The √2 normalization fixes the geometry to a unit-diagonal frame regardless of level; the correlation meter is inherently dimensionless [−1,1]. Adaptive point-density/decay (fade old points) auto-tunes the cloud's visual mass to signal level so it neither saturates on loud passages nor vanishes on quiet ones.
techniques.implementation_notes: Dead simple: per-sample 2D scatter with alpha-fade trails. For 44.1k samples/frame at 60fps you decimate or accumulate into a 2D density buffer (a small histogram texture) and color-map it — much cleaner than raw points and GPU-trivial in wgpu. Correlation is a running sum (O(1) update). One of the highest insight-per-line-of-code wins available.

references.title: Goniometer — Sound on Sound glossary
Ziemer & Schmidt, Goniometers are a Powerful Acoustic Feature for MIR (arXiv)
jsGoniometer — GonioMeter/VectorScope/CorrelationMeter reference implementation

techniques.name: Dynamic-range / crest-factor field + loudness histogram (compression-damage surfacing)
techniques.one_line: Overlay peak vs RMS/LUFS envelopes and show the crest factor as a shrinking gap — the loudness-war 'flatlining' of dynamics made literally visible.
techniques.the_math: Sliding-window RMS: RMS(n)=sqrt((1/W)Σ_{i∈win} x_i^2). True/sample peak P(n)=max_{i∈win}|x_i|. Crest factor CF(n)=P(n)/RMS(n), in dB CF_dB=20·log10(P/RMS) — high CF = punchy/dynamic, low CF (→0 dB) = squashed. Perceptual loudness via ITU-R BS.1770 K-weighting: pre-filter (shelving + high-pass), then LUFS = −0.691 + 10·log10(Σ_channels G_c · meanSquare_c). Build a loudness histogram (distribution of short-term LUFS over the track); a narrow tall spike = dynamic-range collapse, a broad spread = healthy dynamics. PSR/PLR = peak minus short-term/integrated loudness.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Over-compression and clipping — the dynamic-range-collapse problem — at a glance: the peak and RMS lines converging until they kiss, and a loudness histogram that piles into a single narrow bin instead of spreading. Reveals clipping as a flat-topped waveform + true-peak overs, and exposes a master that 'sounds loud but lifeless' as a CF that never exceeds ~6 dB.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Less spectacular than a goniometer, but the convergence of two envelopes squeezing shut is a visceral, almost physical depiction of dynamics being crushed — and a glowing loudness histogram has real aesthetic appeal.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Histogram binning can auto-range to the observed loudness span; CF is a ratio (level-invariant). K-weighting is a fixed perceptual normalization, so the LUFS axis is content-absolute and comparable across tracks without manual calibration — exactly the kind of self-anchoring axis that makes cross-clip comparison meaningful.
techniques.implementation_notes: RMS/peak are O(W) sliding (running sum-of-squares = O(1) update). BS.1770 needs two biquad filters (K-weighting) + gating logic — well-specified, ~50 lines, citable standard. Histogram is a simple accumulation. All realtime-trivial; the LUFS gating (−70/−10 LUFS relative gate) is the only fiddly part. High-value, low-risk addition for a sound studio app.

references.title: 'Dynamic Range' & The Loudness War — Sound on Sound (crest factor explained)
LUFS vs RMS vs True Peak metering 101

techniques.name: Preattentive insight layer: encode each detected problem to a Cleveland-McGill-ranked channel with Tufte data-ink discipline
techniques.one_line: Not a transform but the meta-technique — map each information-theoretic descriptor to the most perceptually accurate, preattentively-detectable visual channel so problems pop in <200ms.
techniques.the_math: Cleveland-McGill accuracy ordering of encodings (use the highest-ranked channel that fits): position-on-common-scale > position-on-nonaligned-scale > length > angle/slope > area > volume > color-saturation/shading. Preattentive detection (Healey/Ware): targets defined by a single channel (hue, orientation, length, motion, flicker) are found in time independent of distractor count (search slope ≈ 0 ms/item, < ~200 ms), whereas conjunctions require serial search (slope rises with N). Tufte data-ink ratio = (ink encoding data)/(total ink); maximize → remove chartjunk. Design rule: encode the SCALAR you most need to monitor (e.g., correlation ρ, crest factor) on a POSITION axis (a moving line/dot on a common scale) — the most accurate channel — and reserve hue only for categorical state (ok/warning/clip), since hue is preattentive but low-accuracy for magnitude.
techniques.what_it_reveals: This is the lens that makes everything above 'insightful' rather than merely pretty: it explains WHY a phase problem reads instantly on a goniometer (orientation is preattentive) but would be missed in a numeric table (serial reading). It tells you to put a single red flash on a true-peak over (motion/flicker + hue = doubly preattentive) and to graph crest factor as a position-coded line (highest magnitude-accuracy), not as a color gradient.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Indirectly maximizes breathtaking-ness AND insight: a display engineered around preattentive pop-out and data-ink is both calmer (less chartjunk) and more arresting (the one thing that matters glows/moves while everything else recedes).
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Drives autonomous behavior: the system can auto-promote a descriptor to a more salient channel (e.g., escalate a quietly-drawn correlation line to a flashing alert) when its value crosses a learned/adaptive threshold — the visualization re-allocates its preattentive 'budget' to whatever is currently anomalous, dimensioning salience to the data's state.
techniques.implementation_notes: No DSP cost — it's a design/rendering policy enforced in egui: keep a small ruleset mapping {descriptor, severity} → {channel, salience}. Cheap to implement, huge payoff. The risk is over-alerting (too many preattentive cues compete and none pop); cap concurrent salient cues to ~1–2 (the whole point of preattentive coding collapses if many channels fire at once).

references.title: Graphical perception — Wikipedia (Cleveland-McGill hierarchy)
Perception and Visualization (Univ. of Iowa STAT4580 — preattentive features, Cleveland-McGill)
Graph Design: The Data-ink Ratio and Expert Users (Semantic Scholar PDF)

key_references.title: A Basic Tutorial on Novelty and Activation Functions for Music Signal Processing (TISMIR)
key_references.why: Authoritative, recent ISMIR-published derivation of the full spectral-flux novelty pipeline: log-compression Y=log(1+γ|X|), half-wave-rectified temporal difference, and adaptive local-average thresholding Δ̄(n). The single best source for the onset/novelty math in this facet.

key_references.title: FMP Notebooks — Novelty-based Segmentation (AudioLabs Erlangen)
key_references.why: Gives the exact Foote checkerboard-kernel math — sign(l)·sign(m) structure, Gaussian taper exp(−(l²+m²)/2σ²), and the diagonal correlation Δ(n)=ΣΣ K(l,m)S(n+l,n+m) — plus reference Python. Definitive for the self-similarity/structure technique.

key_references.title: Foote, Automatic Audio Segmentation Using a Measure of Audio Novelty (CCRMA)
key_references.why: The original 2000 paper introducing the self-similarity matrix + checkerboard novelty score; the foundational citation for structure-surfacing visualization.

key_references.title: Bello & Duxbury et al., Complex-domain onset detection (DAFx 2003)
key_references.why: Source for the steady-state complex predictor X_S(n,k)=|X(n−1,k)|exp(j(φ+Δφ)) and CD(n)=Σ|X−X_S|, the phase-aware novelty that catches soft tonal onsets pure flux misses.

key_references.title: Ziemer & Schmidt, Goniometers are a Powerful Acoustic Feature for MIR (arXiv 2302.01090)
key_references.why: Grounds the goniometer/vectorscope as a quantitative MIR feature (not just a studio toy), including the 45° L/R rotation and correlation-coefficient phase meter used for phase-problem surfacing.

key_references.title: Spectral flatness — Wikipedia / HandWiki
key_references.why: Clean statement of Wiener entropy = geometric mean / arithmetic mean of the power spectrum, its [0,1] / dB range, scale-invariance, and MPEG-7 AudioSpectralFlatness standardization.

key_references.title: Perception and Visualization — Univ. of Iowa STAT4580
key_references.why: Consolidates the cognitive-science backbone: preattentive features (<200ms, search-slope ≈0), the Cleveland-McGill encoding-accuracy hierarchy, and Tufte data-ink — the theory of why a viz reveals a problem at a glance.

key_references.title: Spectral entropy as an information-theoretic correlate (JASA EL127)
key_references.why: Peer-reviewed validation that Shannon entropy of the normalized power spectrum is a meaningful, discriminative acoustic descriptor — anchors the spectral-entropy field technique.

cross_links: Time-frequency representations facet: every descriptor here (entropy, flatness, centroid, flux, complex-domain novelty) is computed ON the STFT magnitude/phase, so the choice of window, hop, and FFT size directly sets the resolution and noise of these insight layers — these are the 'reductions' of that facet's raw spectrogram.

Color/perceptual-mapping facet: the preattentive-channel and Cleveland-McGill ranking here dictates which descriptors should be position-coded vs hue-coded; the entropy/flatness fields specifically need perceptually-uniform colormaps (viridis/magma) to be read accurately.
Stereo/spatial-field facet: the goniometer + correlation meter overlaps directly with spatial-audio visualization (mid/side, width, mono-compatibility) — same √2 L/R rotation underlies both.

Beat/rhythm/tempo facet: the spectral-flux, complex-domain, and Foote novelty curves here are the front-end activation functions that downstream tempo-estimation and beat-tracking (autocorrelation / comb-filter) consume — this facet produces what that facet analyzes.

GPU/realtime-rendering facet: the self-similarity matrix and goniometer density buffer are the two techniques here that most benefit from wgpu texture/compute-shader rendering rather than CPU egui painting.

facet: Rendering math & realtime systems — the GPU/CPU plumbing that makes sound visualization live
summary: This facet is the substrate every other visualization sits on: how spectra get from rustfft into pixels at 60-120 fps without stutter. The decisive architectural pattern, validated across multiple production spectrograms (calebj0seph/spectro, glspect, ChartGPU), is to keep the heavy data in a GPU texture or buffer and do ALL per-frame resampling, log-frequency warping, colormapping, and antialiasing in the fragment/compute shader — the CPU only uploads a thin slice of new FFT data each frame via texSubImage2D into a ring-buffer texture. The most autonomous-dimensioning ideas here are (a) using a *warp lookup texture* so the same magnitude texture can be displayed on linear/mel/log/CQT axes with zero CPU recompute, (b) GPU mipmaps + trilinear minification to antialias the spectrogram automatically as the window resizes or zooms, and (c) min/max envelope tiling + LTTB downsampling that adapt vertex count to pixel width so a 100M-sample waveform draws in <4ms. The math is dominated by: the one-pole/EMA recurrence for temporal smoothing (α = Δt/(τ+Δt)), perceptually-uniform colormap interpolation in CIELAB, geometric/constant-Q bin spacing for log-frequency, and the LTTB triangle-area heuristic. These are individually cheap; the breathtaking-ness comes from doing them fast enough that the image is *alive* and reactive to the millisecond.

techniques.name: Ring-buffer spectrogram texture + shader-side scroll (texSubImage2D circular queue)
techniques.one_line: Store the spectrogram as a wrap-around GPU texture; scroll by moving a read-pointer in the shader, not by copying pixels.
techniques.the_math: Texture T is R×C (R = freq bins, C = time columns) holding magnitudes. A write head w advances mod C each new FFT frame: column index c_write = w; w ← (w+1) mod C. Only ONE column is uploaded per frame: texSubImage2D(T, x=w, y=0, width=1, height=R, newColumn). In the fragment shader, screen u∈[0,1] (time) maps to texel column via c = ((u + w/C) mod 1.0)·C, i.e. sample coordinate s = frac(u + w_norm), w_norm = w/C. Wrap mode = REPEAT makes the modulo free. Cost per frame = O(R) upload + O(pixels) shader sample, independent of history length C.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Nothing about the sound directly — it is pure plumbing — but it is what makes a *continuously scrolling* spectrogram possible at 60fps with arbitrarily long history. Without it you re-blit O(R·C) pixels every frame and stutter once C grows past a few thousand columns.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The wow is indirect: the image flows like water with zero hitching even at 4096-bin resolution and minutes of history, because the per-frame cost is constant. A naive memmove-the-image approach visibly judders; this does not.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: History length C and bin count R are decoupled from frame cost, so you can size the texture to whatever the window/zoom demands and the scroll cost never changes. The shader read-pointer adapts automatically to any time-axis pan/zoom by remapping u→s.
techniques.implementation_notes: In Rust: allocate a wgpu::Texture (R32Float, usage COPY_DST|TEXTURE_BINDING), queue.write_texture() one column/frame. In egui, register it via egui_wgpu Renderer::register_native_texture and paint as an Image, or use a CallbackTrait paint pass for the custom warp shader. Gotcha: write_texture has alignment constraints (bytes_per_row must be 256-aligned) — for a single-column upload of an R-tall texture this is fine since you upload a column as height=R,width=1. Eventual-consistency of the queue is acceptable; tearing is invisible at audio frame rates.

references.title: spectro — making-of (circular queue + texSubImage2D, modulo-1.0 wrap)
glspect — real-time audio spectrogram in OpenGL (circular queue start/end pointers in fragment shader)

techniques.name: Warp lookup texture for log/mel/CQT frequency axis (indirection texture)
techniques.one_line: A 1-D 'where to sample' texture remaps the linear FFT bins onto any nonlinear frequency axis entirely in the shader.
techniques.the_math: FFT gives magnitudes on linear bins f_k = k·fs/N, k=0..N/2. Desired display axis is nonlinear g(y) for screen row y∈[0,1]. Build a 1-D LUT texture W of length P where W[p] = (bin index to sample for display position p)/(N/2), precomputed once on CPU. For log axis: f(y)=f_min·(f_max/f_min)^y ⇒ bin = f(y)·N/fs. For mel: m=2595·log10(1+f/700), inverted f=700·(10^{m/2595}−1). For constant-Q / geometric: f_b = f_min·2^{b/B}, B bins per octave (Brown 1991), Q = 1/(2^{1/B}−1). Shader: float src = texture(W, y).r; float mag = texture(SpectrogramTex, vec2(scrollU, src)).r; linear interpolation in the magnitude texture upsamples between bins automatically.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Reveals pitch/harmonic structure: on a log or CQT axis, octaves are equally spaced and a musical chord's overtones become a rigid translatable comb — impossible to see on a linear axis where low-frequency detail is crushed into a few pixels. Mel reveals perceptual/vocal structure.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Flipping a dropdown linear→log→mel→CQT re-renders instantly with NO recomputation of any FFT, because only the tiny LUT changes. The whole spectrogram smoothly 'rubber-sheets' into the new axis — a genuinely magical interaction.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This is a core autonomous-dimensioning mechanism: the same magnitude data self-adapts to any axis by swapping a 256–1024-entry LUT. f_min/f_max can be auto-derived from the signal's energy distribution (e.g. set f_min to the lowest bin with >threshold long-term energy), so the visible band auto-fits the content.
techniques.implementation_notes: LUT is a tiny R32Float 1-D texture (or 2-D Nx1) recomputed only when axis type or f_min/f_max changes. Use LINEAR filtering on BOTH the LUT and the magnitude texture so inter-bin magnitude interpolation is free. For CQT proper (not just relabeled FFT) you'd need per-bin variable window lengths — but the relabel-via-LUT approximation is what 95% of visualizers ship and is vastly cheaper. Gotcha: the LUT must store *fractional* bin indices for smooth interpolation, not integers.

references.title: spectro making-of — 'a texture tells us where in the FFT data we should sample from' (linear & mel)
Brown (1991) — An efficient algorithm for the calculation of a constant-Q transform
The Constant-Q Transform — A Visual Guide (geometric bins, Q factor)

techniques.name: Perceptually-uniform colormap LUT with CIELAB interpolation
techniques.one_line: Map magnitude→color through a 1-D LUT whose stops are interpolated in CIELAB so equal magnitude steps look equally bright.
techniques.the_math: Color gradient defined by K anchor colors. Convert each anchor sRGB→linear (c_lin = c≤0.04045? c/12.92 : ((c+0.055)/1.055)^2.4)→XYZ (via 3×3 D65 matrix)→L*a*b*: with Xn,Yn,Zn=(95.047,100,108.883), t=X/Xn etc., f(t)=t>δ³? t^{1/3} : t/(3δ²)+4/29, δ=6/29; L*=116·f(Y/Yn)−16, a*=500·(f(X/Xn)−f(Y/Yn)), b*=200·(f(Y/Yn)−f(Z/Zn)). Interpolate the LUT in Lab (optionally quadratic/Catmull-Rom between stops), convert back to sRGB, bake into a 256–1024 entry RGBA8 1-D texture. At render: color = texture(ColorLUT, normalize(mag)). Magnitude normalization is typically log: v = (20·log10(mag)−dB_floor)/(dB_ceil−dB_floor), clamped to [0,1].
techniques.what_it_reveals: Makes faint partials and noise floor *legible* — perceptually-uniform maps (viridis/magma/inferno) have monotonic luminance, so the eye reads magnitude ordering correctly and weak harmonics aren't hidden in a perceptual dead-zone the way they are with jet/rainbow. ΔE in Lab ≈ constant per dB.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The difference between a muddy jet spectrogram and a crisp magma one is night-and-day; subtle reverb tails, breath noise, and harmonic ghosts pop into view. Lab interpolation removes the ugly banding/Mach-band artifacts of naive RGB lerps.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: dB_floor/dB_ceil can auto-track the signal: e.g. set dB_ceil to a smoothed running 99th-percentile of magnitudes and dB_floor to the 5th-percentile, so the colormap's dynamic range auto-fits loud and quiet passages (auto-gain). LUT length can scale with bit-depth needs.
techniques.implementation_notes: All Lab math is CPU, done once when the colormap or dynamic range changes — the per-pixel shader cost is a single 1-D texture tap. Pre-bake matplotlib's viridis/magma/inferno/cividis 256-stop arrays (public domain) to skip the Lab solve entirely; do live Lab interp only for user-authored gradients. Gotcha: store the LUT in an sRGB texture format or apply sRGB-encode after Lab→linear so the GPU's filtering stays perceptually sane.

references.title: spectro making-of — 'quadratic interpolation … interpolating in the Lab color space'
Kenneth Moreland — Color Map Advice for Scientific Visualization
mina86 — sRGB↔L*a*b*↔LCh conversions (exact formulas, δ=6/29 piecewise)
viridis vignette — monotonically increasing luminance, perceptual uniformity

techniques.name: GPU mipmap + trilinear minification as automatic spectrogram antialiasing
techniques.one_line: Let the texture sampler's mipmap chain anti-alias the spectrogram for free whenever it's drawn smaller than its native resolution.
techniques.the_math: When a spectrogram texture of size R×C is displayed in a viewport of P_x×P_y pixels with P<C (zoomed out / narrow window), each pixel's footprint covers ρ=C/P_x texels. Point sampling aliases (moiré, dropped peaks). Mipmaps precompute levels L where level ℓ is a 2× box-downsample: M_ℓ[i] = ¼(M_{ℓ−1}[2i]+M_{ℓ−1}[2i+1]+…) (2-D) or ½ averaging along the minified axis (1-D-ish for time). The sampler picks LOD λ = log2(ρ) = log2(max(|∂s/∂x|,|∂t/∂y|)·texSize), trilinear-blends levels ⌊λ⌋ and ⌈λ⌉. Anisotropic filtering takes N taps along the warped footprint when time and freq minify by different ratios.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Preserves real spectral energy that naive subsampling drops: without prefiltering, a zoomed-out spectrogram folds high-time-resolution detail into false low-frequency moiré (spectral folding/aliasing). Mip averaging keeps the true band energy visible.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Zoom and resize stay buttery and shimmer-free — no crawling moiré on diagonal harmonics, no flickering when the window animates. It looks 'expensive' and polished with essentially zero extra code.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This is antialiasing that *self-scales* to the display size: the GPU automatically selects the mip LOD from the on-screen footprint each frame, so as the user resizes or zooms the correct level of detail is chosen with no app logic. Anisotropic taps auto-adapt to non-square footprints (time vs freq minify independently).
techniques.implementation_notes: In wgpu: create the texture with mip_level_count>1 and usage RENDER_ATTACHMENT|TEXTURE_BINDING, generate mips (no built-in genMipmaps — run a tiny downsample render/compute pass per level, or use the wgpu 'mipmap' example pattern). Sampler: min_filter=Linear, mipmap_filter=Linear, optional anisotropy_clamp. Gotcha: the scrolling ring-buffer texture is awkward to mip live; pragmatic approach is to mip only on zoom-out of a frozen/history view, or keep mips of a separate 'overview' texture. Box-average is correct for magnitude (energy) only if you mip in a perceptually-appropriate domain — mip the linear magnitude, not the dB, to conserve energy.

references.title: Anisotropic filtering — Wikipedia (footprint-adaptive minification)
Mipmap selection in too much detail — pema.dev (LOD λ=log2 footprint)
Fix your downsampling ASAP — aliasing/spectral folding from un-prefiltered subsampling

techniques.name: One-pole / EMA temporal smoothing of spectra (frame-rate-correct)
techniques.one_line: Smooth each frequency bin over time with a single-tap IIR whose coefficient is derived from a desired time constant, independent of frame rate.
techniques.the_math: Per bin, per frame: z_i = α·y_i + (1−α)·z_{i−1} (exponential moving average = first-order IIR low-pass). Frame-rate-correct coefficient from a time constant τ (seconds) and frame interval Δt: α = Δt/(τ+Δt), equivalently α = 1−e^{−Δt/τ} for the exact pole. Transfer fn H(z)=α/(1−(1−α)z^{−1}); −3dB cutoff f_c ≈ α/(2π·Δt) for small α. Asymmetric (attack/release) metering: use α_attack when y_i>z_{i−1}, α_release otherwise — fast rise, slow fall, the classic VU/peak-meter ballistics. Spectral 'max-hold': z_i=max(y_i,(1−α)z_{i−1}).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Trades time resolution for visual stability: separates the stationary spectral envelope (slow τ) from transients (fast τ). Attack/release ballistics make rhythmic energy 'breathe' and reveal envelope dynamics the raw jittery FFT hides. Choosing τ exposes structure at a chosen timescale.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Turns a noisy, strobing bar/spectrum into a smooth, organic, dancing surface — the single most impactful 1-line change for 'liveness.' Asymmetric ballistics give that satisfying snap-up/glide-down feel of pro meters.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: α auto-derives from a wall-clock τ and the *measured* Δt each frame, so smoothing stays perceptually constant whether the app runs at 30, 60, or 144 fps (critical on variable-refresh displays). τ itself can adapt to tempo: estimate the signal's onset rate and shorten τ for fast material, lengthen for sustained.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivial in Rust: a Vec<f32> state of length = bins, one multiply-add per bin per frame — negligible cost, do it CPU-side before upload, or in the compute/fragment shader if smoothing the texture. MUST recompute α from the real measured frame dt (egui gives you ctx.input(|i| i.stable_dt)) or smoothing speed drifts with fps. Gotcha: smooth in linear magnitude or power, not dB, to avoid biasing toward the noise floor; clamp to avoid denormals stalling the FPU.

references.title: mbedded.ninja — EMA filter as first-order IIR low-pass (z_i=α y_i+(1−α)z_{i−1})
Prosig — RC filtering & exponential averaging, α↔time-constant relation

techniques.name: Min/max envelope tiling + LTTB downsampling for huge-waveform line rendering
techniques.one_line: Adapt vertex count to pixel width: draw a per-pixel min/max band when zoomed out, switch to LTTB shape-preserving points when zoomed in.
techniques.the_math: For W screen pixels over a span of M samples, stride s=⌈M/W⌉. Per pixel column p compute (min_p,max_p)=(min,max over samples [p·s,(p+1)·s)) → draw a vertical line/triangle-strip band; vertex count = O(W) not O(M). LTTB (Largest-Triangle-Three-Buckets, Steinarsson 2013) for shape-preserving point reduction to T points: split into T buckets; keep first & last; for each middle bucket pick the point maximizing triangle area with the previous selected point A=(x_a,y_a) and the next bucket's average C=(x_c,y_c): area = ½|x_a(y_b−y_c)+x_b(y_c−y_a)+x_c(y_a−y_b)|, choose argmax over candidate b. O(M) single pass. Render N−1 segments as instanced triangle-strip quads (one instance per segment, expand to a thick line with joins in the vertex shader).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Preserves true peaks, transients, and clipping that naive stride-decimation silently drops — min/max keeps the full dynamic range per pixel (you SEE every transient spike), LTTB keeps sharp inflections and the visual gestalt of the waveform.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A 100M-sample (hours-long) file scrolls and zooms at 60fps with every transient crisp — the waveform feels weightless. Instanced thick lines with proper joins look like a polished DAW, not a jagged plot.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The defining autonomous-dimensioning case: the algorithm reads the current viewport (pixel width W, visible sample span M) every frame and emits exactly enough geometry to fill the pixels — vertex budget self-scales to zoom level. Tier selection (min/max band ↔ LTTB ↔ raw lines) auto-switches on the M/W ratio crossing 1.
techniques.implementation_notes: Two-tier: when M/W≫1 run min/max (parallelizes trivially on a compute shader — ChartGPU does this: step=max(1u,count/screenW)); when M/W≈1 draw raw instanced lines. LTTB is inherently sequential (hard to GPU-parallelize) so run it CPU-side on viewport change, cache the result, redraw cheaply until viewport moves. Frame budget: aim <4ms GPU for the main pass, defer aggregation to viewport-change events (don't recompute per frame). Instanced lines: N−1 instances, each a quad from segment endpoints, miter/round joins in WGSL vertex shader. wgpu SoA vertex buffer, stride 8 (two f32).

references.title: ChartGPU — 1M+ points 60fps, min/max tiling (step=count/screenW), LTTB compute, instanced draws
WebGPU Charts at 120 FPS — frame budget <4ms, upload/transform/draw lanes, viewport-driven LOD
LTTB — triangle-area heuristic & O(n) single pass (Steinarsson 2013 thesis impl)
webgpu-instanced-lines — N−1 instanced segments with joins/caps

techniques.name: egui_wgpu CallbackTrait three-phase pipeline (prepare/paint) as the integration backbone
techniques.one_line: Hook custom spectral/point shaders into egui's own render pass via CallbackTrait's prepare→paint phases, sharing the frame with the rest of the UI.
techniques.the_math: Not numeric — it's the dataflow contract. Per frame: (1) prepare(device,queue,encoder,resources): upload the new FFT column (queue.write_texture), update uniform buffer U={scrollU=w/C, offsetX, scaleX, scaleY, dB_floor, dB_ceil, axisType}, optionally dispatch a compute pass (min/max, mipgen, LTTB indices) into the shared encoder BEFORE the UI pass; (2) paint(render_pass): bind pipeline+texture+sampler+uniforms, issue the draw into the SAME wgpu::RenderPass egui uses. Viewport transform applied in shader: screen = (data + offset)·scale. CallbackResources holds persistent wgpu objects across frames.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Plumbing only — but it's what lets a GPU spectrogram coexist with egui widgets (sliders for τ, f_min/f_max, colormap) in one window without a second window or a blit round-trip, keeping latency to a single frame.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Indirectly enables everything above to render inline, composited under/over immediate-mode controls, at the display refresh — the visualization and its controls feel like one fused live instrument.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The screen_descriptor handed to prepare carries the current pixels-per-point and viewport size every frame, so the shader's scale/LOD uniforms auto-track window resize and DPI without app bookkeeping — the pipeline reshapes itself to the egui rect it's painted into.
techniques.implementation_notes: Use eframe with the wgpu backend (not glow) to get egui_wgpu. Implement egui_wgpu::CallbackTrait; register via egui::PaintCallback inside a Frame allocated by ui.allocate_rect. Store pipeline/bind groups/textures in CallbackResources (type-map keyed). Gotcha: prepare may be called even when paint is culled — don't put irreversible state changes there; the encoder you write into runs before the UI pass so compute results are ready. Alternative (simpler, slightly less flexible): just register the spectrogram texture with Renderer::register_native_texture and paint it as a plain egui Image, doing warp/colormap in a pre-pass to an offscreen target. rustfft feeds the CPU side; everything downstream is wgpu.

references.title: egui discussion #4583 — custom renderpass in CallbackTrait (prepare gives encoder, paint gives RenderPass)
egui-wgpu renderer source — CallbackTrait, CallbackResources, register_native_texture
Egui WGPU custom rendering example (gist)

Directly maps onto every plumbing decision in this facet.
