key_references.why: The exact Rust integration contract (prepare/paint phases, CallbackResources, register_native_texture) for embedding all of the above into the project's egui desktop app.

cross_links: Log-frequency / constant-Q axis (warp-LUT technique) is the rendering substrate for any pitch/harmonic-structure facet (chromagram, harmonic combs, music-theory overlays) — they choose WHAT bins mean; this facet makes the axis swap free.

Perceptually-uniform CIELAB colormap LUT is shared with any heatmap-style facet (scalogram/wavelet, MFCC, self-similarity matrices, recurrence plots) — same 1-D LUT bake, same dB-normalization auto-gain.
EMA/one-pole temporal smoothing is the same recurrence used by beat/onset envelope-followers and loudness/LUFS metering facets; attack/release ballistics couple to tempo-estimation facets.
Min/max tiling + LTTB feeds waveform/oscilloscope and amplitude-envelope facets, and the instanced-line renderer is reused by any 2D/3D point-cloud or phase-space (Lissajous/recurrence) facet.
Ring-buffer scrolling texture is the shared timeline mechanism for STFT spectrogram, scalogram, and chroma-gram facets alike — all are 'scroll a 2-D field' visualizations.
wgpu CallbackTrait integration backbone is what every GPU-heavy facet (3D spectrogram terrain, particle systems, raymarched spectra) plugs into for live egui-embedded rendering.

facet: Topological Data Analysis of sound: persistent homology / sliding-window (SW1PerS) periodicity and persistence-diagram / barcode visuals
summary: Takens delay-embedding turns a sound into a point cloud; persistent homology measures the SHAPE of that cloud across all scales at once, and the resulting barcode / persistence diagram is a coordinate-free, noise-robust signature that ear and waveform cannot show. The capstone pipeline is Perea-Harer SW1PerS: mean-center and sphere-normalize sliding windows, compute Vietoris-Rips H1 persistence, and read periodicity as the LIFESPAN (death - birth) of the single most persistent 1-cycle, normalized so a perfect circle scores 1. The same machinery distinguishes periodic (one round H1 loop), quasiperiodic/dissonant (a d-torus with b1=d loops and b2=C(d,2) voids -- the tritone literally grows an extra hole), and chaotic/noisy (no persistent class) sound by birth-death structure rather than orbit geometry. It is profoundly autonomous-dimensioning: persistence is scale-free, so the "correct" neighborhood radius epsilon is READ OFF the diagram (the most persistent bar) instead of chosen, the optimal window length self-tunes to ~one period, and the field of coefficients (Z2 vs Z3) auto-reveals Mobius twists in the timbre. Feasibility is the real constraint -- exact Vietoris-Rips is O(N^3)-ish in the number of simplices -- but subsampling to N~200-400 points plus Ripser-style cohomology+clearing (a Rust port exists: lophat/teia) makes a ~1-2 Hz refresh barcode realistic; persistence images/landscapes give a stable, GPU-blittable raster.

techniques.name: SW1PerS periodicity score (sliding-window 1-D persistence)
techniques.one_line: Embed sound as a sphere-normalized delay point cloud, then score periodicity as the lifespan of the most persistent H1 loop -- a single number in [0,1] that is shape-agnostic and damping-resistant.
techniques.the_math: Sliding-window embedding (Takens): SW_{d,tau} f(t) = [ f(t), f(t+tau), f(t+2tau), ..., f(t+d*tau) ] in R^{d+1}. Sample basepoints t_0..t_{N-1} along the signal -> point cloud X = {x_i} subset R^{d+1}. Mean-center and sphere-normalize EACH window: x_hat = (x - mean(x)) / || x - mean(x) ||_2, so X_hat lies on the unit sphere S^d (removes DC offset, trend, and amplitude/damping). Optimal window length resonates with the period: w = d*tau ~= (d/(d+1)) * (2*pi / L) where L = expected #periods in window; equivalently, mapping time linearly onto [0,2pi] and using tau = 2pi/(L(d+1)) makes the embedded ellipse 'roundest' (maximally circular) iff L(d+1)tau = 0 mod pi. Compute the Vietoris-Rips filtration on X_hat with Euclidean (or geodesic/chordal) metric: simplex sigma enters at filtration value max edge length within sigma. Extract the 1-D persistence diagram dgm1 = {(b_j, d_j)}. Maximum persistence mp = max_j (d_j - b_j). On a unit-sphere point cloud the roundest possible circle gives mp = sqrt(3), so the normalized periodicity score is  s = 1 - mp / sqrt(3)  in [0,1], with s -> 0 for a perfectly periodic (round-loop) signal and s -> 1 for aperiodic/noisy. (Perea-Harer also use the robust variant s(n,m) = 1 - (d^n - b^m)/3^{n/2}, n=m=2.)
techniques.what_it_reveals: A scalar that says 'how periodic is this 50 ms of sound, independent of WHAT the waveform shape is.' It separates a clean sustained tone, a damped/decaying note, a vibrato'd note, and noise -- all of which can look similar in a spectrogram column -- because it measures circularity of the trajectory, not its spectral content. Resistant to amplitude damping (the sphere-normalization) and to nonsinusoidal shape (any closed orbit, square/saw/voice, still rounds to one H1 loop). It is the single hidden invariant a recurrence plot hints at but never quantifies.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The barcode beneath it is the show-stopper: a stack of horizontal bars where ONE long bar dwarfs a litter of short noise bars, and that one bar's length IS the pitch's 'realness.' Animate it per audio frame and the long H1 bar breathes in and out as a note stabilizes, wavers, and decays -- you are literally watching a hole in the geometry of sound open and close. Pair it with the rotating sphere-embedded loop and you have a coordinate-free, mathematically rigorous 'is this a note?' meter no other visualizer shows.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: This is the apex of self-scaling. (1) Persistence is scale-free: the threshold epsilon that 'is' the loop is not chosen -- it is the birth value of the most persistent bar, read off the diagram. (2) The window length self-tunes: sweep w, pick the w that MINIMIZES s (maximizes mp); the embedding auto-resonates to ~one fundamental period, so it tracks pitch without an explicit f0 estimate. (3) Sphere-normalization auto-removes amplitude/DC, so dynamic range never needs manual gain. (4) Embedding dimension d follows the rule d >= 2*(#harmonics) (M>=2N), self-set from the signal's spectral richness. The diagram's own axes (max birth, max death) auto-bound the plot.
techniques.implementation_notes: Hardest of the family but tractable. Pipeline in Rust: (a) build delay vectors with rustfft-free direct indexing of the sample buffer; (b) subsample to N~200-400 points (Rips simplex count ~ N^3 for H1, so N is the throttle) via farthest-point sampling for even coverage; (c) compute the NxN distance matrix; (d) run Vietoris-Rips persistence via a Ripser-style cohomology+clearing+apparent-pairs engine -- Rust ports exist (lophat = lock-free Morozov-Nigmetov; teia = Rips+persistent cohomology), or FFI to Ripser C++. At N=300, H1-only, with cohomology+clearing this is single-digit to low-tens of ms -> 1-5 Hz barcode refresh, ample for a 'note quality' meter (not per-60fps-frame). Cap homology at dim 1, set a max edge length (enclosing radius) to prune the complex. Gotcha: exact mp = sqrt(3) only for ideal dense sampling -- with finite N the round-loop persistence is slightly under sqrt(3), so calibrate the normalizer empirically or clamp s to [0,1].

references.title: Perea & Harer, Sliding Windows and Persistence: An Application of Topological Methods to Signal Analysis (Found. Comput. Math. 2015) arXiv:1307.6188
Perea et al., SW1PerS: Sliding windows and 1-persistence scoring (BMC Bioinformatics 2015, PMC4537550)

techniques.name: Persistence barcode / diagram as the rendered visual object
techniques.the_math: A persistence diagram is a multiset of points {(b_j, d_j) : b_j <= d_j} in the half-plane above the diagonal y=x; each point is one homology class (a loop for H1, a void for H2). Equivalent barcode: each class is a horizontal interval [b_j, d_j]; bar length = persistence = d_j - b_j. Distance from a diagram point to the diagonal = persistence / sqrt(2); points hugging the diagonal are topological noise, points far above are signal. Stability (Cohen-Steiner et al.): bottleneck distance d_B(dgm(X),dgm(Y)) = inf over bijections gamma sup_x ||x - gamma(x)||_inf is bounded by ||X-Y||_inf, i.e. a small perturbation of the sound moves the diagram only slightly -- the picture is provably noise-robust. p-Wasserstein W_p = (inf_gamma sum_x ||x-gamma(x)||_inf^p)^{1/p} gives a finer, frame-to-frame morph metric.
techniques.one_line: The barcode/diagram itself: a stack of birth-death bars (or above-diagonal scatter) where length = topological importance and proximity-to-diagonal = noise -- a multiscale fingerprint shown all at once.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Every neighborhood radius epsilon simultaneously, instead of one slice. A spectrogram shows energy vs frequency vs time; the diagram shows HOLES vs scale -- a completely orthogonal axis. You see at a glance: 1 long H1 bar = periodic; 2-3 long bars = quasiperiodic/chord/dissonance; a smear of short bars near the diagonal = noise/transient. The diagonal-distance separation gives an automatic, provable signal/noise cut.
techniques.why_breathtaking: It is a genuinely alien, beautiful diagram -- not another scrolling spectrogram. Render it as a live above-diagonal constellation with a shaded 'noise band' along y=x: persistent classes float up as bright stars while noise twinkles on the diagonal. Cross-fade frames with a Wasserstein-optimal transport morph and the stars drift smoothly as the chord changes. Color H1 vs H2 differently and a dissonant interval visibly spawns a second star.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-scaling axes: the plot bounds are exactly [0, max death] on both axes, set by the data. The diagonal noise band auto-positions (it IS y=x). No epsilon, no bin count, no FFT window to pick -- the diagram is the parameter sweep. Bottleneck/Wasserstein give a built-in, scale-aware temporal smoothing distance for animation.
techniques.implementation_notes: Pure egui 2-D drawing once dgm is computed -- trivial: scatter points / draw line segments, shade the y=x band, color by homology dim. The cost is entirely upstream (the Rips computation in technique 1). Frame-to-frame Wasserstein morph for smooth animation is an assignment problem (Hungarian/auction, O(k^3) in #points k, but k is tiny ~ tens) -- cheap. The diagram is the cheap, gorgeous payoff of an expensive computation; reuse one Rips run to draw barcode AND diagram AND feed the score.

references.title: Cohen-Steiner, Edelsbrunner, Harer, Stability of Persistence Diagrams (bottleneck stability)
Bauer, Ripser: efficient computation of Vietoris-Rips persistence barcodes (J. Appl. Comput. Topol. 2021) arXiv:1908.02518

techniques.name: Toroidal H1/H2 persistence for quasiperiodicity & dissonance (b1=d, b2=C(d,2))
techniques.one_line: For sounds with incommensurate frequencies (chords, dissonance, beating) the sliding window densely fills a d-torus; persistent H1 count and H2 voids quantify how many independent pitches and how dissonant -- the tritone literally grows an extra hole.
techniques.the_math: A quasiperiodic signal f(t) = sum_{i=1}^{d} a_i cos(theta_i t + phi_i) with frequencies theta_1..theta_d linearly INDEPENDENT over the rationals has a sliding-window point cloud dense in a d-dimensional torus T^d embedded in S^{2d}. Homology of the torus: betti b_k = C(d,k), so b0=1, b1=d (one loop per incommensurate frequency), b2 = C(d,2) = d(d-1)/2 voids, ..., b_d=1. A consonant interval (small-integer ratio, e.g. 3:2 fifth) is actually PERIODIC at the combined period -> collapses toward a single round loop (1 long H1 bar); a dissonant interval (tritone, ratio ~ sqrt(2):1, irrational) stays quasiperiodic -> a genuine 2-torus with TWO persistent H1 bars + a persistent H2 void. Parameter rule for d-torus detection: embedding dim must satisfy M >= 2d and the window must resonate with all d frequencies; lower bounds on the Rips H1/H2 persistence of the torus guide window/dimension choice. Field-coefficient subtlety: for f = cos(t) + a*cos(k t) with |a|>1 the embedding lies on a Mobius/Klein boundary -- max H1 persistence is maximized only with coefficients in a field whose characteristic is NOT a prime factor of k (Z2 mis-sees a Klein bottle as a torus); so computing PH over Z2 AND Z3 and comparing reveals timbral TWIST.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The number of perceptually independent pitches (= #persistent H1 bars) and consonance vs dissonance as a TOPOLOGICAL invariant: consonant -> low-dimensional round loop, dissonant -> higher-genus torus with extra holes. This is roughness/tension measured by genus, not by a psychoacoustic critical-band heuristic. The Z2-vs-Z3 comparison exposes harmonic structure (the k in cos(kt)) as a Mobius twist -- invisible to every spectral method.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Watch a clean fifth as a single serene ring, then bend it to a tritone and SEE a second hole tear open in the geometry -- dissonance made visible as topology. Render the embedded 2-torus (project S^{2d}->R^3 via PCA), spin it, and overlay its two H1 generator loops in different colors; the diagram simultaneously shows two stars climbing off the diagonal plus an H2 void. A consonance->dissonance glissando becomes a genus-changing morph -- jaw-dropping and pedagogically perfect.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The torus dimension d -- i.e. how many independent pitches -- is DISCOVERED, not assumed: it equals the count of persistent H1 bars, which the diagram self-reports. Embedding dimension then auto-sets to M>=2d. The right field of coefficients is selected by running a couple and keeping the one that maximizes persistence. Window resonance auto-tunes per the lower-bound estimates. Nothing about the number of voices is hard-coded; the homology counts it.
techniques.implementation_notes: Most expensive variant: needs H2, and Vietoris-Rips H2 simplex count ~ N^4, so keep N small (150-250) and cap max edge length aggressively. lophat/teia or Ripser-FFI can do H0-H2; restrict to dim<=2. Running two coefficient fields (Z2, Z3) doubles cost but each is independent -> spawn on two threads. Realistic as an on-demand 'analyze this chord' button (sub-second on a 200-point cloud) rather than a 60fps loop. The torus PCA projection + generator-loop overlay is a wgpu/glow line render, cheap once cocycles are extracted (Ripser can emit representative cocycles).

references.title: Perea, Persistent Homology of Toroidal Sliding Window Embeddings (ICASSP 2016)
Perea et al., Sliding Window Persistence of Quasiperiodic Functions (dissonance detection) arXiv:2103.04540

techniques.name: Persistence landscape & persistence image (stable raster vectorization)
techniques.one_line: Convert the spiky, hard-to-animate diagram into a smooth stack of tent functions (landscape) or a Gaussian-blurred heat image -- a fixed-size, stable, GPU-blittable raster you can fade, stack as a scrollable 'topo-spectrogram,' and feed to ML.
techniques.the_math: Landscape (Bubenik): for each point (b,d) define the tent f_{(b,d)}(t) = max( 0, min( t - b, d - t ) ) (peak height = persistence/2 at t=(b+d)/2). The k-th landscape function lambda_k(t) = k-th largest value of { f_{(b,d)}(t) } over all diagram points; this yields a decreasing sequence lambda_1 >= lambda_2 >= ... of piecewise-linear functions. It is stable: ||lambda(X) - lambda(Y)||_inf <= d_B(dgm(X),dgm(Y)). Persistence Image (Adams): map each diagram point to birth-persistence coordinates (b, p=d-b); place an isotropic Gaussian g_{(b,p)}(x,y) = (1/2 pi s^2) exp( -((x-b)^2+(y-p)^2)/(2 s^2) ), weight by a nondecreasing function w(p) (e.g. w(p)=p, so long-lived classes glow brighter), and the image surface rho(x,y) = sum_j w(p_j) g_{(b_j,p_j)}(x,y); integrate rho over a pixel grid to get a fixed-size image vector. Both are stable under bottleneck/Wasserstein perturbation of the diagram.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The same topological signal as the diagram but as a continuous, differentiable surface: ridges = persistent loops, height = importance. Because output size is fixed and the map is stable, you can stack one image-column per frame into a scrolling 'topological spectrogram' -- a never-seen view where the vertical axis is PERSISTENCE/HOLE-SCALE instead of frequency, revealing how the sound's topological complexity evolves over a phrase.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Buttery, glowing heatmaps instead of jittery dots -- a persistence image of an evolving note is a soft plume that brightens and rises as the loop stabilizes; a dissonant chord shows two plumes. Stacked over time it scrolls like a spectrogram from another dimension. Trivially shaded with any colormap on the GPU, and it cross-fades smoothly (stability guarantees no flicker), so it animates beautifully where raw diagrams pop.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Self-scaling output: image grid bounds = [0,max birth] x [0,max persistence] set by the data; the weight w(p)=p auto-emphasizes signal over diagonal noise so no manual threshold. Landscape depth (how many lambda_k to keep) self-truncates -- only as many nonzero layers as there are classes. The Gaussian bandwidth s is the one knob, and it can be auto-set to a fraction of the median persistence (data-driven). Fixed-size vector means downstream coloring/ML needs no reconfiguration as content changes.
techniques.implementation_notes: Cheapest post-processing in the family and the most GPU-friendly. Given a diagram of k (small) points, the landscape is O(k log k) to build (sort tent breakpoints); the persistence image is a sum of k Gaussians splatted onto, say, a 64x64 or 128x128 texture -- a perfect wgpu/glow fragment-shader job (one additive-blended quad per diagram point, or a single shader looping k<~50 points). Sub-millisecond. Upload as a texture, scroll a ring buffer of columns for the topo-spectrogram. Only knob is bandwidth s; auto-derive from median persistence. The Rips cost upstream is still the gate, but this stage is free and makes the whole pipeline animate gorgeously.

references.title: Bubenik, Statistical Topological Data Analysis using Persistence Landscapes (JMLR 2015)
Adams et al., Persistence Images: A Stable Vector Representation of Persistent Homology (JMLR 2017)

key_references.title: Perea & Harer, Sliding Windows and Persistence (arXiv:1307.6188; Found. Comput. Math. 2015)
key_references.why: The foundational SW1PerS paper: defines the sliding-window embedding, proves max-persistence quantifies periodicity, and gives the window-size/embedding-dimension theory. All the core math (resonance condition, mp -> periodicity) comes from here.

key_references.title: Perea, Deckard, Haase, Harer, SW1PerS (BMC Bioinformatics 2015, PMC4537550)
key_references.why: The practical algorithm + exact scoring formula: mean-center/sphere-normalize, s = 1 - mp/sqrt(3) and the robust s(n,m) variant, plus concrete embedding-dimension and time-normalization-to-[0,2pi] recipes needed to implement it.

key_references.title: Perea, Persistent Homology of Toroidal Sliding Window Embeddings (ICASSP 2016)
key_references.why: Extends SW1PerS to quasiperiodic AUDIO: shows windows fill a d-torus with b1=d loops / b2=C(d,2) voids, with parameter guidance for window size and embedding dimension -- the basis for multi-pitch/chord topology.

key_references.title: Perea et al., Sliding Window Persistence of Quasiperiodic Functions (arXiv:2103.04540)
key_references.why: Music dissonance application: dissonant intervals (tritone) are quasiperiodic -> extra persistent H1 loop; gives Rips persistence lower bounds and the consonance-vs-dissonance-as-topology argument that makes the visual jaw-dropping.

key_references.title: Bauer, Ripser: efficient computation of Vietoris-Rips persistence barcodes (arXiv:1908.02518; JACT 2021)
key_references.why: The feasibility keystone: cohomology + clearing + apparent/emergent pairs + implicit matrix reduction make Rips H1 fast enough for near-realtime; defines the algorithmic target a Rust port must hit.

key_references.title: Chaplin, LoPHAT: Lockfree Persistent Homology Algorithm Toolkit (Rust crate)
key_references.why: Direct Rust path: a maintained crate implementing the parallel lock-free Morozov-Nigmetov reduction (plus teia for Rips+persistent cohomology), so the persistence engine need not be FFI'd -- critical for a pure Rust+egui app.

key_references.title: Tralie & Saul, Ripser.py / TDALabs sliding-window audio notebooks
key_references.why: Pedagogical reference implementation of sliding-window-of-audio persistence with concrete parameter choices and the periodic-vs-noise diagram contrast; the de-facto how-to for the audio pipeline and a port target.

key_references.title: Cohen-Steiner, Edelsbrunner & Harer, Stability of Persistence Diagrams
key_references.why: Proves bottleneck stability -- the guarantee that the barcode/diagram is noise-robust and animates smoothly frame-to-frame; the theoretical license for using these as live visuals and for Wasserstein-morph interpolation.

cross_links: Delay-coordinate (Takens) embedding facet: TDA CONSUMES the exact same delay embedding -- persistent homology is the topological layer that sits directly on top of the attractor point cloud the chaos/nonlinear facet already builds, sharing the tau and dimension d machinery.

Recurrence plot facet: a recurrence plot thresholds the distance matrix at ONE epsilon; the Vietoris-Rips filtration is that same distance matrix swept over ALL epsilon -- persistent homology is the scale-free, parameter-free generalization of the recurrence plot, making this the natural mathematical capstone above it.

Chromagram / harmonic-pitch facets: the torus-genus dissonance measure (consonance = round loop, dissonance = extra hole) is an orthogonal, topological view of the same consonance/roughness that critical-band and chroma methods estimate spectrally.

Self-similarity matrix / novelty (structure) facet: 'Moebius beats' applies sliding-window persistence to the audio NOVELTY function, so rhythm/beat structure also has a twisted-topology signature -- linking TDA to the music-structure facet.

Dimensionality-reduction / projection facets (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP): projecting the high-dim sphere/torus embedding to R^3 for the rotating-loop render reuses the same projection tooling, and persistence images are a fixed-size embedding usable as features for those reducers.

facet: Optical-flow / motion-field and warp-based spectrogram visualization — seeing time-frequency MOTION, not just energy
summary: Every standard spectral view paints |STFT|² as a static intensity field; even reassignment, which relocates energy to its true coordinates, still renders a frozen image. This facet treats the time-frequency plane as a flowing medium and derives a velocity field over it, then renders that field as streamlines, LIC texture, or Middlebury-HSV color. There are two distinct routes to the field. (1) ANALYTIC flow — the reassignment phase-gradient already gives a closed-form displacement vector (local group delay, channelized instantaneous frequency) and its second-order extension gives the chirp slope; this is a per-bin motion field computed FROM the STFT with no iteration, the strongest and most novel item here. (2) IMAGE-SEQUENCE flow — treat consecutive |STFT| frames as a movie and run Horn-Schunck or Lucas-Kanade to get a dense optical-flow field that makes glissandi, vibrato, formant glides and pitch-bends read as continuous gesture. Both self-dimension via flow-magnitude auto-scaling (percentile normalization of the velocity field), and both are realtime-feasible: the analytic route is three extra FFTs per frame; LIC and HSV color run as wgpu fragment shaders.

techniques.name: Analytic reassignment vector field (phase-gradient flow)
techniques.one_line: Render the reassignment displacement (group-delay, instantaneous-frequency) as a per-bin velocity field — the spectrogram's true motion in closed form, no iteration.
techniques.the_math:
From one STFT X(t,ω) with window h, compute two auxiliary STFTs with the time-weighted window h_T(t)=t·h(t) and the derivative window h_D(t)=h'(t). The reassigned coordinates are
t̂(t,ω) = t − Re{ X_{Th}(t,ω)·X*(t,ω) / |X(t,ω)|² }
ω̂(t,ω) = ω + Im{ X_{Dh}(t,ω)·X*(t,ω) / |X(t,ω)|² }.
Equivalently t̂ = t − ∂φ/∂ω (local group delay) and ω̂ = ∂φ/∂t (channelized instantaneous frequency), φ=arg X. The DISPLACEMENT field is the vector v(t,ω) = (t̂−t, ω̂−ω) = (−∂φ/∂ω, ∂φ/∂t − ω). For a pure tone v→0 (energy already at its true place); for a chirp of slope α the frequency residual grows with α, so |v| encodes how 'moving' each bin is. Second-order extension recovers the chirp rate (local frequency velocity) q̂ = ∂ω̂/∂t = Im{ X_{Dh}²X*² ... } via the modulation operator — the analytic d(IF)/dt that quantifies glissando slope per bin.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Whether each time-frequency cell is stationary or in motion, and in which direction — the eye cannot hear 'this partial is gliding up at 40 Hz/s while that one is steady.' Vibrato becomes a swirling oscillation in the field; a pitch-bend a coherent upward stream; a formant transition a shear line. The second-order term color-codes chirp slope, separating rising from falling sweeps that look identical in a plain spectrogram.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The spectrogram stops being a photograph and becomes a fluid you watch flow. Streamlines trace each partial's gesture; the whole plane shimmers along the direction of pitch motion. It is the rare visualization that is simultaneously a rigorous estimator (sub-bin instantaneous frequency) and a gorgeous flow texture.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The field is intrinsically content-adaptive: v is zero where the signal is locally sinusoidal and grows with non-stationarity, so it auto-emphasizes exactly the moving parts. Display scaling is a single robust step — normalize arrow length / LIC speed / HSV saturation by the 95th percentile of |v| over the visible window, so a steady drone and a wild siren both fill the dynamic range without manual gain. Frequency-residual ω̂−ω is naturally bounded by the bin spacing, giving an automatic clamp.
techniques.implementation_notes: Very feasible. Cost is 3× rustfft per frame (X, X_Th, X_Dh) plus elementwise complex ops — trivial next to an existing spectrogram. h_T and h_D are precomputed once from the analysis window (h_D via spectral derivative or finite difference). The whole vector field is a Vec<[f32;2]> the size of the spectrogram; feed it straight to the LIC/arrow/HSV renderers below. Gotchas: divide-by-|X|² is unstable in noise — gate bins below a magnitude floor (set the vector to zero), exactly the synchrosqueezing |W|>γ threshold. Unwrap nothing — the Re/Im cross-spectrum form avoids explicit phase unwrapping entirely, which is the whole point.

references.title: Reassignment method — Wikipedia (digital t̂, ω̂ via T h and D h windows)
Fulop & Fitz, Algorithms for the time-corrected instantaneous-frequency (reassigned) spectrogram
Flandrin, Auger, Chassande-Mottin — Time-Frequency Reassignment and Synchrosqueezing
Chirp-rate estimation for time-frequency reassigned spectrograms (2nd-order phase)

techniques.name: Dense optical flow on STFT frames (Horn-Schunck / Lucas-Kanade)
techniques.one_line: Treat consecutive |STFT| frames as an image sequence and solve the brightness-constancy flow to get a (Δtime, Δfreq) velocity at every cell.
techniques.the_math:
Let I(x,y,n) be the log-magnitude spectrogram (x=time-bin, y=freq-bin, n=frame). Brightness-constancy: I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0 (one equation, two unknowns — the aperture problem). HORN-SCHUNCK adds global smoothness and minimizes
E(u,v) = ∬ [ (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)² + α²(|∇u|² + |∇v|²) ] dx dy,
whose Euler-Lagrange equations
I_x(I_x u + I_y v + I_t) − α²∇²u = 0,  I_y(I_x u + I_y v + I_t) − α²∇²v = 0
are solved by Jacobi iteration u←ū − I_x(I_xū+I_yv̄+I_t)/(α²+I_x²+I_y²) (v analogous), ū the local average. LUCAS-KANADE instead assumes constant flow in a window W and solves the 2×2 normal equations
[Σw I_x²  Σw I_xI_y ; Σw I_xI_y  Σw I_y²] (u;v) = −(Σw I_xI_t ; Σw I_yI_t),
i.e. (u,v)=(AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb with the STRUCTURE TENSOR M=AᵀA. The eigenvalues of M give a confidence/coherence mask (both large ⇒ trustworthy flow; one small ⇒ aperture-ambiguous, fade out).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Frame-to-frame transport of spectral energy: where partials are sliding, splitting, merging, or being born. Because it tracks the IMAGE, it captures motion the analytic per-bin field cannot — e.g. a whole harmonic stack translating coherently, or energy migrating between formants — and the structure-tensor eigenvalues expose which regions are genuinely directional vs. textureless noise.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Vibrato becomes a literal back-and-forth current; a riser/sweep a sheet of energy streaming upward; the attack of a note a burst radiating outward. Overlaid on the magnitude image as streamlines or HSV it turns the spectrogram into a living weather map of the sound.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: α (HS smoothness) can be set from the image gradient statistics so it adapts to SNR; the percentile-normalized |(u,v)| auto-scales display speed. A multi-scale (Gaussian-pyramid) HS handles both slow drones and fast sirens without retuning, automatically choosing the coarse level where large motions become sub-pixel. The structure-tensor eigenvalue mask self-selects where to draw flow at all.
techniques.implementation_notes: Feasible but heavier than the analytic route. HS is ~10–50 Jacobi sweeps over the spectrogram per frame — fine on CPU for moderate FFT sizes, ideal on a wgpu compute/fragment ping-pong (each sweep is a stencil). I_t is the inter-frame difference; warp magnitudes are tiny (sub-bin) so a single pyramid level often suffices. Gotchas: log-magnitude (not linear) is essential or loud bins dominate; pre-smooth with a small Gaussian to make I_x,I_y well-defined; the time axis is already 'flow' (hop), so interpret u relative to hop size. Lucas-Kanade is cheaper and embarrassingly parallel but blocky — good for a fast preview, HS for the beauty pass.

references.title: Horn–Schunck method — energy functional & Euler-Lagrange (search-grounded summary)
Lucas–Kanade method — structure tensor least-squares (Wikipedia mirror)
CMU 16-385 Optical Flow: Horn-Schunck lecture notes
Multiresolution Horn-Schunck with bilinear interpolation (arXiv 2511.16535)

techniques.name: Line Integral Convolution (LIC) of the TF flow field
techniques.one_line: Smear a white-noise texture along the streamlines of the spectrogram's velocity field so the whole plane shows continuous flow direction as a silky directional grain.
techniques.the_math:
Given velocity field v(r) on the TF plane, define the streamline σ_r(s) through r by dσ/ds = v(σ)/|v(σ)|. The LIC output at pixel r is the convolution of a kernel k along that streamline against an input noise texture N:
D(r) = ∫_{−L/2}^{+L/2} k(s) · N(σ_r(s)) ds.
A box kernel k≡1/L makes it Fast-LIC (reuse partial sums along shared streamlines). MAGNITUDE is folded in two ways: (a) scale streamline length L ∝ |v| so fast regions get longer, smoother streaks; (b) use a periodic ripple kernel k(s)=cos(2π(s−φ)/T)·Hann(s) and advance the phase φ each frame to ANIMATE flow that visibly moves along v — speed of the moving grain ∝ |v|. Final image: hue = atan2(v_freq, v_time) (Middlebury), value = LIC grayscale, so direction and texture coexist.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Turns a sparse arrow field into a dense, gapless field-line portrait — every cell participates, so coherent gestures (a glide, a sweep) appear as long unbroken streaks while turbulent/noisy regions look granular and short. The animated ripple makes the DIRECTION of motion unambiguous (up-glide vs down-glide), which static arrows leave to inference.
techniques.why_breathtaking: LIC is the canonical 'this looks like flowing silk / van Gogh sky' technique from scientific flow viz; applied to sound it makes the spectrogram look like a frozen fluid that you then set in motion. It is widely regarded as the most aesthetically striking dense-field visualization.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Streamline length L and animation speed both bind to the auto-normalized |v|, so the texture self-tunes: still regions show fine isotropic noise, moving regions show long oriented streaks — no parameter the user must set per clip. Noise texture frequency can be set from the display resolution so streak width stays perceptually constant under zoom.
techniques.implementation_notes: Excellent GPU fit. The streamline integral is a fragment-shader loop: at each pixel, step ±L/2 along v (bilinearly sampled), accumulate kernel·noise. 20–40 steps each way at 60fps is routine in wgsl. Inputs: the velocity field (RG32F texture) from either technique above, plus a white-noise R8 texture. Animate by passing φ as a uniform. Gotchas: normalize v to unit step but keep |v| in a separate channel for length/hue; clamp at field zeros (don't integrate through dead bins); use a low-discrepancy/blue noise input for cleaner streaks than pure white noise.

references.title: Line integral convolution — Wikipedia (D(r)=∫k(s)N(σ_r(s))ds, ripple-kernel animation)
Cabral & Leedom, Imaging Vector Fields Using Line Integral Convolution (SIGGRAPH 1993)
Stalling & Hege, Fast and Resolution Independent Line Integral Convolution
Forssell & Cohen, LIC for curvilinear grids, variable-speed & unsteady flows

techniques.name: Synchrosqueezed warp (frequency-only reassignment transport)
techniques.one_line: Use the instantaneous-frequency estimate to SQUEEZE / warp energy vertically onto its true frequency, an invertible reassignment that you can animate as a morph from blurry STFT to razor ridges.
techniques.the_math:
From the STFT (or CWT) coefficient W(t,ω), estimate the instantaneous frequency by the phase time-derivative
ω̂(t,ω) = Im{ ∂_t W(t,ω) / W(t,ω) } / (2π)   for |W(t,ω)| > γ,  else undefined.
Synchrosqueezing reallocates energy ONLY in frequency (unlike full 2-D reassignment): the squeezed transform sums every coefficient into the output bin labeled by its IF estimate,
T(t, η) = Σ_{ω : |ω̂(t,ω) − η| ≤ Δη/2} W(t,ω) · (normalization).
Because transport is vertical only, the map is invertible (you can resynthesize), and the warp field is the scalar displacement d(t,ω)=ω̂(t,ω)−ω. Animate with a parameter λ∈[0,1]: y_display = ω + λ·d, morphing the smeared STFT (λ=0) into concentrated ridges (λ=1).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Exactly how far, and in which direction, each bin's energy is 'wrong' — the vertical warp field d is the pure pitch-error map. Watching the λ-morph, you see partials snap from fat blurred bands into hairline curves, exposing close harmonics, FM sidebands, and microtonal detail invisible in the raw spectrogram. Because it is invertible, it doubles as a separation/denoise tool you can audition.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The morph is mesmerizing: a foggy spectrogram crystallizes into glowing filament curves before your eyes, and you control the dissolve. The ridges trace melody and vibrato as clean light-threads.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The output frequency grid η can be auto-set to the range actually occupied by ω̂ values (data-driven axis), and the magnitude threshold γ is set from the noise floor estimate so only meaningful bins are transported — the transform automatically ignores empty/noisy regions. Bin width Δη can adapt to local ridge density.
techniques.implementation_notes: Feasible; shares the analytic machinery. IF needs ∂_t W, which is the same derivative-window STFT (X_Dh) already computed for reassignment — so synchrosqueeze is nearly free if you build the analytic field. The vertical scatter-add is a histogram per time column (CPU loop or GPU atomic add into a column texture). The λ-morph is a pure vertical displacement in the vertex/fragment shader — buttery at 60fps. Gotchas: scatter-add aliasing — splat with a small kernel, not nearest-bin; threshold γ matters, expose it but default from median |W|; for resynthesis keep the complex values, not just magnitudes.

references.title: Daubechies, Lu & Wu — Synchrosqueezed wavelet transforms (arXiv 1105.0010)
MathWorks — Wavelet Synchrosqueezing (IF reassignment to ridge centroids)
The Synchrosqueezing transform for instantaneous spectral analysis (Springer)
Flandrin et al. — Reassignment & Synchrosqueezing (SST as freq-only reassignment)

techniques.name: Structure-tensor ridge-orientation field (energy-free slope map)
techniques.one_line: Compute the local gradient structure tensor of the spectrogram image; its dominant eigenvector gives ridge orientation (chirp slope) and its eigenvalue ratio gives coherence — a cheap motion-direction field with no inter-frame data.
techniques.the_math:
On the (smoothed) log-magnitude image I(x,y) form the gradient g=(I_x,I_y) and the windowed second-moment (structure) tensor
J = G_ρ * [ I_x²  I_xI_y ; I_xI_y  I_y² ]
(G_ρ a Gaussian). Eigen-decompose J = λ₁ e₁ e₁ᵀ + λ₂ e₂ e₂ᵀ with λ₁≥λ₂. The RIDGE runs along e₂ (the minimum-gradient direction); its angle θ = ½·atan2(2 J_xy, J_xx − J_yy) is the local time-frequency SLOPE (chirp rate in bins/frame ≈ tan θ). Anisotropy/coherence
C = ((λ₁−λ₂)/(λ₁+λ₂))²
measures how line-like (vs blob/noise) the region is — use it as the field's confidence/opacity. The resulting orientation field can feed LIC or arrows directly.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The instantaneous slope of every spectral ridge — separating flat sustained tones (θ≈0) from sweeps (θ≠0) and giving the sign of the glide — computed from a single frame, no temporal differencing. Coherence C cleanly masks harmonic ridges from broadband noise. It is the geometric (image-domain) cousin of the analytic chirp rate.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Renders the spectrogram as a brushed-metal / hair-flow field where each strand follows a partial's slope; combined with LIC the harmonics look combed. Less flashy than full flow but extremely clean and immediate.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: ρ (integration scale) sets the trade between angular accuracy and locality and can be tied to the FFT bin spacing automatically; coherence C is a self-normalizing [0,1] confidence that auto-fades textureless regions. Orientation is double-angle so it needs no range tuning. It degrades gracefully on any content without per-clip parameters.
techniques.implementation_notes: Cheapest item here — three separable Gaussian convolutions plus a closed-form 2×2 eigensolve per pixel, all trivially a single fragment shader, easily 60fps at full spectrogram resolution. No inter-frame state, so no latency. Gotchas: orientation is undirected (±θ ambiguous) — to get the SIGN of a glide combine with I_t or with the analytic ω̂−ω residual; smooth the tensor (the G_ρ step) not the image too aggressively or thin ridges vanish; double-angle averaging (use 2θ) avoids wrap artifacts when pooling.

references.title: Structure tensor — Wikipedia (J, eigen-decomposition, orientation & coherence)
OpenCV — Anisotropic image segmentation by a gradient structure tensor (θ, coherence C formulas)
Luengo — The Hessian and the Structure Tensor

techniques.name: Middlebury HSV color-coding of the flow field
techniques.one_line: Map the velocity field's angle to hue and its (auto-normalized) magnitude to saturation so the whole motion field reads at a glance in one image.
techniques.the_math:
For each cell with flow (u,v): direction φ = atan2(v,u) ∈ (−π,π], magnitude m = √(u²+v²). Encode
Hue H = (φ + π)/(2π) · 360°,
Saturation S = clamp( m / m_ref , 0, 1 )  with m_ref = percentile_95( m ) over the visible window,
Value V = 1 (or tie V to the underlying spectrogram magnitude to fuse energy + motion in one frame).
Convert HSV→RGB per pixel. Stationary cells (m≈0) → white/low-saturation; strong coherent motion → vivid, hue telling up-glide (e.g. cyan) from down-glide (e.g. red).
techniques.what_it_reveals: Direction and speed of every time-frequency cell's motion simultaneously, with no arrows to declutter — rising vs falling sweeps become opposite hues, vibrato a fast hue-cycling shimmer, a steady tone pale. Fusing V with energy overlays 'what is loud' and 'what is moving' in a single legible picture.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Instantly readable, saturated, poster-like; the standard that made optical-flow fields iconic in vision. On audio it gives a vibrant aurora over the spectrogram that pulses with pitch motion.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: The single free parameter m_ref is set automatically from the 95th-percentile of the field, so the color scale self-calibrates per clip and per visible window — a whisper-quiet flute and a screaming siren both use the full saturation range. A fixed color wheel means the legend never needs retuning.
techniques.implementation_notes: Trivial and free — a per-pixel HSV→RGB in the fragment shader consuming the same RG32F velocity texture used by LIC. The only CPU step is the percentile (a cheap histogram over the visible field, recomputed per frame or smoothed over time to avoid flicker). Gotchas: temporally smooth m_ref (EMA) so the colors don't pump frame-to-frame; pick a hue wheel that is perceptually uniform (e.g. based on a cyclic colormap) so equal angle steps look equal; consider desaturating below a small m floor to keep noise neutral.

references.title: Baker et al., A Database and Evaluation Methodology for Optical Flow (Middlebury color wheel)
Pyimof — Vector field color coding (HSV hue=direction, sat=magnitude)
Color coding of flow vectors: direction=hue, length=saturation (ResearchGate figure)

key_references.title: Reassignment method — Wikipedia
key_references.why: Gives the exact digital reassignment formulas t̂, ω̂ via the time-weighted (Th) and derivative (Dh) windows — the closed-form analytic motion field at the heart of this facet.

key_references.title: Fulop & Fitz — Algorithms for the time-corrected instantaneous-frequency (reassigned) spectrogram
key_references.why: Canonical, implementation-ready derivation of the cross-spectrum reassignment estimators that avoid phase unwrapping — directly portable to a rustfft pipeline.

key_references.title: Flandrin, Auger & Chassande-Mottin — Time-Frequency Reassignment and Synchrosqueezing
key_references.why: Unifies reassignment (2-D displacement field), synchrosqueezing (vertical-only invertible warp), and the second-order chirp-rate quantities — the theoretical spine linking all the analytic techniques here.

key_references.title: Line integral convolution — Wikipedia
key_references.why: Exact LIC integral D(r)=∫k(s)N(σ_r(s))ds plus the ripple-kernel animation and magnitude-via-length tricks — the recipe for the breathtaking dense flow texture.

key_references.title: Horn–Schunck method — energy functional and Euler-Lagrange iteration
key_references.why: The variational image-sequence flow used to derive a dense velocity field directly from consecutive spectrogram frames, with the iterative update scheme suited to a GPU stencil.

key_references.title: Daubechies, Lu & Wu — Synchrosqueezed wavelet transforms (arXiv 1105.0010)
key_references.why: Founding synchrosqueezing paper: IF estimate ω̂=Im{∂_tW/W}/2π with |W|>γ threshold and the invertible vertical reassignment that powers the warp/morph technique.

key_references.title: Structure tensor — Wikipedia
key_references.why: Closed-form ridge orientation θ and coherence C from a single frame — the cheapest motion-direction/chirp-slope field and a confidence mask for all the others.

cross_links: Time-frequency reassignment facet: this facet REUSES the reassignment vectors but, instead of relocating energy into a still image, treats the displacement as a velocity field to be flowed/streamlined — the explicit 'motion FROM the spectrogram' the reassignment facet stops short of.

Generative/fluid-simulation facet: LIC and HSV here render a field DERIVED from the audio's own phase gradient, whereas generative fluids inject audio features as forcing terms into a free Navier-Stokes sim — same fluid aesthetic, opposite epistemics (measured vs decorative).

Instantaneous-frequency / pitch-tracking facet: the analytic ω̂ and synchrosqueezed IF estimate are the same quantity used for monophonic/polyphonic pitch ridges; this facet visualizes the FULL 2-D field instead of extracting a single contour.

GPU shader / wgpu rendering facet: every renderer here (LIC streamline integral, HSV color, structure-tensor eigensolve, synchrosqueeze vertical morph) is a fragment/compute shader consuming one RG32F velocity texture — shares the wgpu pipeline and the percentile auto-normalization used elsewhere.

Chirp/transient analysis facet: the second-order phase derivative (chirp rate q̂) and the structure-tensor slope θ both quantify glissando steepness, feeding any facet that classifies sweeps, risers, and FM gestures.

facet: Differentiable / Neural & Feature-Inversion Visualization (DDSP control surfaces, gradient saliency "what the model hears", feature inversion / morphing, GAN-diffusion latent walks)
summary: This facet covers visuals produced or explained by gradients flowing through a trained model, rather than by a fixed signal transform. Four sub-families dominate: (1) DDSP interpretable control surfaces -- a harmonic+filtered-noise synth whose f0 / loudness / normalized-harmonic-distribution parameters are exactly the panel you draw and which animates with the sound; (2) gradient saliency overlays (Grad-CAM, integrated gradients, SmoothGrad) that paint which time-frequency cells drive a classifier, literally rendering what a network attends to; (3) feature inversion / morphing that reconstructs and blends audio (and its spectrogram) from a learned representation by gradient descent on the input; and (4) GAN/VAE/diffusion latent-space walks (GANSynth, RAVE, Riffusion) rendered as evolving spectro-visual manifolds via slerp. The standout for insight is saliency -- it shows model reasoning, not signal, the only family here revealing structure no DSP transform can. For autonomous dimensioning, latent-walk and PCA-of-latent methods self-organize control axes to the content, and DDSP's two-feature (f0, loudness) bottleneck is a content-driven auto-reduction. Feasibility is the catch: all require a trained model plus an inference runtime (ort/tract/candle/wonnx in Rust), heavier than pure-FFT visuals but realtime-capable on CPU/wgpu once a small ONNX model is bundled.

techniques.name: DDSP Interpretable Control Surface (harmonic + filtered-noise synth as a live, drawable panel)
techniques.one_line: Render the sound's own additive-synth control parameters -- f0, loudness, and the normalized harmonic distribution -- as an animated, editable surface that IS the synthesis model.
techniques.the_math: Harmonic additive synthesizer (Engel et al. 2020, ICLR): x(n) = sum_{k=1..K} A_k(n) * sin(phi_k(n)), with instantaneous phase as the running integral of frequency phi_k(n) = 2*pi * sum_{m=0..n} f_k(m) + phi_{0,k}, and harmonic constraint f_k(n) = k * f0(n). Amplitudes factorize as A_k(n) = A(n) * c_k(n) where A(n) is overall loudness and c(n) is the normalized harmonic distribution with sum_k c_k = 1, c_k >= 0. All non-negative network outputs pass a modified sigmoid y = 2*sigmoid(x)^log(10) + 1e-7. The filtered-noise (subtractive) branch is an LTV-FIR via frequency sampling: per frame l the net emits a magnitude transfer function H_l, window it h_l = IDFT(W (.) H_l) with W a Hann window (size 257), shift to causal form, convolve with uniform noise N_l in [-1,1]: Y_l = H_l * N_l. Training uses the multi-scale spectral loss L = sum_i ( ||S_i - S_hat_i||_1 + alpha*||log S_i - log S_hat_i||_1 ) over FFT sizes i in {2048,1024,512,256,128,64}, alpha=1.0.
techniques.what_it_reveals: It decomposes the sound into perceptually meaningful, separable controls a spectrogram never hands you directly: a clean f0 contour, a loudness envelope, and a normalized harmonic spectral shape decoupled from loudness, plus a residual noise filter for the inharmonic part. You see the harmonic/noise split, the spectral tilt, and the exact integer-harmonic structure as independent editable curves -- the synthesis recipe of the sound.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A stacked, glowing control surface: a flowing f0 ribbon, a loudness band, and a heatmap of c_k(n) (harmonic index vs time) shimmering as the spectral shape morphs -- plus a separate noise-filter band. Because it is the actual differentiable synth, dragging any curve re-renders audio live, so the visual is also a playable instrument.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Strong. DDSP's encoder auto-reduces arbitrary monophonic audio to just two interpretable features (f0, loudness); K (harmonic count) auto-truncates at Nyquist (k*f0 < fs/2) so the harmonic axis self-sizes with pitch. The normalized c_k auto-rescales the spectral-shape heatmap per frame, and the noise-filter resolution is set by the model rather than hand-tuned.
techniques.implementation_notes: Two paths. (a) Pure-DSP, no model: estimate f0 (YIN/pYIN/CREPE-lite) + A-weighted loudness + per-harmonic magnitudes by sampling the STFT at k*f0 bins -- fully realtime in Rust with rustfft, no neural net, ships today; gives the entire control-surface visual without training anything. (b) Full DDSP timbre-transfer needs a trained net via ort/candle and is heavier. The additive resynth itself (cumulative-sum phase, sin oscillator bank) is cheap and trivially realtime on CPU; wgpu for the heatmap. Gotchas: phase accumulation must be done in f64 or wrapped to avoid drift; f0 octave errors corrupt the whole harmonic stack -- gate on voicing confidence.

references.title: DDSP: Differentiable Digital Signal Processing (Engel et al., ICLR 2020) -- arXiv 2001.04643
Magenta DDSP project page (interactive control-surface demos)
A Review of Differentiable DSP for Music and Speech Synthesis (Hayes et al., Frontiers 2023)

techniques.name: Grad-CAM Saliency Overlay on the Spectrogram (what the classifier attends to)
techniques.one_line: Paint a class-conditional heatmap over the spectrogram showing which time-frequency regions actually drove a CNN's decision, revealing the network's attention rather than the signal.
techniques.the_math: Grad-CAM (Selvaraju et al. 2017) on a spectrogram-input CNN. Target class c with pre-softmax score y^c. For a chosen conv layer with feature maps A^k, the importance weight is the global-average-pool of the gradient of y^c over feature-map cells: alpha_k^c = (1/Z) * sum_i sum_j ( d y^c / d A^k_{ij} ), Z = number of (i,j) cells. The localization map is the ReLU of the weighted channel sum: L^c = ReLU( sum_k alpha_k^c * A^k ). ReLU keeps only features with positive influence on class c. Bilinearly upsample L^c to the spectrogram's time x freq grid and alpha-blend as a colormap. Grad-CAM++ sharpens via higher-order terms alpha_k^c = sum_{ij} w^{kc}_{ij} * ReLU(d y^c / d A^k_{ij}); Score-CAM removes gradients entirely (mask the input by each upsampled activation, weight by the resulting score change).
techniques.what_it_reveals: The MODEL'S reasoning, not the signal: which formant band identifies a vowel, which onset transient flags a snare, which narrow harmonic ridge betrays a deepfake. Two spectrograms identical to the eye can yield wildly different saliency, exposing that the classifier keys on a region a human would never notice. This information is provably absent from any signal-only transform -- it exists only relative to a trained decision boundary.
techniques.why_breathtaking: A warm, semi-transparent attention cloud floating over the cool spectrogram, pulsing and migrating across frequency bands as the predicted class changes frame-to-frame -- like an MRI of listening. Cycling the target class c makes whole regions light up and go dark, which is mesmerizing.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Moderate-to-strong. The heatmap normalizes to its own per-frame max so the colormap self-scales; the chosen layer's receptive field auto-sets spatial granularity (deeper = coarser, more semantic). With a multi-label model, auto-rank classes by y^c and overlay only the top-N active ones, so the number of attention layers adapts to how many sound events are present.
techniques.implementation_notes: Needs a CNN ingesting (mel-)spectrograms and a runtime exposing intermediate activations AND gradients. In Rust, ort (ONNX Runtime) does forward inference but not easy backprop -- use candle or burn (autograd) and ship a small audio CNN (compact PANNs/YAMNet-style). Realtime feasible at block granularity (compute saliency every ~10-20 frames, interpolate) on CPU for a small net; wgpu/Metal/CUDA via candle, or wonnx on GPU. Gotchas: retain gradients on the target activation; upsampling artifacts; Grad-CAM degrades on transformers (use attention rollout / IG instead). Score-CAM is gradient-free (forward-only, ort-friendly) at the cost of N extra inferences per frame.

references.title: Grad-CAM: Visual Explanations from Deep Networks via Gradient-based Localization (Selvaraju et al. 2017) -- formula reference
A Framework for Evaluating Faithfulness in XAI for Anomalous Sound Detection (freq-band saliency on spectrograms) -- arXiv 2601.19017
Phoneme Discretized Saliency Maps for Explainable Detection of AI-Generated Voice -- arXiv 2406.10422

techniques.name: Gradient Attribution Maps: Integrated Gradients & SmoothGrad (axiomatic saliency)
techniques.one_line: Per-cell, signed attribution over the spectrogram that is theoretically faithful (sensitivity + implementation-invariance), de-noised by path-integration and noise-averaging, working for any differentiable model including transformers.
techniques.the_math: Vanilla saliency M(x) = d y^c / d x (gradient of class score w.r.t. each spectrogram cell) is noisy and violates sensitivity. Integrated Gradients (Sundararajan et al. 2017) attribute along a straight path from baseline x' (silence / zero spectrogram) to input x: IG_i(x) = (x_i - x'_i) * integral_{a=0..1} d F(x' + a(x - x')) / d x_i da, approximated by a Riemann sum over M steps: IG_i ~= (x_i - x'_i) * (1/M) * sum_{m=1..M} d F(x' + (m/M)(x-x')) / d x_i. Completeness: sum_i IG_i = F(x) - F(x'). SmoothGrad (Smilkov et al. 2017) averages gradients over n Gaussian-perturbed copies to kill noise: M_hat(x) = (1/n) * sum_{j=1..n} d F(x + Normal(0, sigma^2 I)) / d x. Combine as SmoothGrad-IG. Display |attribution| or signed (diverging colormap) reshaped to time x freq and blended over the spectrogram.
techniques.what_it_reveals: Cell-level signed evidence: which exact partials pushed the decision toward (warm) or away from (cool) the class. Unlike Grad-CAM's coarse blob, IG gives per-bin attribution -- you can see that only the odd harmonics of a clarinet drove a 'clarinet' call, or that a single 8 kHz codec artifact betrayed a synthetic voice. It exposes spurious shortcuts (model keying on background hum) invisible by ear.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Sharper and more filigree than Grad-CAM -- a delicate, signed lacework of red/blue evidence tracing individual harmonics and transients, with completeness meaning the colors literally sum to the prediction. SmoothGrad denoising makes it shimmer cleanly rather than sparkle with noise.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Moderate. Baseline choice and step count M auto-trade fidelity vs cost; attribution magnitude self-normalizes per clip. IG is model-agnostic (any differentiable F), so the same overlay machinery adapts across CNN, RNN, and transformer audio models without layer-specific code -- the dimensioning is set by the loaded model, not hand-tuned.
techniques.implementation_notes: Cost = M (~20-50) x n (~10-25) backward passes per explained frame, the heaviest saliency family -- run on a sparse keyframe schedule and temporally interpolate, or precompute offline for a static 'explained' view. Requires autodiff (candle/burn; ort alone won't backprop). Numerically stable, simple once gradients exist. Gotcha: pick a meaningful baseline (zero-magnitude spectrogram ~ silence); a bad baseline makes attributions meaningless. For realtime, prefer single-shot SmoothGrad (skip the path integral) at n~8.

references.title: Understanding Integrated Gradients with SmoothTaylor for DNN Attribution (Goh et al.) -- arXiv 2004.10484
Visualizing the Impact of Feature Attribution Baselines (Distill, 2020) -- IG baselines
Toward Faithful Explanations in Acoustic Anomaly Detection -- arXiv 2601.12660

techniques.name: Activation Maximization / DeepDream for Sound (synthesize what a neuron wants to hear)
techniques.one_line: Gradient-ascend an input spectrogram to maximally excite a chosen neuron or class, producing the network's hallucinated ideal sound and its evolving spectro-visual.
techniques.the_math: Activation maximization finds the input maximizing a unit's activation a_l (neuron, channel mean, or class logit y^c) with regularizer R: x* = argmax_x [ a_l(x) - lambda * R(x) ], iterated by gradient ascent x <- x + eta * d a_l / d x (optionally normalized eta * g/||g||). DeepDream variant maximizes the L2 norm of an existing layer's activations on a seeded input: objective = 0.5*||A^l(x)||^2, update x <- x + eta * d(0.5*||A^l||^2)/d x. Regularizers prevent high-frequency garbage: total variation TV(x) = sum |x_{i+1,j} - x_{i,j}| + |x_{i,j+1} - x_{i,j}|, plus jitter/blur and a frequency-space prior. For audio (DreamSound / time-series DeepDream) the optimized spectrogram or waveform is grown iteratively then resynthesized (Griffin-Lim or a neural vocoder) so you can watch AND hear the dream.
techniques.what_it_reveals: It externalizes the network's internal concept of a sound: the prototypical texture a 'siren' or 'snare' neuron is tuned to, stripped of any real recording. The optimization trajectory shows how the model builds a class from noise -- global pitch/rhythm scaffolding first, then fine harmonic detail -- revealing the feature hierarchy directly. It is the generative dual of saliency.
techniques.why_breathtaking: The spectrogram crystallizes out of noise frame by frame into uncanny, hyper-regular harmonic textures -- psychedelic, rhythmic, alien-instrument visuals that morph as you climb the gradient. A known source of award-worthy, otherworldly audio-reactive material.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Moderate. The target unit/class auto-defines the visual's theme; regularizer strength lambda self-trades detail vs coherence. Octave / multi-scale ascent (DeepDream's classic trick) grows structure across resolutions automatically, so emergent feature scale adapts to the chosen layer depth.
techniques.implementation_notes: Needs autodiff over a trained audio net (candle/burn). This is an OPTIMIZATION LOOP per frame (tens-to-hundreds of ascent steps), so it is offline/generative, not live-input realtime -- but playback of a pre-rendered dream IS realtime. Resynthesis adds a Griffin-Lim pass (pure Rust + rustfft, cheap) or a small vocoder. Gotchas: without TV/jitter regularization you get checkerboard noise; mel-to-linear inversion for playback is lossy. Lighter realtime cousin: skip optimization and visualize live per-neuron activations as a glowing unit grid (forward-only, ort-friendly).

references.title: DreamSound: Deep Activation Layer Sonification (Georgia Tech)
Finding the DeepDream for Time Series: Activation Maximization for Univariate Time Series -- arXiv 2408.10628

techniques.name: GAN / VAE Latent-Space Walk as an Evolving Spectro-Visual Manifold (GANSynth, RAVE)
techniques.one_line: Render the smooth, structured journey through a generative model's latent space as a continuously morphing spectrogram surface, audio-reactively steered by the live signal's encoding.
techniques.the_math: A generator G: z -> spectrogram (GANSynth) or decoder D: z -> waveform (RAVE). Interpolate between latent points on the prior hypersphere with spherical linear interpolation (slerp), keeping the path on the constant-norm manifold the model trained on: slerp(z0, z1; t) = sin((1-t)*Omega)/sin(Omega) * z0 + sin(t*Omega)/sin(Omega) * z1, Omega = arccos( <z0,z1> / (||z0||*||z1||) ), t in [0,1]. Linear lerp shrinks the norm and drifts off-manifold, dulling samples; slerp preserves it. For semantic axes, run GANSpace-style PCA on early-layer activations over many random z, get principal components v_p, and walk z + sigma*v_p to move along the p-th discovered attribute. For RAVE, encode live input with E: x -> z (z ~ Normal(mu(x), sigma(x))) and render D(z), so manifold position is DRIVEN by the incoming sound; AdaIN swaps statistics for style transfer. Riffusion-style diffusion does the same in diffusion latent, denoising x_{t-1} = (1/sqrt(alpha_t))*(x_t - (1-alpha_t)/sqrt(1-alphabar_t) * eps_theta(x_t,t)) + sigma_t*eps, and slerps between two seeds/prompt embeddings to morph spectrograms.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The geometry of the model's learned sound space -- how timbres sit relative to one another, which directions are brightness vs pitch vs attack, and how perceptually smooth the manifold is. A walk shows two instruments are NEIGHBORS (smooth morph) while a third is far (audible/visible discontinuity), revealing similarity structure no spectrogram of any single sound contains. Encoding live audio reveals where the real signal sits relative to the prior (in-distribution vs surprising).
techniques.why_breathtaking: A liquid, continuously self-reorganizing spectrogram that flows between sonic identities with no hard cuts -- slerp keeps every intermediate frame a plausible, vivid sound-image. Driven by live input it becomes a generative mirror of the performance: arguably the most genuinely wow audio-reactive output in this whole family, and the basis of much award-winning AI sound art.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Strongest in the facet. The latent dimensionality IS a learned auto-reduction of the audio (RAVE compresses to a handful of latents chosen to balance fidelity/compactness); GANSpace PCA auto-discovers the meaningful control axes and ranks them by variance, so steering dimensions self-organize to the content rather than being hand-designed. Encoding live audio makes the walk's path auto-dimensioned by the signal itself.
techniques.implementation_notes: RAVE is the realtime-friendly anchor: generates 48 kHz faster than realtime, runs on CPU (even Raspberry Pi), with a causal buffer-wise reconfiguration for live use -- export to ONNX/TorchScript, run via ort or candle; encode->walk->decode fits a live audio thread, render decoded spectrogram on wgpu. GANSynth/diffusion (Riffusion) are far heavier (full U-Net denoising) -- offline-render the walk, play back realtime. Gotchas: must slerp (not lerp) or samples collapse; ONNX export of these models is fiddly; latency budget = model forward time per buffer, so pick a small RAVE checkpoint. PCA for GANSpace can be precomputed once and shipped as a small matrix.

references.title: RAVE: A Variational Autoencoder for Fast High-Quality Neural Audio Synthesis (Caillon & Esling) -- arXiv 2111.05011
GANSynth: Adversarial Neural Audio Synthesis (Engel et al., ICLR 2019) -- arXiv 1902.08710
Spherical Linear Interpolation (slerp) -- Wikipedia (formula + latent-space use)

techniques.name: Feature Inversion / Representation Morphing (reconstruct & blend audio from a learned representation)
techniques.one_line: Invert a deep representation back to a spectrogram/waveform by gradient descent on the input, and morph two sounds by interpolating their feature vectors -- visualizing what the representation keeps and what it discards.
techniques.the_math: Given a target representation Phi* = Phi(x_target) from layer Phi, recover an input by optimizing x_hat = argmin_x ||Phi(x) - Phi*||^2 + lambda*R(x), via x <- x - eta * d(||Phi(x)-Phi*||^2 + lambda*R(x))/d x, with R a natural-signal prior (total variation or spectrogram-consistency). For MORPHING, blend Phi_blend = (1-t)*Phi(x_A) + t*Phi(x_B) (or slerp on a normalized embedding) and invert Phi_blend -> audio. Special cases: (a) Mel->linear->waveform via Griffin-Lim phase recovery by alternating projection -- fix magnitude |S|, iterate s_hat = ISTFT(|S| * exp(i*angle(STFT(s_hat)))) until phase converges; (b) Mel-spectrogram inversion via ADMM (Masuyama et al. 2025) for higher fidelity than plain Griffin-Lim; (c) a neural vocoder is the learned fast inverse of Phi. The visualization renders the reconstruction-error map |S_target - S_recon| (what the representation throws away) and the morph trajectory's spectrogram sequence.
techniques.what_it_reveals: The reconstruction residual is a direct picture of a representation's lossiness -- mel-bin inversion blurs high-frequency fine structure, and the residual map shows exactly where. Morphing reveals which sonic attributes a representation treats as continuous (smooth crossfade) vs categorical (jump), exposing the representation's internal coordinate system. It answers 'what does this embedding actually keep?' -- invisible without inverting.
techniques.why_breathtaking: Morph sequences produce dreamlike spectrograms that are neither A nor B -- ghostly hybrid instruments emerging mid-interpolation. The residual-error overlay is a striking subtraction-ghost of the original, glowing exactly where the model is blind.
techniques.autonomous_dimensioning: Moderate. The representation defines its own coordinate system, so the morph axis is content-adaptive; the residual auto-normalizes to its max. The inversion's step count adapts to convergence (stop when ||Phi(x)-Phi*|| < epsilon), so cost self-scales with how invertible the target is.
techniques.implementation_notes: Two regimes. Griffin-Lim mel/linear inversion is pure Rust + rustfft, cheap, realtime -- great for the 'what mel discards' residual view with no model at all, ships today. Deep feature inversion (optimize x to match a deep Phi*) needs autodiff (candle/burn) and is an offline optimization loop. Neural-vocoder inversion (HiFi-GAN-style) via ort is fast forward-only but needs a bundled model. Gotcha: deep inversion is non-convex and seed-sensitive; regularize heavily or initialize from Griffin-Lim. Recommended first cut: Griffin-Lim residual map before any learned inversion.

references.title: Mel-Spectrogram Inversion via Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers -- arXiv 2501.05557
Griffin-Lim algorithm (phase recovery from magnitude spectrogram) -- reference implementation
Signal Representations for Synthesizing Audio Textures with GANs (representation comparison/inversion) -- arXiv 2103.07390

key_references.why: Comprehensive survey tying DDSP, neural synthesis, and differentiable signal models together; best single map of the field and its control-surface visualizations.

key_references.title: RAVE: A Variational Autoencoder for Fast High-Quality Neural Audio Synthesis (Caillon & Esling) -- arXiv 2111.05011
key_references.why: The realtime-feasible anchor for latent-walk visuals: faster-than-realtime CPU synthesis, encodable from live audio, exportable to ONNX for Rust -- makes the breathtaking latent-manifold visual actually shippable in an egui app.
